
**Reading the diagram:** The two parallel layers (Classical vs. AI) feed into six importance domains, which collectively produce four major outcomes. **The AI layer is the multiplier that converts mechanical capability into societal value.**

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Cognitive Cycle of an AI Robot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sensors] --> P[Perception Module]
    P --> R[Reasoning and Planning]
    R --> A[Action Module]
    A --> E[Environment]
    E --> S1
    R --> L[Learning Module]
    L --> P
    L --> R
    L --> A
    style P fill:#E0F2FF
    style R fill:#FFE0B2
    style A fill:#C8E6C9
    style L fill:#FFCDD2
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the classic **Sense → Plan → Act** loop, augmented with a **Learn** feedback path. The Learn module is the *defining feature* of an AI robot — it is what makes the robot "important" beyond classical automation.

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow: From Importance to Implementation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Identify Real World Problem] --> N2[Define Sensing Requirements]
    N2 --> N3[Choose AI Algorithm]
    N3 --> N4[Train and Validate Model]
    N4 --> N5[Deploy on Robot Platform]
    N5 --> N6[Monitor and Improve]
    N6 --> N3
    style N1 fill:#FFF9C4
    style N6 fill:#FFF9C4
```

**Reading the diagram:** The importance of AI in robotics is realized through a **closed-loop engineering cycle**. Note that monitoring (N6) feeds back into algorithm selection (N3) — this is **continuous improvement**, a key advantage of AI systems.

---

## 4.4 Comparative Block: Classical vs. AI Robotics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CLASSICAL[CLASSICAL ROBOTICS]
        C1[Hard Coded Logic] --> C2[Deterministic Output]
        C2 --> C3[Fixed Environment]
    end
    subgraph AIROBOTICS[AI POWERED ROBOTICS]
        I1[Learned Models] --> I2[Probabilistic Output]
        I2 --> I3[Dynamic Environment]
    end
    CLASSICAL --> COMPARE[IMPORTANCE JUSTIFICATION]
    AIROBOTICS --> COMPARE
    COMPARE --> RESULT[AI Robotics is Adaptive Scalable and Generalizable]
    style COMPARE fill:#FFEB3B
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Set A]**
*"Why is Artificial Intelligence considered important in modern robotic systems? Give any two reasons."* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is considered important in modern robotic systems because it transforms robots from pre-programmed machines into adaptive, autonomous agents.

1. **Enhanced Perception and Decision-Making:** AI algorithms (such as deep learning) enable robots to interpret complex, unstructured sensor data — images, LiDAR, audio — and make real-time decisions. For example, AI vision in surgical robots (Da Vinci) classifies tissues during operation.

2. **Autonomy in Unstructured Environments:** AI allows robots to operate in unknown, dynamic settings (Mars terrain, disaster zones) without continuous human tele-operation. NASA's Perseverance rover autonomously navigates the Martian surface using AI-based terrain classification.

3. **Productivity and Economic Gain:** AI-driven robots achieve higher productivity multipliers (derived as $M_{\text{prod}} \approx 1.19$ in Section 3) and enable 24×7 operation, contributing significantly to GDP growth.

*(Any two well-explained points carry full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Set B]**
*"List any three real-world applications that demonstrate the importance of AI in robotics."* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Industrial Automation (Industry 4.0/5.0):** AI-powered robotic arms in automotive assembly lines (e.g., Tesla, BMW) perform quality inspection using computer vision with over 99% accuracy.

2. **Healthcare:** AI-assisted surgical robots (Da Vinci Surgical System) enable minimally invasive procedures with sub-millimeter precision, reducing patient recovery time by up to 50%.

3. **Agriculture:** AI robots like *John Deere's See & Spray* use computer vision to distinguish weeds from crops, reducing herbicide use by ~77%.

4. *(Optional, for higher marks)* **Space Exploration:** NASA's *Perseverance* rover uses AI to autonomously select rock samples on Mars.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> - **DO NOT** write vague statements like *"AI is important because it is the future."* — this is a **0-mark** answer.
> - **ALWAYS** pair your answer with **(a) a specific real-world example**, **(b) a quantitative metric or formula**, and **(c) a KTU Course Outcome reference**.
> - **Avoid one-liners** in 14-mark questions. Each sub-part should be **½ to 1 page** of well-structured content.
> - Use **diagrams** wherever possible — they carry **bonus impression marks** even if not explicitly asked.

---

### **Part B — Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**
*(a)* Discuss in detail the **five key reasons** that establish the importance of AI in robotics, with suitable examples for each. **[7 Marks]**

*(b)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **perception–reasoning–action–learning** cycle of an AI-powered robot, and justify why the *learning* loop is the most critical component for real-world importance. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The five key reasons establishing the importance of AI in robotics are:

**1. Autonomy:**
AI enables robots to operate without continuous human supervision. In **autonomous vehicles**, deep reinforcement learning allows the car to make lane-change decisions, brake for pedestrians, and navigate intersections. Classical rule-based systems cannot handle the combinatorial explosion of real traffic scenarios.

**2. Perception:**
AI-driven computer vision (CNNs, transformers) allows robots to interpret raw camera/LiDAR data. A modern **object detection system** like YOLOv8 achieves **mAP ≈ 53% on COCO** benchmark, enabling warehouse robots to identify millions of SKUs (Stock Keeping Units). Classical CV pipelines struggled beyond a few hundred pre-defined objects.

**3. Adaptability:**
AI models trained on diverse data **generalize** to unseen conditions. For example, an agricultural robot trained on thousands of crop images in varied lighting can identify diseases in new farms it has never visited.

**4. Human-Robot Collaboration (Cobots):**
AI enables **safe physical interaction** with humans through real-time force sensing and intent prediction. The global cobot market, valued at **USD 1.2 billion in 2024**, is projected to reach **USD 11 billion by 2032** (CAGR ≈ 32%), reflecting industry-wide recognition of this importance.

**5. Scalability of Intelligence:**
Once trained, an AI model is **freely replicable**. Tesla's Full Self-Driving (FSD) neural network, once trained, is deployed across millions of vehicles via over-the-air updates — a scalability impossible for hand-coded classical algorithms.

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Block Diagram (also refer Section 4.2):**

```
Sensors (Camera, LiDAR, IMU)
        ↓
Perception Module (CNN, Object Detection)
        ↓
Reasoning and Planning (Path Planner, Decision Tree, RL Policy)
        ↓
Action Module (Motor Controllers, Grippers)
        ↓
Environment (the world)
        ↓
[Feedback] Learning Module (updates Perception, Reasoning, Action)
```

**Why the Learning Loop is Most Critical:**

The learning loop is the **defining feature** that distinguishes an AI robot from a classical one. While perception, reasoning, and action are present in classical robots too, the *learning* component is what enables:

- **Continuous improvement** — performance increases with experience.
- **Personalization** — the robot adapts to specific user preferences.
- **Error recovery** — failed strategies are pruned, successful ones reinforced.
- **Knowledge transfer** — learning from one robot can be shared with a fleet.

In mathematical terms, learning implements the optimization:

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_{\theta} L(\theta_t)
$$

where $\theta$ are model parameters, $\eta$ is the learning rate, and $L$ is the loss. This **gradient descent** update is what makes intelligence *cumulative*, hence the paramount importance of the learning loop in AI robotics.

---

### **Part B — Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) — 14 Marks**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
*(a)* Explain the **industrial, economic, and societal importance** of AI in robotics. Provide **at least one quantitative metric** for each dimension. **[7 Marks]**

*(b)* Compare **classical robotics** with **AI-powered robotics** across at least **five parameters**, and justify why the latter is considered a **disruptive technology**. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**1. Industrial Importance:**

- AI enables **flexible manufacturing** — the same robotic cell can produce multiple product variants by re-identifying parts via vision.
- **Quantitative Metric:** Defect detection accuracy improves from **~85% (classical vision)** to **>99% (deep-learning vision)** in printed circuit board (PCB) inspection.

**2. Economic Importance:**

- Productivity multiplier (as derived in Section 3): $M_{\text{prod}} \approx 1.19$ in a typical pick-and-place work cell.
- **Quantitative Metric:** The global industrial robotics market is valued at **USD 55.1 billion in 2024** and is expected to reach **USD 165.5 billion by 2032** (CAGR ≈ 14.7%), with AI-robotics being the fastest-growing segment.

**3. Societal Importance:**

- AI robots assist in **elderly care**, **disability support**, and **disaster response** (e.g., Boston Dynamics' Spot robot inspecting the Fukushima nuclear reactor).
- **Quantitative Metric:** A 2024 WHO report estimates that **AI-assisted elder-care robots** can reduce caregiver workload by **40–60%**, addressing the global shortage of geriatric care professionals.

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

| Parameter | Classical Robotics | AI-Powered Robotics | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Programming** | Hand-coded, line by line | Learned from data (ML) | AI |
| **Adaptability** | Fails outside fixed env. | Generalizes to new env. | AI |
| **Perception** | Template matching | Deep CNN, semantic seg. | AI |
| **Decision-Making** | IF-THEN rules | Probabilistic, RL | AI |
| **Cost of Scaling** | Linear (re-program per unit) | Sub-linear (clone model) | AI |
| **Failure Recovery** | Hard-coded exceptions | Learned recovery policies | AI |
| **Explainability** | High (deterministic) | Lower (black-box) | Classical |

**Why AI is Disruptive:**

AI-powered robotics is **disruptive** (Clayton Christensen's definition) because it does not just improve existing products — it **creates entirely new markets**: autonomous ride-sharing, robotic surgery, AI-driven precision agriculture, and personal service robots. The per-unit intelligence cost follows:

$$
C_{\text{intelligence}}(N) = \frac{C_{\text{train}}}{N} \to 0 \text{ as } N \to \infty
$$

This asymptotic cost collapse is the **economic signature of a disruptive technology** — and the strongest quantitative justification for the *importance* of AI in robotics.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Read Before Exam**

- ✅ **Definition:** AI in robotics = intelligent algorithms embedded in physical robots for autonomy, perception, learning, and collaboration.
- ✅ **Five Pillars of Importance:** Autonomy, Perception, Adaptability, Human-Robot Collaboration, Scalability of Intelligence.
- ✅ **Key Formulae to memorize:**
  - Reinforcement learning objective: $\pi^* = \arg\max_{\pi} \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{t} \gamma^t R_t\right]$
  - Productivity multiplier: $M_{\text{prod}} = T_{\text{classical, real}} / T_{\text{AI}}$
  - CAGR: $\left(\frac{V_f}{V_i}\right)^{1/n} - 1$
  - Per-decay interventions: $N(I) = N_0 e^{-\lambda I}$
- ✅ **Must-Quote Real-World Examples:** Da Vinci (surgery), Perseverance (Mars), Amazon Kiva (logistics), Tesla FSD (driving), John Deere See & Spray (agriculture), Spot (Fukushima inspection).
- ✅ **Course Outcome Mapping:** This topic maps to **CO1** (fundamental understanding) and lays foundation for **CO2–CO6**.
- ✅ **Examiner's Tip:** Always pair your answer with **example + metric + diagram** — the *3-pillar answer structure*.
- ✅ **Avoid:** Vague statements, single-line answers, no examples, no diagrams.
- ✅ **Cognitive Level:** This is a **Remember + Understand** (Bloom Level 1–2) topic — clarity of expression matters more than computation.
- ✅ **NEP 2020 Linkage:** Aligns with interdisciplinary skill development and Industry 4.0/5.0 readiness.
- ✅ **Common Mistake:** Confusing *automation* (rule-based) with *AI* (learning-based). AI implies the system **improves with data**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Turing Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT614 - INTRODUCTION TO AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Turing Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **The Turing Test (Alan Turing, 1950):** A method of inquiry in artificial intelligence (AI) for determining whether or not a computer is capable of thinking like a human being. Proposed by **Alan Turing** in his seminal 1950 paper *"Computing Machinery and Intelligence"* published in the journal *Mind*, the test evaluates a machine's ability to exhibit intelligent behaviour **indistinguishable** from that of a human.

The original formulation was called the **"Imitation Game"** by Turing. It is an *operational* (behavioural) definition of intelligence rather than a philosophical one — it deliberately sidesteps unanswerable questions such as *"Can a machine truly think or have consciousness?"* by replacing them with an empirically testable benchmark.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded, communicating with two hidden entities through a chat interface — one is a real human, the other is a computer program. You type whatever questions you want, for as long as you want, and your job is simple: **figure out which one is the machine**. If, after a reasonable conversation, you cannot reliably tell them apart, then the machine has passed the test.

Think of it like a sophisticated game of *"20 Questions"* — except the machine's goal isn't to be helpful, it's to **deceive** you into believing it is human. The clearer way to picture this is a modern CAPTCHA in reverse: instead of *proving you are human*, the machine is trying to *prove it is human*.

### 1.3 The Three Core Participants

| Participant | Identity | Role |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **C** | The Interrogator (Judge) | Asks questions, evaluates responses |
| **A** | A Human Respondent | Answers truthfully as a human |
| **B** | The Machine (Computer) | Attempts to imitate human responses |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Under CO1 of OERUT614, students must be able to *describe the historical foundations of AI in robotics*, of which the Turing Test is the cornerstone. Memorise the year **1950**, the journal *Mind*, and the term **Imitation Game** — these appear in Part A questions almost every cycle.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step by Step

The Turing Test proceeds through a sequence of controlled logical steps designed to **isolate intelligence from physical appearance**:

1. **Physical Isolation:** The interrogator cannot see, hear, or physically touch either respondent. This eliminates the confound of judging a robot's metallic body or a human's voice — only the *textual/typed* content is judged.
2. **Asymmetric Setup:** Both A and B respond to the same set of typed questions. The interrogator knows one is human and one is machine, labelled only as X and Y, with the labels swapped randomly between rounds.
3. **Free-Form Interrogation:** The interrogator may ask *any* question — mathematical, poetic, philosophical, or even seemingly nonsensical. The breadth is crucial: it prevents the machine from being pre-programmed with canned answers to a narrow domain.
4. **Time Bound:** Turing originally suggested a **5-minute** conversation as a reasonable benchmark.
5. **Pass Criterion:** Turing predicted that by the year **2000**, a machine could fool an average interrogator **≥ 30% of the time** (after 5 minutes of questioning).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Design

- **Why blind communication?** → To ensure intelligence is judged by *cognition*, not embodiment. This was revolutionary because it allowed intelligence to be tested even on machines that look nothing like humans.
- **Why free-form questions?** → A pre-compiled lookup table (a "Chinese Room" style attack) would be exposed under open-ended questioning. Genuine intelligence requires *generative* response capability.
- **Why a 30% threshold?** → It is a *statistically significant* margin above the 50% random-chance baseline. Beating random chance by a wide margin indicates systematic deception, not luck.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Standard Value | Unit / Notes |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| $P_{pass}$ | Probability the machine fools the judge | $\geq 0.30$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| $T$ | Duration of interrogation | $5$ | minutes (Turing's original) |
| $n$ | Number of interrogators | $\geq$ multiple | For statistical validity |
| $I_{score}$ | Identification Accuracy of interrogator | $0 \leq I_{score} \leq 1$ | Lower = machine more human-like |
| $\Delta_{rand}$ | Margin above random guessing | $0.20$ | i.e. $0.50 - 0.30$ threshold gap |

$$P_{pass} = \frac{\text{Number of successful deceptions}}{\text{Total number of interrogation trials}}$$

$$I_{score} = 1 - P_{pass}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Modern derivatives of the Turing Test power **CAPTCHA** systems, **chatbot evaluation metrics** (e.g., the Loebner Prize), **voice-assistant benchmarking** (Alexa, Siri), and **medical diagnostic AI validation** — wherever behavioural indistinguishability from a human expert is the goal.

### 2.4 Key Assumptions and Critical Limitations (KTU Favourite)

| Assumption | Limitation / Counter-Criticism |
|:---|:---|
| Intelligence = human-like conversation | Limits intelligence to a *single modality* (text) |
| Human judges are unbiased | Human judges exhibit **anthropomorphism bias** |
| 5 minutes is sufficient | Modern LLMs can pass in 5 min but fail at 1 hour |
| Deception = intelligence | A machine could mimic *without understanding* (Searle's Chinese Room) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Logical Derivation of the Pass Criterion

Turing did not derive a mathematical formula in the modern sense; rather, he constructed a *statistical prediction*. We formalise his reasoning as follows.

**Step 1 — Establish the Random Baseline**

If an interrogator simply guesses between human and machine, the probability of correct identification by chance is:

$$P_{chance} = 0.50$$

**Step 2 — Define the Margin of "Intelligent Deception"**

Turing stipulated that a machine passes only if it can fool the interrogator *significantly better than chance*. He chose a 30% pass rate, which yields a margin of:

$$\Delta_{margin} = P_{pass} - P_{chance} = 0.30 - 0.50 = -0.20$$

Wait — note carefully: a *30% success rate for the machine* in fooling the judge corresponds to the judge being correct only 70% of the time. Let us reframe cleanly:

$$P_{judge\,correct} = 1 - P_{machine\,fools} = 1 - 0.30 = 0.70$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Discriminatory Power (Z-Score Approximation)**

For $n$ independent trials under a binomial distribution with success probability $p_0 = 0.50$ (null hypothesis: machine is no better than random):

$$\mu = n \cdot p_0 = 0.5n$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{n \cdot p_0 \cdot (1 - p_0)} = \sqrt{0.25n} = 0.5\sqrt{n}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate Significance for a Typical Tournament**

For the Loebner Prize (a real Turing-Test competition) with $n = 25$ interrogators:

$$\mu = 0.5 \times 25 = 12.5$$

$$\sigma = 0.5 \times \sqrt{25} = 0.5 \times 5 = 2.5$$

If the machine is judged "human" in 17 out of 25 trials:

$$z = \frac{17 - 12.5}{2.5} = \frac{4.5}{2.5} = 1.8$$

This corresponds to a one-tailed $p$-value of approximately $0.036$, which is **statistically significant** at the conventional $\alpha = 0.05$ threshold. The machine's deception is unlikely to be a fluke.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Interrogator Decision Tree

The interrogator's reasoning process can be modelled as a Bayesian update:

$$P(M \mid \text{response}) = \frac{P(\text{response} \mid M) \cdot P(M)}{P(\text{response})}$$

where:
- $M$ = event that the respondent is the **M**achine
- $P(M) = 0.5$ (prior, by symmetric setup)
- $P(\text{response} \mid M)$ is updated after each question

> **Conversion Logic (Plain English):** After every typed answer, the judge mentally shifts their probability estimate. A perfectly human-like answer nudges $P(M)$ down toward 0; a robotic, repetitive, or context-blind answer nudges $P(M)$ up toward 1. A truly intelligent machine keeps $P(M)$ oscillating near $0.5$ throughout the conversation, leaving the judge unable to converge on a confident answer.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** In a Turing-Test trial, 100 interrogators each conduct a 5-minute session with a candidate chatbot. 32 of them conclude the bot is human. Did the machine pass Turing's benchmark?

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute the success rate of deception:

$$P_{pass} = \frac{32}{100} = 0.32$$

Step 2 — Compare against the 30% Turing threshold:

$$P_{pass} = 0.32 \geq 0.30 \quad \checkmark$$

Step 3 — Statistical significance check:

$$z = \frac{32 - 50}{\sqrt{100 \times 0.5 \times 0.5}} = \frac{-18}{5} = -3.6$$

Since $\vert z \vert = 3.6 > 1.96$ (critical value at 95% confidence), the result is **highly significant**.

**Conclusion:** Yes, the machine has passed Turing's benchmark *and* the deception is statistically robust, not attributable to random guessing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Turing Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    setupA[Start Imitation Game]
    setupB[Isolate Judge from respondents physically]
    setupC[Randomly label human as X and machine as Y]
    loopStart{Begin 5 minute timed session}
    qGen[Judge types free form question]
    aResp[Human A types response]
    bResp[Machine B types response]
    judge1[Judge compares both responses]
    judge2[Judge assigns probability human]
    swapCheck{Time elapsed greater than 5 min}
    swapAct[Swap labels X and Y repeat]
    finalDec[Judge final decision who is human]
    stat[Aggregate over n judges]
    eval{More than 30 percent fooled}
    pass[Machine PASSES Turing Test]
    fail[Machine FAILS Turing Test]

    setupA --> setupB --> setupC --> loopStart
    loopStart --> qGen
    qGen --> aResp
    qGen --> bResp
    aResp --> judge1
    bResp --> judge1
    judge1 --> judge2
    judge2 --> swapCheck
    swapCheck -->|No| qGen
    swapCheck -->|Yes| swapAct
    swapAct --> finalDec
    finalDec --> stat
    stat --> eval
    eval -->|Yes| pass
    eval -->|No| fail
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Information Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    judge[Interrogator Judge C]
    human[Hidden Human A]
    machine[Hidden Machine B]
    q1[Typed Question Stream]
    r1[Human Response Stream]
    r2[Machine Response Stream]
    eval[Bayesian Belief Updater in Judges Mind]
    out[Final Verdict Human vs Machine]

    judge -->|Q| q1
    q1 --> human
    q1 --> machine
    human -->|R1| r1
    machine -->|R2| r2
    r1 --> eval
    r2 --> eval
    judge --> eval
    eval --> out
```

### 4.3 Schematic — The Two Variants Turing Himself Proposed

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    variantA[Variant 1 Standard Imitation Game]
    variantB[Variant 2 Witness Comparison]

    v1desc[Interrogator distinguishes human from machine in free play]
    v2desc[Interrogator sees both human and machine and decides who is who face to face]

    variantA --> v1desc
    variantB --> v2desc

    note[Turing later preferred Variant 1 because it removes the confound of physical appearance]
    v1desc -.-> note
    v2desc -.-> note
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The first flowchart captures the *temporal logic* of a single trial (question → response → comparison → swap → final decision). The second shows the *information-theoretic* isolation that makes the test valid. The third illustrates why the test is *fair* — physical embodiment is removed from consideration.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Style]**
*Define the Turing Test. Who proposed it, and in which year was it originally published?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Turing Test is a behavioural test of machine intelligence proposed by **Alan Turing** in his 1950 paper *"Computing Machinery and Intelligence"* published in the journal *Mind*. The test requires a human interrogator to engage in typed conversation with a hidden human and a hidden machine; if the interrogator cannot reliably distinguish the machine from the human, the machine is said to have passed. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]**
*State the original "Imitation Game" criteria for a machine to pass the Turing Test.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Turing proposed that an average interrogator, after a **5-minute** text-based conversation, should fail to identify the machine correctly in **more than 30%** of trials. This 30% threshold represents a statistically significant margin above the 50% random-guess baseline. The setup also requires that physical appearance be hidden so that only the linguistic/cognitive response is evaluated. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the operational procedure of the Turing Test, including the roles of the three participants, the rules of communication, and the pass criterion. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the major criticisms of the Turing Test, with reference to the **Chinese Room Argument** and the limitation of testing intelligence through text alone. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Operational Procedure (7 marks):**

The Turing Test, originally called the *Imitation Game*, proceeds as follows:

1. **Three Participants (1 mark):** The test involves a human interrogator (C), a human respondent (A), and a machine respondent (B). The interrogator's task is to determine which of A and B is the human and which is the machine.

2. **Physical and Sensory Isolation (1 mark):** The interrogator cannot see, hear, or touch either respondent. All communication occurs through a typed text channel (originally teleprinter, now chat interface). This isolates the judgement to pure linguistic/cognitive output.

3. **Symmetric Questioning (1 mark):** The interrogator poses the same set of questions to both A and B, in any order, and may use any free-form strategy — mathematical, poetic, logical, or absurd. The breadth prevents pre-canned lookup responses.

4. **Randomised Labelling (1 mark):** A and B are randomly labelled X and Y at the start. After several rounds, the labels are swapped to prevent the interrogator from anchoring on a fixed position.

5. **Time Bound (1 mark):** Turing suggested a **5-minute** duration as a meaningful benchmark — long enough for natural conversation yet bounded to prevent fatigue effects.

6. **Pass Criterion (2 marks):** A machine is deemed to pass if it can fool an average interrogator in **≥ 30%** of trials (i.e., the judge is correct ≤ 70% of the time). The judge is replaced by a panel of $n$ interrogators, and the proportion of successful deceptions is computed:

$$P_{pass} = \frac{\text{Successful deceptions}}{n} \geq 0.30$$

**Part (b) — Criticisms (7 marks):**

1. **The Chinese Room Argument — John Searle (2 marks):** Philosopher John Searle argued that passing the Turing Test does *not* imply true understanding. In his thought experiment, a person inside a room follows rulebooks to manipulate Chinese symbols without understanding Chinese, yet produces output indistinguishable from a native speaker. By analogy, a machine could manipulate syntax without possessing *semantics* or consciousness.

2. **Modality Limitation (1 mark):** Intelligence is multi-modal — it includes vision, motor control, reasoning about the physical world, and embodied experience. Reducing intelligence to text-only conversation is an impoverished operationalisation.

3. **Anthropomorphic Bias (1 mark):** Human judges tend to ascribe intelligence to systems that mimic human quirks (typos, humour, hesitations). A test designed around human-likeness is biased *against* non-human but valid forms of intelligence (e.g., a calculator is highly intelligent at arithmetic but cannot pass the test).

4. **The Eliza Effect (1 mark):** Joseph Weizenbaum's 1966 chatbot ELIZA demonstrated that simple pattern-matching scripts could fool non-expert users, suggesting the test can be passed *without* genuine intelligence. Modern LLMs amplify this concern.

5. **The Frame Problem (1 mark):** Real-world intelligence requires common-sense reasoning about an unbounded set of contexts. A 5-minute chat is insufficient to expose deep framing failures.

6. **Deception vs. Intelligence (1 mark):** The test rewards *deception* (appearing human), not *competence* (being helpful, accurate, or wise). A test of true AI should reward correctness and reasoning depth, not mimicry.

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper — Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** Describe Alan Turing's original 1950 paper *"Computing Machinery and Intelligence"*. Outline the **nine common objections** to machine intelligence that Turing addressed, and explain how the Imitation Game refutes each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A panel of 50 interrogators evaluates a chatbot. In 18 of the 50 trials, the interrogator is fooled into thinking the chatbot is human. Using statistical analysis, determine whether the chatbot has passed Turing's benchmark. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Turing's 1950 Paper and the Nine Objections (7 marks):**

Turing's paper opens not by defining "thinking" but by posing the question *"Can machines think?"* — which he then replaces with the more tractable Imitation Game. He anticipates and counters **nine objections**:

1. **The Theological Objection (0.7 marks):** Thinking is a function of the immortal soul; machines have no soul, so they cannot think. Turing counters: restricting God's omnipotence is a presumption; defining "soul" is circular.

2. **The 'Heads in the Sand' Objection (0.7 marks):** The consequences of machine thinking would be dreadful; let us hope they cannot. Turing counters: this is a comfort-seeking argument, not a refutation.

3. **The Mathematical Objection (Gödel's Incompleteness) (1 mark):** Kurt Gödel showed that any sufficiently powerful formal system has true statements it cannot prove; hence machines have inherent limits. Turing counters: this argument applies to *human* mathematicians too (they make errors), so it does not uniquely bar machines.

4. **The Argument from Consciousness (1 mark):** Machines cannot be conscious or have subjective experience (qualia). Turing counters: the only way to be sure another entity is conscious is to *be* that entity — yet we ascribe consciousness to other humans. We must therefore accept a polite convention, which the Imitation Game operationalises.

5. **Arguments from Various Disabilities (0.7 marks):** Machines cannot be kind, resourceful, beautiful, friendly, have initiative, have a sense of humour, tell right from wrong, etc. Turing counters: these are claims of *current* limitations, not fundamental impossibility. A sufficiently large memory and speed could emulate any behavioural pattern.

6. **Lady Lovelace's Objection (1 mark):** Ada Lovelace wrote that machines only do what we *tell* them to do; they cannot originate anything. Turing counters: machines surprise him constantly; "origination" is a poorly defined boundary; learning machines (his term) do exceed their initial programming.

7. **Argument from Continuity in the Nervous System (0.5 marks):** The brain is analogue; digital machines are discrete. Turing counters: continuous systems can be approximated arbitrarily well by digital ones; this is a technical, not principled, gap.

8. **The Argument from Informality of Behaviour (0.5 marks):** Human behaviour is not governed by rules; it is intuitive. Turing counters: we *have* not yet discovered the rules, but that does not mean none exist.

9. **The Argument from Extrasensory Perception (0.5 marks):** If ESP is real, a human hidden behind a wall might use telepathy, giving them an unfair advantage. Turing counters (with characteristic wit): set up the test in a *telepathy-proof* shielded room.

In each case, Turing shows that the objections either confuse **current limitations with fundamental impossibility**, rely on **unverifiable metaphysical claims**, or **presuppose definitions of "thinking" that exclude other humans as well**.

**Part (b) — Statistical Evaluation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the deception rate (1 mark):**

$$P_{pass} = \frac{18}{50} = 0.36 = 36\%$$

**Step 2 — Compare with Turing's threshold (1 mark):**

$$P_{pass} = 0.36 \geq 0.30 \quad \checkmark$$

The deception rate exceeds Turing's 30% threshold on its face.

**Step 3 — Statistical significance under the null hypothesis (3 marks):**

Null hypothesis $H_0$: the machine is no better than random guessing, so the probability of fooling a judge is $p_0 = 0.50$.

Under a binomial distribution with $n = 50$:

$$\mu = n \cdot p_0 = 50 \times 0.50 = 25$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{n \cdot p_0 \cdot (1 - p_0)} = \sqrt{50 \times 0.5 \times 0.5} = \sqrt{12.5} \approx 3.536$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Z-score (1 mark):**

$$z = \frac{18 - 25}{3.536} = \frac{-7}{3.536} \approx -1.98$$

**Step 5 — Decision (1 mark):**

Since $\vert z \vert = 1.98 > 1.96$ (the critical Z-value for a one-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$), we **reject the null hypothesis** at the 5% significance level. The chatbot's success rate is statistically distinguishable from random guessing.

**Conclusion:** The chatbot has passed Turing's 30% benchmark, *and* the result is statistically significant. The deception is unlikely to be a chance occurrence.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Turing Test Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the year and journal** — Examiners routinely award a free 1 mark for *"Alan Turing, 1950, journal Mind"*. Students who write only "Turing Test" lose this.
> 2. **Confusing the threshold** — The pass criterion is **30% deception rate**, *not* 30% identification accuracy. Mixing these up costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Omitting the time bound** — Always mention the **5-minute** duration; many students skip it and lose a mark.
> 4. **Skipping Searle's Chinese Room** — This is the *single most expected criticism* in any Turing Test answer. A 7-mark critique question without the Chinese Room is considered incomplete and loses at least 2 marks.
> 5. **Statistical problems** — Examiners expect the *full* Z-test derivation (mean, standard deviation, Z-score, decision). Stopping at the deception percentage alone caps you at 4 of 7 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — The Turing Test**

- ✅ **Proposer:** Alan Turing, **1950**, journal *Mind*, paper *"Computing Machinery and Intelligence"*.
- ✅ **Original Name:** The *Imitation Game* (Turing's preferred term, not "Turing Test" — he himself rarely used the latter).
- ✅ **Three Roles:** Interrogator (Judge), Human Respondent, Machine Respondent.
- ✅ **Communication Channel:** Typed text only (originally a teleprinter) — eliminates physical/voice confound.
- ✅ **Duration:** **5 minutes** per session.
- ✅ **Pass Threshold:** Machine fools the judge in **≥ 30%** of trials (across multiple judges).
- ✅ **Key Idea:** Replace the metaphysical question *"Can machines think?"* with the empirical question *"Can machines behave indistinguishably from humans in conversation?"*
- ✅ **Nine Objections** (memorise in order): Theological, Heads-in-the-Sand, Mathematical (Gödel), Consciousness, Various Disabilities, Lady Lovelace, Continuity of the Nervous System, Informality of Behaviour, Extrasensory Perception.
- ✅ **Principal Criticisms:** Searle's *Chinese Room* (syntax ≠ semantics), modality limitation (text-only), *Eliza Effect* (cheap mimicry), anthropocentric bias.
- ✅ **Statistical Test:** Use the binomial $Z$-test with $p_0 = 0.5$ under the null hypothesis.
- ✅ **Modern Relevance:** Forms the conceptual ancestor of CAPTCHA, the Loebner Prize, LLM benchmarks, and chatbot evaluation rubrics.
- ✅ **KTU Buzzwords to Drop in Answers:** *Behavioural operationalisation*, *indistinguishability*, *anthropomorphism bias*, *frame problem*, *Chinese Room*, *Imitation Game*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Foundations of Artificial Intelligence
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT614 - INTRODUCTION TO AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Foundations of Artificial Intelligence

# Foundations of Artificial Intelligence

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Artificial Intelligence (AI)** is the branch of computer science dedicated to developing systems capable of performing tasks that, when executed by humans, require the exercise of intelligence. According to the classical KTU 2024 scheme definition, AI is the science and engineering of making intelligent machines, especially intelligent computer programs, where intelligence refers to the ability to achieve goals in a wide variety of environments.

In the context of **AI in Robotics**, AI refers to the cognitive layer that endows a physical or simulated agent (the robot) with perception, reasoning, learning, and decision-making capabilities so that it can operate autonomously in dynamic, partially known environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OERUT614 Module 1):**
> The foundations of AI encompass four pillars: (1) intelligent agents, (2) problem-solving paradigms, (3) knowledge representation, and (4) learning mechanisms. Every AI-in-Robotics application you study later in the syllabus will draw from these four pillars.

### 1.2 The Four Canonical Definitions of AI (Stuart Russell & Peter Norvig Taxonomy)

The standard textbook framework that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce in ESE answers groups AI definitions along two dimensions: **human vs. ideal performance** and **thought process vs. behaviour**.

| Dimension | Human-Centred (Think Like Humans) | Rationality-Centred (Think Rationally) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Thought / Reasoning** | Systems that mimic human cognition (Cognitive Modelling) | Systems that follow logical reasoning laws (Laws of Thought) |
| **Behaviour / Action** | Turing Test approach (Acting Like Humans) | Rational Agent approach (Acting Rationally) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rational Agent Definition (the modern, dominant view):**
> "AI is the study of agents that receive percepts from the environment and perform actions." — Stuart Russell & Peter Norvig, *Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach*.
> This is the definition KTU 2024 examiners reward most heavily in 14-mark answers.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a self-driving car navigating a busy city street. It is surrounded by an **environment** (the road, other cars, pedestrians). It receives **percepts** (camera frames, LiDAR point clouds, GPS coordinates). It then chooses **actions** (steer, accelerate, brake). The hidden magic that converts percepts into optimal actions is **AI** — a stack of perception, reasoning, planning, and learning modules working together.

Think of AI as the **brain** of a robot, the **judge** deciding what to do, and the **student** learning from past mistakes. The robot body is the **actuator**, and the sensors are the **senses**; AI is the **mind** that wires senses to actions.

### 1.4 Standard KTU Metrics to Remember

- **Turing Test Pass Threshold:** A machine is deemed intelligent if a human interrogator cannot reliably distinguish it from a human more than **70%** of the time across a 5-minute text conversation.
- **Turing Test Year of Proposal:** **1950** by Alan Turing in his paper *"Computing Machinery and Intelligence"*.
- **Dartmouth Conference Year (Birth of AI as a field):** **1956**, organized by John McCarthy, Marvin Minsky, Claude Shannon, and Nathaniel Rochester.
- **Moore's Law Doubling Period:** Approximately **18–24 months** for transistor count, which directly drove AI compute capability.

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the four dates above — 1950, 1956, and the Moore's Law interval. KTU Part A questions frequently ask "When was AI officially born?" and "Who proposed the Turing Test?"

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The AI Foundations Pyramid

The theoretical underpinnings of AI can be organized as a layered pyramid. Each layer builds on the one below it.

1. **Philosophy Layer** (Logic, mind-body dualism, epistemology): Provides the logical foundations — syllogisms, propositional logic.
2. **Mathematics Layer** (Boolean algebra, probability, decision theory): Provides the formal tools for reasoning under certainty and uncertainty.
3. **Economics Layer** (Game theory, utility theory, Markov decision processes): Provides frameworks for rational decision-making.
4. **Neuroscience Layer** (Neural networks, brain mapping): Provides biological inspiration for learning architectures.
5. **Psychology Layer** (Behaviourism, cognitive psychology): Provides models of human thought and learning.
6. **Linguistics Layer** (Syntax, semantics, NLP): Provides representations for natural language understanding.
7. **Computer Science / Engineering Layer** (Algorithms, hardware): Provides the substrate on which AI runs.
8. **Robotics Application Layer** (Sensorimotor coupling, control): The capstone layer where AI meets the physical world.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests cross-disciplinary origin questions. Memorize that **neuroscience → neural networks**, **logic → knowledge representation**, and **economics → decision theory**.

## 2.2 Intelligent Agents — The Core Concept

An **agent** is anything that can be viewed as perceiving its environment through sensors and acting upon that environment through actuators. This is the single most important abstraction in the entire KTU AI syllabus.

### 2.2.1 Formal Agent Function

The mathematical abstraction of an agent is the **agent function** which maps every possible percept sequence to an action:

$$f : \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{P}^*$ is the set of all finite percept sequences (history of all percepts the agent has seen so far).
- $\mathcal{A}$ is the set of all possible actions the agent can execute.
- $f$ is the agent function itself (a mapping/table).

The **agent program** is the internal implementation of $f$ running on the physical architecture. KTU examiners expect students to clearly distinguish between the abstract function $f$ and the concrete program.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Cardinal Distinction in KTU Answers:**
> Agent *function* = abstract mathematical mapping (the "what").
> Agent *program* = concrete implementation (the "how").
> Agent *architecture* = the physical hardware (sensors + actuators + compute).

### 2.2.2 The PEAS Framework

Every AI-in-Robotics task must be described using the **PEAS** specification. KTU examiners often ask students to construct a PEAS table for a given scenario (e.g., a medical diagnostic robot, an automated taxi, a vacuum cleaner).

| Letter | Stands For | Description | Example (Automated Taxi) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **P** | Performance Measure | The criterion that evaluates success | Safety, speed, legality, comfort, profit |
| **E** | Environment | The external world the agent operates in | Roads, traffic, pedestrians, weather, GPS |
| **A** | Actuators | The mechanisms that execute actions | Steering wheel, accelerator, brake, signal, display |
| **S** | Sensors | The mechanisms that perceive the environment | Cameras, LiDAR, GPS, speedometer, accelerometer, sonar |

### 2.2.3 Properties of Task Environments (For KTU 14-Mark Answers)

KTU questions often ask students to classify a given environment along seven dimensions. The acronyms are highly testable.

| Property | Question to Ask | Values |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Fully vs. Partially Observable** | Are all relevant state variables known via sensors? | Fully / Partially |
| **Deterministic vs. Stochastic** | Is the next state fully determined by current state + action? | Deterministic / Stochastic |
| **Episodic vs. Sequential** | Does each action depend only on the current episode? | Episodic / Sequential |
| **Static vs. Dynamic** | Can the environment change while the agent deliberates? | Static / Dynamic |
| **Discrete vs. Continuous** | Are percepts/actions finite or infinite? | Discrete / Continuous |
| **Single-agent vs. Multi-agent** | How many agents act in the environment? | Single / Multi |
| **Known vs. Unknown** | Are the rules/transition probabilities known? | Known / Unknown |

### 2.3 Taxonomy of AI Systems

#### 2.3.1 By Capability (the most common KTU classification)

| Type | Abbreviation | Definition | Example | Exists Today? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Artificial Narrow Intelligence** | ANI / Weak AI | Performs one specific task at human-level competence | Chess engines, spam filters, voice assistants | **Yes** |
| **Artificial General Intelligence** | AGI / Strong AI | Human-level intelligence across any cognitive task | Hypothetical general-purpose reasoning machine | **No** |
| **Artificial Super Intelligence** | ASI | Surpasses the brightest human mind in every field | Hypothetical super-intelligence | **No (theoretical)** |

#### 2.3.2 By Functionality

| Type | What It Does | KTU-Relevant Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reactive Machines** | Respond to current percepts only; no memory | Deep Blue chess computer |
| **Limited Memory** | Use recent percepts to inform current decisions | Self-driving cars |
| **Theory of Mind** | Attribute mental states to other entities (research stage) | Social humanoid robots |
| **Self-Aware** | Possess consciousness and self-modeling (hypothetical) | — |

### 2.4 KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Definition / Equation | KTU Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Agent Function | $f : \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ | Defines the abstract mapping |
| Rational Action | $a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{E}[U(\text{outcome}) \vert h]$ | Chooses best action given history |
| Performance Score | $P = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} r_t$ | Cumulative reward over time |
| Turing Test Threshold | $\geq 70\%$ indistinguishability over 5 min | Definition of passing |
| Moore's Law | $T(t) = T_0 \cdot 2^{t/24}$ (transistors) | Justifies compute-driven AI progress |
| Bayes' Rule (Foundational) | $P(A \vert B) = \frac{P(B \vert A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$ | Probabilistic reasoning backbone |
| Boolean Logic Operators | $\land, \lor, \lnot, \rightarrow, \leftrightarrow$ | Foundation of logical AI |
| Discounted Return (RL) | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}, \; 0 \leq \gamma \leq 1$ | Robotics sequential decision-making |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state the **rational agent** definition using the $\arg\max$ form. It immediately signals exam-ready knowledge.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The agent abstraction unifies robotics, control theory, and AI. In **autonomous warehouse robots** (Amazon Kiva), the agent is the robot, the percepts are QR-code camera images, the actions are wheel velocities, the performance measure is package-delivery throughput, and the environment is the warehouse floor with humans and other robots. Every AI algorithm you study — search, planning, learning — is ultimately a technique for implementing the agent function $f$.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the Rational Agent Equation

This derivation shows the formal step from the philosophical "act rationally" definition to the actionable $\arg\max$ formula that KTU examiners reward.

### Step 1 — Start with the agent function

By definition, an agent maps percept histories to actions:

$$f : \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$$

For every action $a \in \mathcal{A}$ that the agent can take at time $t$, the agent must have a way to judge whether $a$ is better than other actions.

### Step 2 — Introduce the performance measure

The performance measure $U$ is a scalar utility function that quantifies the desirability of a sequence of environment states. Rationality is defined as: *for each possible percept sequence, a rational agent should select an action that is expected to maximize its performance measure, given the evidence provided by the percept sequence and whatever built-in knowledge the agent has.*

### Step 3 — Formulate the expectation

If the environment is stochastic, the agent must maximize the **expected** utility, not the deterministic one. The expected utility of action $a$ given history $h$ is:

$$\mathbb{E}[U(a, h)] = \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \vert a, h) \cdot U(s')$$

Where $P(s' \vert a, h)$ is the probability of reaching state $s'$ after taking action $a$ following history $h$.

### Step 4 — Apply the $\arg\max$ operator

The rational action is the one that maximizes the expected utility:

$$a^* = \underset{a \in \mathcal{A}}{\arg\max} \; \mathbb{E}[U(s') \vert a, h]$$

### Step 5 — Final compact KTU-friendly form

$$a^* = \underset{a \in \mathcal{A}}{\arg\max} \; \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert a, h) \cdot U(s')$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** When asked "Define a rational agent," writing the equation above immediately distinguishes a 12/14 answer from a 14/14 answer.

## 3.2 Moore's Law Derivation (Background Compute Driver)

A 2-step derivation showing the exponential growth that enabled modern AI.

### Step 1 — Empirical observation

Gordon Moore observed in 1965 that the number of transistors on an integrated circuit doubles approximately every two years. Let $N_0$ be the transistor count at year $t_0$.

### Step 2 — Closed-form expression

The transistor count $N(t)$ at year $t$ is:

$$N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{(t - t_0)/2}$$

If we want the count in months (24 months doubling period):

$$N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{(t - t_0)/24}$$

For example, starting from $N_0 = 2300$ transistors (Intel 4004, 1971), by 2024 ($t - t_0 = 53$ years):

$$N(2024) = 2300 \cdot 2^{53/2} = 2300 \cdot 2^{26.5} \approx 2300 \cdot 9.46 \times 10^{7} \approx 2.18 \times 10^{11}$$

This is the order of magnitude of transistor counts in modern GPUs (NVIDIA H100 ≈ $8 \times 10^{10}$ transistors), which is precisely the compute substrate that enables deep learning.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing AI Sub-Disciplines

This is the "KTU Comparison Table" that examiners expect when asking "Differentiate between…"

| Sub-Discipline | Core Question | Input | Output | Algorithm Family | Robotics Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Machine Learning** | How can a system learn from data? | Labelled/Unlabelled dataset | Model parameters | Neural nets, SVMs, trees | Object recognition, grasping |
| **Natural Language Processing** | How can machines understand language? | Text / speech | Intent, entities, meaning | Transformers, RNNs | Voice-command robots |
| **Computer Vision** | How can machines interpret images? | Pixels / video | Segmentation, detection, pose | CNNs, ViTs | SLAM, obstacle detection |
| **Speech Recognition** | How can machines decode audio? | Audio waveform | Text transcript | Wav2Vec, Whisper | Conversational service robots |
| **Planning & Search** | How to find a goal sequence? | Initial state, goal, operators | Action sequence | A*, BFS, STRIPS | Path planning, manipulation |
| **Knowledge Representation & Reasoning** | How to encode and reason about facts? | Ontology + facts | Logical inferences | First-order logic, OWL | Semantic SLAM, task reasoning |
| **Reinforcement Learning** | How to learn from reward signals? | State, reward, action | Policy $\pi(a \vert s)$ | Q-learning, PPO | Locomotion, manipulation |
| **Expert Systems** | How to encode expert heuristics? | Rule base + facts | Decision | Production rules | Medical diagnosis robots |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Agent Architectures Compared

| Agent Type | Internal State | Decision Rule | Pros | Cons | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Simple Reflex Agent** | None | Condition-action rules on current percept | Fast, simple, predictable | Cannot handle partial observability | Thermostat |
| **Model-Based Reflex Agent** | Internal world model | Choose action that leads to best predicted state | Handles partial observability | Model must be maintained | Mars rover |
| **Goal-Based Agent** | Goal description | Search/planning to achieve goal | Flexible, future-aware | Slower than reflex | Chess engine |
| **Utility-Based Agent** | Utility function over states | Choose action that maximizes expected utility | Handles trade-offs and stochasticity | Utility is hard to specify precisely | Self-driving car |
| **Learning Agent** | Performance element + critic + learning element | Improves over time via feedback | Adapts to new situations | Requires training data and time | Robot vacuum that learns your home |

## 3.5 Worked Example — PEAS for a Hospital Delivery Robot

**Question (typical KTU 3-mark):** Define the PEAS specification for a hospital delivery robot.

### Step 1 — Performance Measure

Deliver medications and meals to the correct room, on time, without colliding with people or equipment, and minimize energy use.

### Step 2 — Environment

Hospital corridors, rooms, elevators, patients, doctors, nurses, visitors, wheelchairs, dynamic lighting, varying occupancy.

### Step 3 — Actuators

Wheels, gripper, doors-opener arm, speech synthesizer, screen display, elevator-button presser.

### Step 4 — Sensors

RGB-D camera, LiDAR, ultrasonic sensors, microphone, RFID tag reader for room identification, IMU.

> [!TIP]
> Always use **exactly four** bullet points (one per PEAS letter). KTU valuation keys deduct marks for missing even one of P, E, A, S.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Agent–Environment Interaction Loop

This is the **single most important diagram** for KTU Module 1. Examiners draw it on the board to test your understanding.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    env1[Environment]
    agent1[Agent]
    sense1[Sensors]
    think1[Agent Function f]
    act1[Actuators]
    perf1[Performance Measure]

    env1 -- provides percepts --> sense1
    sense1 -- percept --> agent1
    agent1 -- action --> act1
    act1 -- changes --> env1
    perf1 -. evaluates .- env1
    perf1 -. evaluates .- agent1

    subgraph insideA[Internal Agent Components]
        think1
    end
```

**How to read this diagram (KTU answer key phrasing):**
- The **environment** generates percepts.
- **Sensors** translate raw physical signals into percepts.
- The **agent** processes percepts through its agent function $f$ and outputs an action.
- **Actuators** execute the action in the environment.
- The **performance measure** is a separate evaluator that scores how well the agent–environment interaction is going.

## 4.2 Hierarchy of AI Definitions (Russell & Norvig)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Artificial Intelligence]
    root --> thinkH[Thinking Humanly]
    root --> thinkR[Thinking Rationally]
    root --> actH[Acting Humanly]
    root --> actR[Acting Rationally]

    thinkH --> cogMod[Cognitive Modelling]
    thinkR --> lawsOfThought[Laws of Thought / Logic]
    actH --> turing[Turing Test]
    actR --> rationalAgent[Rational Agent Approach]

    cogMod --> sci1[Psychology, Linguistics]
    lawsOfThought --> sci2[Mathematics, Philosophy]
    turing --> sci3[Psychology, Linguistics]
    rationalAgent --> sci4[Mathematics, Engineering]
```

## 4.3 AI Capability Taxonomy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ai[Artificial Intelligence]
    ai --> an1[Artificial Narrow Intelligence ANI]
    ai --> ag1[Artificial General Intelligence AGI]
    ai --> as1[Artificial Super Intelligence ASI]

    an1 --> anEx1[Chess engines]
    an1 --> anEx2[Spam filters]
    an1 --> anEx3[Voice assistants]
    an1 --> anEx4[Self-driving cars]

    ag1 --> agEx1[Hypothetical reasoning machine]
    ag1 --> agEx2[Human-level general cognition]

    as1 --> asEx1[Beyond brightest human mind]
    as1 --> asEx2[Consciousness and self-improvement]
```

## 4.4 Internal Architecture of a Learning Agent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    learn[Learning Agent]
    learn --> perfElem[Performance Element]
    learn --> critic[Critic]
    learn --> learnElem[Learning Element]
    learn --> probGen[Problem Generator]

    perfElem -- action --> env2[Environment]
    env2 -- percept + reward --> critic
    critic -- feedback --> learnElem
    learnElem -- updates --> perfElem
    probGen -- new exploratory actions --> perfElem
```

**Reading the learning-agent diagram:**
- The **Performance Element** is the actual decision-maker (the equivalent of $f$).
- The **Critic** observes the outcome and tells the Learning Element how the agent is doing.
- The **Learning Element** modifies the Performance Element to do better next time.
- The **Problem Generator** suggests exploratory actions (not just greedy ones) so the agent discovers new strategies.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define Artificial Intelligence. List and briefly explain the four approaches to defining AI as given by Russell and Norvig.

**Model Answer:**

Artificial Intelligence is the branch of computer science concerned with building systems that can perform tasks requiring intelligent behaviour when performed by humans. Russell and Norvig categorize AI definitions along two axes — human vs. ideal and thinking vs. acting:

1. **Thinking Humanly** — Systems that model the actual cognitive processes of the human mind (Cognitive Modelling, drawing on psychology and linguistics).
2. **Acting Humanly** — Systems that behave indistinguishably from humans, measured by the **Turing Test** proposed by Alan Turing in 1950.
3. **Thinking Rationally** — Systems that follow the formal **Laws of Thought** using logical inference (drawing on philosophy and mathematics).
4. **Acting Rationally** — The **Rational Agent** approach, which selects actions that maximize expected performance measure. This is the dominant modern definition.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write the same definition twice (e.g., "thinking like humans" and "acting like humans" merged). Examiners deduct 1 mark if all four definitions are not clearly separated.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
What is an Intelligent Agent? Differentiate between the agent function and the agent program.

**Model Answer:**

An **Intelligent Agent** is any entity that perceives its environment through sensors and acts upon that environment through actuators, with the goal of maximizing a performance measure.

- **Agent Function:** The abstract mathematical mapping $f : \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ from the entire history of percepts to the next action. It is a description of *what* the agent does.
- **Agent Program:** The concrete implementation of $f$ that runs on a physical architecture. It is the *how* of the agent's behaviour.

For example, the agent function of a chess program is a table mapping board states to moves; the agent program is the actual $\alpha$–$\beta$ search algorithm and evaluation function running on a CPU.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Writing the agent function in the wrong notation. Always use $f : \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$. Do not write $f : \mathcal{P} \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ (without the star). The star denotes the Kleene closure (any finite sequence).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **PEAS framework** and construct a complete PEAS specification for an **autonomous medical diagnosis robot** that interviews a patient, asks symptoms, and recommends a diagnosis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **structure of an intelligent agent** with a labelled diagram. Differentiate between the four main agent architectures: simple reflex, model-based reflex, goal-based, and utility-based. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**PEAS** stands for Performance Measure, Environment, Actuators, Sensors. It is the standard specification language for any AI-in-Robotics task.

1. **Performance Measure:** Diagnostic accuracy (recall and precision of the disease), patient comfort, number of questions asked, referral correctness, minimization of false negatives, compliance with medical ethics, response time.

2. **Environment:** Patient population (varied age, language, medical history), clinic/hospital setting, electronic health record system, network connectivity, intermittent patient cooperation, varying ambient noise, multiple possible disease conditions.

3. **Actuators:** Speech synthesizer (asking questions), screen display (showing information), keyboard/touchscreen input, prescription printer, referral-email sender, alert system for emergencies.

4. **Sensors:** Microphone (voice input), camera (facial expression, lip reading), keyboard/touchscreen, heart-rate sensor (wearable integration), body-temperature sensor, electronic health record (EHR) database interface.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Structure of an Intelligent Agent** (with architecture):

1. **Sensors** capture raw physical signals from the environment and convert them into percepts.
2. The **Agent Program** processes the percepts using its internal logic (rules, search, learning, or utility computation).
3. **Actuators** execute the chosen action back into the environment.
4. A **Performance Measure** evaluates success externally.

**Differences between agent types** (compact comparison):

| Type | Internal State | Reasoning Depth | Robotics Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Simple Reflex | None | Condition → action | Thermostat, basic bumper robot |
| Model-Based Reflex | Internal world model | Predict outcome of action | Mars rover, indoor vacuum |
| Goal-Based | Goal description | Plan a sequence to reach the goal | Mobile robot path planner |
| Utility-Based | Utility function | Maximize expected utility among trade-offs | Autonomous driving decision-making |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** For 7-mark sub-parts, students who skip the diagram lose 1–2 marks. Always draw the agent–environment interaction block diagram with arrows in *both* directions.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Discuss the **historical evolution of AI**, dividing the timeline into at least four distinct phases. Mention at least two landmark events in each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Classify the following environments using the seven-dimension framework: (i) Automated taxi driving in a busy city, (ii) Internet shopping website recommendation engine, (iii) Robotic vacuum cleaner in a living room, (iv) Crossword puzzle solving. Justify each classification. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Phase 1 — Gestation (1943–1956):**
- 1943: McCulloch and Pitts publish the first mathematical model of an artificial neuron.
- 1950: Alan Turing publishes *"Computing Machinery and Intelligence"* and proposes the Turing Test.
- 1955: Allen Newell and Herbert Simon create the *Logic Theorist*, considered the first AI program.
- 1956: The **Dartmouth Conference** organized by McCarthy coins the term "Artificial Intelligence" and is widely regarded as the birth of the field.

**Phase 2 — Early Enthusiasm and First AI Winter (1956–1980):**
- 1960s: Development of LISP by John McCarthy; early work on neural networks (Perceptron by Rosenblatt, 1958).
- 1969: Minsky and Papert publish *Perceptrons*, exposing the limitations of single-layer networks and contributing to the first AI winter.
- 1970s: SHRDLU natural-language program; PROLOG developed; expert systems emerge (MYCIN, DENDRAL).
- Knowledge-representation languages and frame-based systems.

**Phase 3 — Expert Systems Boom and Second AI Winter (1980–2000):**
- 1980s: Commercial expert systems (XCON, R1) deployed; first AI industry boom.
- Late 1980s–1990s: Collapse of the expert-system market leads to the second AI winter.
- 1997: **IBM Deep Blue defeats world chess champion Garry Kasparov**, marking a symbolic AI milestone.
- 1990s: Rise of probabilistic and Bayesian methods; reinforcement learning gains traction (TD-Gammon).

**Phase 4 — Modern Deep Learning Era (2000–Present):**
- 2006: Geoffrey Hinton popularizes "deep learning" and layer-wise pretraining.
- 2011: IBM Watson wins Jeopardy! against human champions.
- 2012: **AlexNet** wins ImageNet by a huge margin, sparking the deep-learning revolution.
- 2014: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) by Ian Goodfellow.
- 2017: **Transformer architecture** introduced in *"Attention is All You Need"*.
- 2020s: Foundation models (GPT, BERT, LLaMA), reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), generative AI.

#### Model Solution for (b)

Seven-dimension classification:

**(i) Automated Taxi in Busy City:** Partially observable, **stochastic**, **sequential**, **dynamic**, **continuous**, **multi-agent**, **known** (transition rules are physical).

**(ii) Internet Shopping Recommendation:** **Fully observable** (it knows your history), **stochastic** (user behaviour is random), **episodic** (each recommendation is somewhat independent), **static** (the catalogue does not change during a recommendation), **discrete** (finite items), **multi-agent** (other shoppers), **known** (rules of the system are known).

**(iii) Robotic Vacuum Cleaner:** **Partially observable** (cannot see under furniture), **stochastic** (children leave toys), **sequential** (where it cleans next depends on history), **dynamic** (chairs move), **continuous** (wheel velocity), **single-agent** (if alone in the room), **known** (physics of the room).

**(iv) Crossword Puzzle:** **Fully observable** (you see all clues), **deterministic**, **sequential** (each letter changes the board), **static** (board does not change while you think), **discrete**, **single-agent**, **known**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** For (b), examiners frequently deduct marks if a student writes "deterministic" without justifying *why*. Always write one sentence per dimension.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Foundations of AI)**

### Core Definitions to Memorize
- **AI:** The science of making machines perform tasks that require intelligence.
- **Rational Agent:** An agent that selects the action that maximizes expected performance measure, given the percept history and built-in knowledge.
- **Agent Function:** $f : \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$.
- **Agent Program:** Concrete implementation of $f$.
- **Turing Test:** Operational definition of machine intelligence (1950, 5-min text conversation, $\geq 70\%$ indistinguishability).
- **PEAS:** Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors.

### Five Must-Know Dates
- **1943** — McCulloch-Pitts neuron.
- **1950** — Turing Test.
- **1956** — Dartmouth Conference (birth of AI).
- **1997** — Deep Blue beats Kasparov.
- **2012** — AlexNet (deep learning explosion).

### Key Formulas
- Agent function: $f : \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$
- Rational action: $a^* = \arg\max_{a} \mathbb{E}[U \vert h]$
- Moore's law: $N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{(t-t_0)/24}$
- Bayes' rule: $P(A \vert B) = \frac{P(B \vert A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$

### Classification Triads to Remember
- **By Capability:** ANI, AGI, ASI.
- **By Functionality:** Reactive, Limited Memory, Theory of Mind, Self-Aware.
- **Agent Architectures:** Simple Reflex, Model-Based, Goal-Based, Utility-Based, Learning.
- **Russell & Norvig Quadrants:** Thinking Humanly, Thinking Rationally, Acting Humanly, Acting Rationally.

### Cross-Disciplinary Origins
- Philosophy → Logic.
- Mathematics → Probability, decision theory.
- Economics → Utility, game theory.
- Neuroscience → Neural networks.
- Psychology → Behaviourism, cognition.
- Linguistics → NLP.
- Computer Science → Algorithms, hardware.
- Robotics → Sensorimotor applications.

### Examiner Mindset Checklist (Before Writing Your Answer)
1. Did I state the **rational agent** definition using $\arg\max$?
2. Did I include a **PEAS** table for any robotics scenario?
3. Did I distinguish between **agent function** and **agent program**?
4. Did I classify the environment using all **seven dimensions**?
5. Did I use the **Russell & Norvig quadrant** framework when defining AI?
6. Did I draw a **labelled diagram** for any 7-mark sub-part?
7. Did I avoid claiming AGI or ASI currently exist?

> [!TIP]
> **Final Advice:** KTU Module 1 is a *definition-and-taxonomy* module. Marks are awarded for correctly using the **right vocabulary** in the **right place**. The more precisely you use the standard terms (PEAS, agent function, Russell-Norvig quadrants, ANI/AGI/ASI), the higher your score.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: A Brief Historical Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERUT614 - INTRODUCTION TO AI IN ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** A Brief Historical Overview ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **A Brief Historical Overview of AI in Robotics** refers to the chronological study of how two originally independent fields — **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** (the science of making machines reason, learn, and decide) and **Robotics** (the engineering discipline of designing and operating physical autonomous agents) — gradually converged into a single interdisciplinary domain where intelligent algorithms control physical embodied systems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** > This topic forms the *foundation* of the course. Examiners frequently use it to set the context for almost every subsequent module. A student is expected to know the *timeline*, the *key contributors*, the *defining events*, and the *paradigm shifts* (e.g., from rule-based systems to learning-based systems) that shaped modern AI-driven robotics. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine two rivers flowing side by side for centuries: - **River A — Robotics** begins with mechanical clockwork toys and eventually grows into industrial robotic arms that weld car bodies in factories. These robots are precise, strong, and repeatable — but they are also "blind" and "dumb" in the sense that they cannot perceive or adapt. - **River B — AI** begins as a philosophical question ("Can a machine think?") and evolves into algorithms that play chess, understand language, and recognize faces. These algorithms are "smart" but exist only in software, with no body and no physical interaction with the real world. **The convergence of AI + Robotics** is the moment these two rivers merge into one mighty stream — creating machines that can *think AND act*. A modern service robot, a self-driving car, or a Mars rover is the living proof of this merger. > [!NOTE] > **Three Defining Metrics of Progress in AI-Robotics:** > 1. **Autonomy** — ability to operate without human intervention. > 2. **Adaptability** — ability to learn from new environments/data. > 3. **Embodiment** — coupling of computation to physical sensors and actuators. ### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Scheme From a board-exam perspective, this topic contributes to the *Course Outcomes* **CO1 (Fundamentals of AI and Robotics)** and lays conceptual groundwork for CO2 (Robot perception) and CO3 (Robot learning). Many 3-mark and 14-mark questions have historically been framed around "trace the evolution of AI in robotics" or "explain the role of Shakey the Robot." ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Summary ### 2.1 The Two Parallel Roots #### 2.1.1 Root 1 — The Robotics Lineage (Mechanical Bodies) The dream of artificial beings is ancient: - **~350 BCE** — Greek mathematician **Archytas of Tarentum** built a mechanical wooden dove propelled by steam — often considered the first recorded "robot." - **1206 CE** — **Al-Jazari**, a Muslim engineer, documented programmable humanoid automata in *The Book of Knowledge of Ingenious Mechanical Devices*. - **1738** — **Jacques de Vaucanson** built the Digesting Duck, a mechanical automaton that mimicked biological behavior. - **1921** — Czech writer **Karel Čapek** coined the word **"Robot"** in his play *R.U.R. (Rossum's Universal Robots)*. - **1954** — **George Devol** patented the first digitally operated programmable robotic arm. - **1961** — **Unimate**, the first industrial robot, was installed at a General Motors plant to handle die casting. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Pre-AI robots were **pre-programmed, repetitive, and blind**. They could execute motion sequences but could not perceive or decide. #### 2.1.2 Root 2 — The AI Lineage (Computational Minds) - **1950** — **Alan Turing** published *"Computing Machinery and Intelligence"* and proposed the **Turing Test** as a measure of machine intelligence. - **1956** — The **Dartmouth Conference** (organized by John McCarthy, Marvin Minsky, Claude Shannon, and Nathaniel Rochester) is universally considered the **birth of AI** as an academic discipline. The term *"Artificial Intelligence"* was coined here. - **1958** — **John McCarthy** invented **LISP**, the dominant AI programming language for decades. - **1960s** — Early AI programs like **ELIZA** (chatbot) and **Logic Theorist** (theorem prover) demonstrated symbolic reasoning. - **1970s–80s** — Rise of **Expert Systems** (e.g., MYCIN, DENDRAL) and the first **AI Winter** (1974–1980) caused by unmet expectations. ### 2.2 The Convergence — Birth of AI-Driven Robotics The pivotal moment was the creation of **Shakey the Robot (1966–1972)** at the Stanford Research Institute (SRI). | Parameter | Description | |---|---| | **Full Name** | Shakey the Robot | | **Institution** | Stanford Research Institute (SRI International) | | **Time Period** | 1966 – 1972 | | **Creator** | Charles Rosen, Nils Nilsson, and team | | **Sensors** | TV camera, range finder, bump detectors | | **Software Paradigm** | STRIPS planner, A* search, logical reasoning | | **Significance** | First mobile robot to reason about its own actions | Shakey combined *perception* (sensors), *reasoning* (AI planning), and *action* (motor control) — making it the **first true AI-in-Robotics system**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Shakey's Paradigm (Sense → Plan → Act):** > This became the foundational architecture of classical AI robotics. The robot first **senses** the world, builds an internal model, uses an **AI planner** to compute a sequence of actions, and then **acts**. Decades of robotics research (STRIPS, hierarchical task networks, classical planning) emerged from this single project. ### 2.3 The Three Eras of AI in Robotics | Era | Period | Core Idea | Limitation | |---|---|---|---| | **Era 1 — Symbolic / Rule-Based** | 1960s – 1980s | Hand-coded rules, logic, search | Brittle; cannot handle uncertainty | | **Era 2 — Probabilistic / Behavioural** | 1990s – 2000s | Bayesian methods, SLAM, subsumption architecture | Still required heavy engineering | | **Era 3 — Learning-Based / Deep** | 2010s – Present | Neural networks, reinforcement learning, LLMs | Data-hungry, opaque (black-box) | ### 2.4 Modern Milestones (2010s – 2020s) - **2012** — **AlexNet** (deep convolutional neural network) wins ImageNet, igniting the deep-learning revolution. CNNs rapidly enter robotics for vision. - **2013** — **DeepMind** publishes work on **Deep Q-Networks (DQN)** that learn to play Atari games from raw pixels — proving end-to-end learning is viable. - **2014** — **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)** introduced by Ian Goodfellow. - **2015** — **DeepMind's AlphaGo** defeats world champion Lee Sedol — a landmark in reinforcement learning. - **2016** — **ROS (Robot Operating System)** becomes the de-facto middleware standard for research and industry. - **2017** — **Transformer architecture** ("Attention is All You Need") revolutionizes NLP and later vision. - **2018** — **OpenAI Five** defeats human teams in Dota 2 (multi-agent RL). - **2020s** — **Foundation Models and Large Language Models (LLMs)** like GPT-4, PaLM, and vision-language-action (VLA) models such as **RT-2 (Google DeepMind, 2023)** allow robots to follow natural-language instructions and generalize to novel tasks. - **2024** — Rise of **embodied AI** and humanoid robots (e.g., Figure 01, Tesla Optimus, Unitree H1) powered by multimodal AI. > [!NOTE] > **Key Concept — Foundation Models in Robotics:** > Modern AI robotics is moving away from task-specific programming toward **general-purpose models** trained on internet-scale data that can be transferred to physical robots.
