This is a **paradigm shift** analogous to the move from hand-crafted features to deep learning in computer vision. ### 2.5 Key People Every KTU Student Must Know | Person | Contribution | Era | |---|---|---| | **Alan Turing** | Turing Test, foundations of computation | 1950 | | **John McCarthy** | Coined "Artificial Intelligence", LISP, Dartmouth | 1956 | | **Marvin Minsky** | Co-founder of AI Lab at MIT, neural networks pioneer | 1950s–60s | | **George Devol** | First programmable industrial robot | 1954 | | **Joseph Engelberger** | Father of industrial robotics, founded Unimation | 1961 | | **Charles Rosen / Nils Nilsson** | Shakey the Robot, STRIPS planner | 1966–72 | | **Hans Moravec** | Pioneered mobile robot navigation and 3D mapping | 1980s–90s | | **Rodney Brooks** | Subsumption architecture, behaviour-based robotics | 1986 | | **Sebastian Thrun** | Stanley (DARPA Grand Challenge winner), self-driving cars | 2005 | | **Geoffrey Hinton / Yann LeCun** | Deep learning pioneers, CNNs | 2010s | | **Demis Hassabis** | DeepMind, AlphaGo, AlphaFold | 2010s–20s | ### 2.6 The "Moravec's Paradox" — A Critical Lesson From History In 1988, **Hans Moravec** observed: > *"It is comparatively easy to make computers exhibit adult-level performance on intelligence tests or playing checkers, and difficult or impossible to give them the skills of a one-year-old when it comes to perception and mobility."* This paradox explains why **AI in robotics is harder than AI in games or chatbots** — physical embodiment, sensor noise, and real-time constraints make robotics a fundamentally harder problem. ### 2.7 Summary Cheat-Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 — One-Glance Revision Box:** > - AI born in **1956 (Dartmouth)**; word *Robot* coined in **1921**. > - First industrial robot: **Unimate, 1961**. > - First AI robot: **Shakey, 1966–72**. > - Three eras: **Symbolic → Probabilistic → Learning-Based**. > - Modern era driven by **Deep Learning (2012)** and **Foundation Models / LLMs (2020s)**. > - Key challenge from history: **Moravec's Paradox**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Logical Flow Because this topic is historical and conceptual, the "derivations" below take the form of **structured logical arguments** that examiners expect students to reproduce. Each sub-section mirrors a high-scoring answer structure. ### 3.1 Analytical Argument 1 — Why Pre-1960 Robots Were "Dumb" **Step 1:** Define the capabilities of pre-AI industrial robots. - They could repeat a fixed motion sequence (e.g., spot-welding on a car chassis). - They had no sensors to perceive environment. - They had no internal model of the world. **Step 2:** Identify the missing component. - The missing component is **intelligence** — specifically, *perception*, *reasoning*, and *decision-making*. **Step 3:** State the consequence. - Such robots cannot adapt to *any* variation. If a part is misplaced by 2 cm, the robot fails. - This is why classical industrial robots are kept in **cages** away from humans. **Conclusion:** Pre-AI robots were *programmed*, not *intelligent*. The need to combine AI and robotics became evident by the mid-1960s. ### 3.2 Analytical Argument 2 — How Shakey Established the Sense-Plan-Act Paradigm **Step 1 — Sense:** Shakey used a TV camera and range finder to perceive the room. A vision module converted raw pixels into symbolic descriptions of objects (e.g., "door", "box", "wall"). **Step 2 — Model:** These symbolic descriptions were fed into a **world model** — an internal data structure representing the current state of the environment. **Step 3 — Plan:** The **STRIPS planner** (Stanford Research Institute Problem Solver) was used to search for a sequence of actions that would transform the current state into a goal state. Mathematically, this is framed as a search problem in state-space: $$ S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\} $$ where each $s_i$ is a symbolic world state, and the planner finds a path from $s_0$ (initial) to $s_n$ (goal) using operators defined by *preconditions* and *effects*. **Step 4 — Act:** The first action in the plan is executed. Sensors re-perceive the world, the model is updated, and the cycle repeats. **Conclusion:** This *closed-loop* cycle (Sense → Plan → Act → Sense → ...) is the **classical architecture of AI-driven robotics** and is the basis of modern frameworks like ROS-based cognitive architectures. ### 3.3 Analytical Argument 3 — The Three Paradigm Shifts and Their Drivers | Shift | Driver / Trigger | Year | Outcome | |---|---|---|---| | Symbolic → Probabilistic | Inability of rules to handle sensor noise; rise of Bayesian methods | ~1990s | Robust SLAM, probabilistic perception | | Probabilistic → Behavioural | Brooks argued that *intelligence emerges from behaviour*, not from internal models | 1986 | Subsumption architecture, simple reactive robots | | Behavioural → Learning-Based | Explosion of data and GPU compute power | 2012 (AlexNet) | Deep RL, imitation learning, end-to-end policies | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** Examiners love to ask: *"Explain how AI in robotics evolved from rule-based systems to learning-based systems."* Memorize the three rows above and you can score full marks. ### 3.4 Analytical Argument 4 — Why the 2010s Were the "Cambrian Explosion" of AI-Robotics **Step 1 — Compute:** GPUs (originally for graphics) turned out to be massively parallel processors ideal for matrix multiplication — the math at the heart of neural networks. **Step 2 — Data:** The internet, smartphones, and ImageNet provided the labelled datasets that neural networks needed to learn from. **Step 3 — Algorithms:** Three breakthroughs converged: - Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (Krizhevsky et al., 2012) - Deep Reinforcement Learning (Mnih et al., 2013) - Self-supervised Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017) **Step 4 — Application:** Robotics researchers could now replace hand-engineered perception (e.g., SIFT, HOG features) with learned features, and replace hand-coded controllers with learned policies. **Conclusion:** The 2010s broke the long-standing bottleneck in AI-robotics — *the need to hand-engineer every capability*. Modern systems learn them from data. ### 3.5 Analytical Argument 5 — The Modern Era: From Task-Specific to General-Purpose Robots **Step 1 — Limitation of the previous era:** Even with deep learning, robots of the 2010s were trained for *one specific task* (e.g., grasp a cup). Transferring to a new task required retraining from scratch. **Step 2 — Foundation models as a solution:** Large models trained on internet-scale data (text, images, video) develop *general representations* of the world. When fine-tuned for robotics, they bring with them a "common-sense" understanding that would otherwise take millions of robotic trials to acquire. **Step 3 — Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models:** Models like **RT-2 (Robotics Transformer 2)** output *motor commands* directly from camera images and natural-language instructions, unifying perception, language, and action in a single neural network. **Step 4 — Embodied AI:** The frontier is *embodied AI* — agents that learn by interacting with the physical world, in simulation (e.g., NVIDIA Isaac Sim) and in reality. **Conclusion:** The historical arc of AI in robotics is the arc of *increasing generality* — from a single fixed motion to a single trained task to potentially any task expressible in natural language. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Timeline of Key Milestones (Mermaid Block-Level Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Ancient Automata
Archytas 350 BCE] --> B[Al Jazari 1206 CE] B --> C[Word Robot coined
Capek 1921] C --> D[Turing Test 1950] D --> E[Dartmouth Conference
AI born 1956] E --> F[Unimate 1961
First industrial robot] F --> G[Shakey 1966 to 1972
First AI robot] G --> H[AI Winter 1974 to 1980] H --> I[Subsumption
Brooks 1986] I --> J[Moravec Paradox 1988] J --> K[Probabilistic Robotics
Thrun 2005] K --> L[AlexNet 2012
Deep learning era] L --> M[AlphaGo 2015] M --> N[Transformers 2017] N --> O[Foundation Models
and VLA 2020s] O --> P[Embodied AI
and Humanoids 2024] ``` ### 4.2 Two-Lineage Convergence Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph R[Robotics Lineage] R1[Mechanical Automata] --> R2[Industrial Robots
Unimate 1961] R2 --> R3[Mobile Robots
Shakey 1966] R3 --> R4[Humanoid Robots
Asimo 2000] R4 --> R5[Modern Humanoids
Optimus Figure 2024] end subgraph A[AI Lineage] A1[Turing Test 1950] --> A2[Dartmouth 1956] A2 --> A3[Expert Systems 1980s] A3 --> A4[Deep Learning 2012] A4 --> A5[LLMs and VLA 2020s] end R3 -. merges with .-> A3 R5 -. merges with .-> A5 ``` ### 4.3 The Sense-Plan-Act Cycle (Shakey Paradigm) ```mermaid flowchart LR Sensors[Perception
Cameras LIDAR IMU] --> Model[World Model
Symbolic State] Model --> Planner[AI Planner
STRIPS A star Search] Planner --> Actions[Motor Commands
Wheel and Arm Drivers] Actions --> World[Physical World] World --> Sensors ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The arrows form a *closed loop*. Every cycle, the robot re-perceives the world and updates its plan. This is the historical foundation of all modern robotic control architectures, including ROS-based systems. ### 4.4 Three Eras Visual Summary ```mermaid flowchart LR E1[Era 1
Symbolic 1960 to 1980
Rules and Logic] -->|AI Winter| E2[Era 2
Probabilistic 1990 to 2010
Bayes SLAM Behaviours] E2 -->|Deep Learning 2012| E3[Era 3
Learning Based 2010 to Present
Deep RL and Foundation Models] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]** *Define the term "Robot" as coined by Karel Čapek and explain its evolution from fictional concept to industrial reality.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The word **"Robot"** was coined by Czech playwright **Karel Čapek** in his 1921 play *R.U.R. (Rossum's Universal Robots)*, derived from the Czech word *robota* meaning "forced labor." Originally, robots in the play were artificial biological workers. The term transitioned to engineering reality in **1954** when **George Devol** patented the first programmable robotic arm, and in **1961** when the **Unimate** robot was installed at a General Motors plant for die-casting. The modern industrial robot, as defined by ISO 8373, is an *automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator programmable in three or more axes*. (3 marks) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]** *Write a short note on the Dartmouth Conference of 1956 and its significance.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Dartmouth Conference** (officially the *Dartmouth Summer Research Project on Artificial Intelligence*) was held in the summer of **1956** at Dartmouth College, USA. It was organized by **John McCarthy, Marvin Minsky, Claude Shannon, and Nathaniel Rochester**. The conference is universally recognized as the **birth of Artificial Intelligence as a formal academic discipline**, and the term *Artificial Intelligence* itself was coined by John McCarthy for this workshop. The conference proposed that *"every aspect of learning or any other feature of intelligence can in principle be so precisely described that a machine can be made to simulate it."* It set the research agenda for AI for decades to come. (3 marks) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question]** *With suitable milestones, trace the historical evolution of Artificial Intelligence in Robotics from ancient automata to modern embodied AI systems.* #### **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Ancient and Pre-Modern Era (7 Marks)** **(i) Ancient Mechanical Automata (1 mark):** The earliest recorded "robot" is attributed to **Archytas of Tarentum (~350 BCE)**, who built a steam-propelled mechanical pigeon. **Hero of Alexandria** documented several automata in his treatise *Pneumatica*. **Al-Jazari (1206 CE)** in his book *The Book of Knowledge of Ingenious Mechanical Devices* described programmable humanoid automata using hydraulic and cam mechanisms — a remarkable 800 years ahead of the European Renaissance. **(ii) Birth of the Word "Robot" (1 mark):** In **1921**, Czech writer **Karel Čapek** introduced the word *robot* in his play *R.U.R. (Rossum's Universal Robots)*, from the Czech *robota* meaning "forced labor." The term was popularized globally and later adopted by the engineering community. **(iii) Foundations of AI (2 marks):** In **1950**, **Alan Turing** proposed the *Turing Test* in his paper *"Computing Machinery and Intelligence,"* asking *"Can machines think?"* In **1956**, the **Dartmouth Conference** — organized by John McCarthy, Marvin Minsky, Claude Shannon, and Nathaniel Rochester — formally established AI as a field. The term *Artificial Intelligence* was coined here, and the optimistic belief that "aspects of learning can be precisely described and simulated" was born. **(iv) First Industrial Robot (1 mark):** In **1954**, **George Devol** invented the first digitally operated, programmable robotic arm. In **1961**, the **Unimate** robot (developed by Devol and Joseph Engelberger) was installed at a General Motors plant in New Jersey, performing die-casting operations. This marked the dawn of industrial robotics. **(v) Birth of AI-Driven Robotics — Shakey (2 marks):** Between **1966 and 1972**, **Shakey the Robot** was developed at the **Stanford Research Institute (SRI)** by Charles Rosen, Nils Nilsson, and team. Shakey was the **first mobile robot to combine perception, reasoning, and action**. It used a TV camera, range finder, and bump sensors, with a **STRIPS planner** for symbolic AI planning. Shakey established the foundational **Sense → Plan → Act** paradigm that influenced all subsequent AI-robotics research. **Part (b) — Modern Era and Future (7 Marks)** **(i) Expert Systems and First AI Winter (1 mark):** The 1970s and 1980s saw the rise of **expert systems** (e.g., MYCIN for medical diagnosis). The first **AI Winter (1974–1980)** occurred due to over-promising and under-delivering, leading to reduced funding. **(ii) Behavioural and Probabilistic Robotics (2 marks):** In **1986**, **Rodney Brooks** proposed the **subsumption architecture**, arguing that intelligent behaviour emerges from layered reactive modules rather than symbolic world models. In **1988**, **Hans Moravec** articulated **Moravec's Paradox** — that sensorimotor skills are far harder for machines than abstract reasoning. The 1990s–2000s saw the rise of **probabilistic robotics** (Bayesian filters, SLAM) led by researchers like **Sebastian Thrun** (Stanley, 2005 DARPA Grand Challenge winner). **(iii) Deep Learning Revolution (2 marks):** In **2012**, **AlexNet** (Krizhevsky, Sutskever, Hinton) won the ImageNet competition by a huge margin using a deep CNN, triggering the modern deep-learning revolution. In **2013**, **DeepMind's DQN** showed that neural networks could learn to play Atari games from raw pixels. In **2015**, **AlphaGo** defeated world champion Lee Sedol. In **2017**, the **Transformer** architecture (Vaswani et al., *"Attention is All You Need"*) revolutionized NLP and eventually vision. **(iv) Foundation Models and Embodied AI (2 marks):** The **2020s** are characterized by **large foundation models** (GPT-4, PaLM) and **Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models** such as **RT-2 (Google DeepMind, 2023)**, which directly map camera input and natural language to robot motor commands. The frontier is **embodied AI** — humanoid robots (Figure 01, Tesla Optimus, Unitree H1) powered by multimodal AI that learn from physical interaction. This represents the historical arc of AI in robotics: from *pre-programmed motion* (Unimate) → *symbolic reasoning* (Shakey) → *learned perception and control* (deep learning) → *general-purpose embodied intelligence* (foundation models). --- **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]** *Explain the contributions of Shakey the Robot to the field of AI in Robotics. Discuss how its Sense-Plan-Act paradigm influences modern robotic architectures.* #### **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Shakey the Robot: Contributions (7 Marks)** **(i) Historical Context and Development (2 marks):** **Shakey the Robot** was developed at the **Stanford Research Institute (SRI International)** between **1966 and 1972** under the leadership of Charles Rosen, with key contributions from **Nils Nilsson** and Bertram Raphael. It was funded by the U.S. Defense Department's **DARPA**. Shakey was a mobile robot (about 6 feet tall) operating in a specially instrumented room, designed to be a *general-purpose mobile automaton* — the first to combine perception, reasoning, and action in a single integrated system. **(ii) Hardware Components (2 marks):** Shakey was equipped with: - A **black-and-white TV camera** for visual perception - An **optical range finder** for distance measurement - **Bump detectors** on its body for collision detection - **On-board processors and a radio link** to a large PDP-10 computer for AI computation (computation was off-board by modern standards) - **Drive wheels and a motor** for locomotion **(iii) Software: STRIPS Planner and Symbolic AI (3 marks):** Shakey's software was the heart of its innovation. It used: - **STRIPS (Stanford Research Institute Problem Solver)** — a planner that searches for a sequence of actions to reach a goal state. Each action is defined by *preconditions* and *effects*. - **A\*** search algorithm for efficient path-finding. - **Symbolic world modelling** — converting sensor data into discrete symbols (e.g., "Door-1 is at (x,y)") that the planner could reason about. - **Logical reasoning** to handle disjunctive goals and partial information. Shakey could be given high-level commands (e.g., *"Push the block off the platform"*) and would autonomously perceive, plan, and execute the task. **Part (b) — Influence on Modern Robotic Architectures (7 Marks)** **(i) The Sense-Plan-Act Paradigm (3 marks):** Shakey established the **Sense → Plan → Act** paradigm, the foundational architecture of classical AI robotics: - **Sense:** Sensor data is processed to build a model of the world. - **Plan:** An AI planner (e.g., STRIPS, HTN, PDDL-based) generates a sequence of actions. - **Act:** The actions are executed; the cycle repeats. This *deliberative architecture* dominated robotics research for decades and is the direct ancestor of modern task-planning systems in ROS (Robot Operating System), including *MoveIt!* (motion planning) and *SMACH* (hierarchical task management). **(ii) Foundational Algorithmic Contributions (2 marks):** The algorithms developed for Shakey — particularly **STRIPS and A\*** — are still taught in every AI and robotics course and form the basis of modern planning systems. Modern descendants include **PDDL planners** (Fast Downward, OPTIC) used in NASA, autonomous driving, and logistics. **(iii) Limitations and Lessons for Modern AI (2 marks):** Shakey's symbolic approach had critical limitations: it could not handle *sensor noise*, *real-time constraints*, or *uncertainty*. This motivated the **probabilistic robotics revolution** of the 1990s (SLAM, Kalman filters) and ultimately the **learning-based revolution** of the 2010s (deep learning, reinforcement learning). However, even modern neural-network-based systems still use the *closed-loop sense-act* structure pioneered by Shakey. The lesson: **reasoning + perception + action** — the trinity Shakey first demonstrated — remains the *conceptual skeleton* of every AI robot today. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Ways Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing "Robot" origin:** Many students write that Isaac Asimov coined the word *robot*. **Wrong.** Karel Čapek coined the term in 1921; Asimov popularized it later (and coined the *Three Laws of Robotics* in 1942). > 2. **Wrong year for AI birth:** Some students cite 1950 (Turing Test) as the birth of AI. **The birth of AI as a discipline is 1956 (Dartmouth).** The Turing Test is a *philosophical proposal*, not the founding event. > 3. **Skipping the Sense-Plan-Act architecture:** Whenever a question mentions Shakey, you **must** draw or describe the Sense-Plan-Act cycle. Examiners explicitly award marks for this. > 4. **Omitting the AI Winters:** A common error is presenting AI history as one smooth curve. The two **AI Winters (1974–80 and late 1980s–90s)** are important and frequently asked. > 5. **Ignoring Moravec's Paradox:** This is a *favourite short-answer topic*. Always mention it when discussing why robotics is hard. > 6. **Mixing up Unimate and Shakey:** *Unimate (1961) = first industrial robot, no AI. Shakey (1966–72) = first AI robot.* Do not confuse them. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — A Brief Historical Overview of AI in Robotics** > - The word **Robot** was coined by **Karel Čapek (1921)**; the word **Artificial Intelligence** was coined by **John McCarthy (1956)**. > - **Dartmouth Conference (1956)** = birth of AI; **Turing Test (1950)** = philosophical foundation of AI. > - **Unimate (1961)** at General Motors = first industrial robot; purely pre-programmed, no AI. > - **Shakey the Robot (1966–1972)** at SRI = first AI-driven mobile robot; introduced the **Sense-Plan-Act** paradigm and the **STRIPS planner**. > - **Two AI Winters:** 1974–1980 and late 1980s–1990s; caused by over-promising and under-delivering. > - **Rodney Brooks (1986)** = Subsumption architecture (behavioural robotics, no central model). > - **Hans Moravec (1988)** = Moravec's Paradox: sensorimotor skills are harder than abstract reasoning for machines. > - **Three Eras of AI in Robotics:** Symbolic (1960s–80s) → Probabilistic/Behavioural (1990s–2000s) → Learning-Based (2010s–present). > - **2012 AlexNet** = deep learning revolution; **2015 AlphaGo** = reinforcement learning milestone; **2017 Transformer** = foundation-model revolution. > - **2020s:** Foundation Models (GPT-4), **Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models like RT-2 (2023)**, and **humanoid robots** (Optimus, Figure 01) = frontier of embodied AI. > - **Key people to remember:** Turing, McCarthy, Minsky, Devol, Engelberger, Nilsson, Brooks, Moravec, Thrun, Hinton, Hassabis. > - **Historical lesson:** Progress in AI-robotics is driven by the interplay of (a) algorithmic breakthroughs, (b) data availability, and (c) compute power — a pattern that has repeated across all three eras.

topics.title: Intelligent Agents and Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT614 - INTRODUCTION TO AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Intelligent Agents and Environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Robotics, an **Intelligent Agent** is formally defined as any entity that can be viewed as **perceiving its environment through sensors** and **acting upon that environment through actuators**, with the objective of achieving a designed performance measure. An Intelligent Agent is therefore characterized by a continuous **Perception → Reasoning/Decision → Action** cycle that maps sequences of percepts to actions, known as the **agent function**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Russell & Norvig Paradigm):**
> An **agent** is anything that can be viewed as perceiving its environment through sensors and acting upon that environment through actuators. A **rational agent** is one that does the *right thing* — meaning its actions maximize its expected performance measure given the available percept sequence and its prior built-in knowledge.

The **Environment** is the surrounding world with which the agent interacts. In a robotic context, the environment may be physical (real world) or simulated (virtual world, e.g., Gazebo, Webots).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Delivery Robot

Imagine a **floor-cleaning robot** in a college hostel. The robot has:
- **Sensors** = camera, infrared cliff sensors, bumpers → these are its *eyes and skin*
- **Actuators** = wheels, brush motor, suction pump → these are its *hands and legs*
- **Environment** = the hostel floor (rooms, walls, furniture, dust, humans walking)
- **Performance Measure** = area cleaned per hour, battery efficiency, no collisions

Just as a student navigates the campus corridor using senses and limbs to reach the canteen, the robot navigates the hostel using sensors and motors. The *intelligence* of the agent lies in choosing the **optimal action** (turn left, stop, clean harder) based on what it *perceives* and what it *wants* to achieve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** An *agent* is not just a program — it is the **program + sensors + actuators + environment + performance measure** working as a unified system. A pure calculator is *not* an agent because it does not act upon an environment.

### 1.3 Core Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Agent** | Entity perceiving environment via sensors and acting via actuators |
| **Agent Function** | Mathematical mapping $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ from percept history to action |
| **Agent Program** | Concrete implementation of the agent function running on the physical architecture |
| **Rationality** | Choosing the action that maximizes expected performance measure on the percept sequence |
| **Omniscience** | Knowing the actual outcome of actions (impossible in real world) |
| **Autonomy** | Ability to compensate for partial/incorrect prior knowledge via learning |
| **Percept** | Agent's perceptual input at a single instant |
| **Percept Sequence** | Complete history of all percepts received so far |
| **PEAS** | Acronym for Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors — task environment descriptor |

### 1.4 Why Intelligent Agents Matter in Robotics

In KTU's outcome-based framework, intelligent agents form the **backbone of every autonomous robotic system** — from autonomous vehicles to industrial manipulators. Without a well-defined agent, no AI algorithm (search, learning, vision) can be properly evaluated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 1 explicitly demands understanding of *(a) Agent types, (b) Rationality vs Omniscience, (c) PEAS specification, (d) Environment classifications, and (e) Structure of intelligent agents*. Mastery of these is a guaranteed question in ESE Part A or Part B.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Agent Function — The Mathematical Heart of an Agent

An agent's behavior is described completely by its **agent function**, which maps every possible percept sequence to an action.

$$f : \mathcal{P}^* \longrightarrow \mathcal{A}$$

where $\mathcal{P}^*$ denotes the set of *all finite sequences* of percepts, and $\mathcal{A}$ is the set of possible actions. For a vacuum-cleaner agent with two locations (A and B) and a dirt sensor, an example agent function is shown in the table below.

> [!NOTE]
> **An agent program is not the same as an agent function.** The function is an *abstract mathematical mapping*; the program is a *concrete implementation* that must run on limited hardware with bounded memory.

### 2.2 Rationality — The "Doing the Right Thing" Principle

A **rational agent** selects, for each possible percept sequence, the action that is expected to **maximize its performance measure**, given the evidence provided by the percept sequence and the agent's built-in prior knowledge.

**Four Key Factors of Rationality:**

1. **Performance Measure** — what defines success (e.g., cleanliness, speed, safety).
2. **Prior Knowledge** — what the agent knows about the environment before operating.
3. **Possible Actions** — what the agent *can* do given its actuators.
4. **Percept Sequence** — everything the agent has perceived so far.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rationality $\neq$ Omniscience.** Omniscience = knowing the true outcome of all actions (impossible). Rationality = maximizing **expected** performance based on available information. A rational agent can still act sub-optimally due to *information asymmetry* or *stochastic environments*.

### 2.3 Task Environment Specification — The PEAS Framework

**PEAS** is the KTU-favoured acronym to fully describe a robotic/AI task:

- **P — Performance Measure:** Quantitative success criterion.
- **E — Environment:** Operating context (real/simulated, known/unknown).
- **A — Actuators:** Mechanical/physical output mechanisms.
- **S — Sensors:** Input perception devices.

**Example PEAS for an Autonomous Driver:**

| Component | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Performance** | Safety, speed, comfort, fuel efficiency, legality |
| **Environment** | Roads, traffic, pedestrians, weather, road signs |
| **Actuators** | Steering wheel, accelerator, brake, signal lights, horn |
| **Sensors** | Cameras, LiDAR, GPS, IMU, ultrasonic, RADAR |

### 2.4 Environment Classification (KTU High-Yield)

Environments are categorized along **7 orthogonal dimensions**:

| Property | Values | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Fully vs Partially Observable** | Full / Partial | Sensors give complete/partial state of world |
| **Deterministic vs Stochastic** | Det. / Stoc. | Next state fully determined by current state + action? |
| **Episodic vs Sequential** | Ep. / Seq. | Decision split into independent episodes? |
| **Static vs Dynamic** | Stat. / Dyn. | Environment changes while agent deliberates? |
| **Discrete vs Continuous** | Disc. / Cont. | Finite/infinite percepts and actions? |
| **Single vs Multi-Agent** | SA / MA | One agent or many interacting agents? |
| **Known vs Unknown** | Known / Unknown | Agent knows the laws/structure of environment? |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorization Tip for KTU:** The 7 properties are exam-favorite. Taxi driving is the *classic example*: *Partially Observable, Stochastic, Sequential, Dynamic, Continuous, Multi-Agent, Known*.

### 2.5 Agent Types — The Five-Tier Hierarchy

From simplest to most capable, intelligent agents are classified into:

1. **Simple Reflex Agent** — Acts only on the *current percept* via condition-action rules. No memory.
2. **Model-Based Reflex Agent** — Maintains an *internal state* tracking unobserved aspects of the world.
3. **Goal-Based Agent** — Uses *goal information* to choose actions that lead to desired states.
4. **Utility-Based Agent** — Uses a *utility function* to choose among conflicting goals, optimizing for *happiness*.
5. **Learning Agent** — Improves performance over time via a *critic*, *learning element*, *problem generator*, and *performance element*.

### 2.6 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical / Formal Form | Key Insight |
|---|---|---|
| Agent Function | $f : \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ | Total history to action mapping |
| Rationality Criterion | $a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{E}[ \text{Perf}(a) \mid \text{percept sequence}]$ | Expected, not actual, performance |
| Learning Update | $Q_{t+1}(s,a) = Q_t(s,a) + \alpha [r_t - Q_t(s,a)]$ | Generic value-update equation |
| Information Gain | $IG(S,A) = H(S) - H(S \vert A)$ | Used in decision tree learning agents |
| Percept Entropy (info richness) | $H(P) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Measures percept uncertainty |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid using the vertical bar `\|` in exam scripts** when writing probability conditions. Use $\text{given}$ or $\vert$ in LaTeX to prevent KTU valuation misreads.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Intelligent agents are deployed in:
- **Autonomous Vehicles** (Waymo, Tesla FSD) — utility-based + learning agents.
- **Industrial Robotic Arms** (KUKA, Fanuc) — goal-based with model-based reflex.
- **Domestic Robots** (Roomba, Pepper) — simple reflex to model-based.
- **Drone Swarms** — multi-agent coordination (game theory).
- **Medical Robotics** (Da Vinci) — model-based + utility-based for surgical decisions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Agent Function to Agent Program

In KTU-style exam questions, students are often asked to **derive the agent function table** for a simplified world. We will now perform a *complete* derivation for the **2-location Vacuum World**.

**Setup of the Vacuum World:**

- Two squares: $A$ and $B$.
- A dirt sensor returns `Clean` or `Dirty`.
- A location sensor returns `A` or `B`.
- Three possible actions: `Left`, `Right`, `Suck`.
- Performance measure: **+1 per clean square per time step, −1 for each movement**.

**Step 1 — Enumerate the Percept Set $\mathcal{P}$**

$$\mathcal{P} = \{(A, Clean), (A, Dirty), (B, Clean), (B, Dirty)\}$$

**Step 2 — Enumerate the Action Set $\mathcal{A}$**

$$\mathcal{A} = \{\text{Left}, \text{Right}, \text{Suck}\}$$

**Step 3 — Construct the Agent Function Table $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$**

The table below shows the rational agent's optimal action for every possible *initial* percept (i.e., percept sequence of length 1).

| Percept Sequence | Optimal Action | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| $(A, Clean)$ | `Right` | Move to B to check for dirt; +0 (no reward) but enables future reward |
| $(A, Dirty)$ | `Suck` | Clean A directly; +1 reward |
| $(B, Clean)$ | `Left` | Move to A to check for dirt |
| $(B, Dirty)$ | `Suck` | Clean B directly; +1 reward |

**Step 4 — Express the Mapping Formally**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f( (A, \text{Clean}) ) &= \text{Right} \\
f( (A, \text{Dirty}) ) &= \text{Suck} \\
f( (B, \text{Clean}) ) &= \text{Left} \\
f( (B, \text{Dirty}) ) &= \text{Suck}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** A *rational* agent selects `Suck` whenever the current square is dirty (immediate +1 reward). When clean, it moves to the other square to maximize the *expected* long-term reward.

### 3.2 Derivation: Rationality Maximization Condition

The mathematical condition for a **rational action** at percept sequence $p_t$ is:

$$
a^*_t = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \; \mathbb{E}\left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k \cdot R(s_{t+k+1}) \;\bigg\vert\; p_t, a, \text{Knowledge} \right]
$$

**Step-by-Step Interpretation:**

- $a^*_t$ = the *optimal* action at time $t$.
- $\arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}}$ = pick the action that yields the maximum.
- $\mathbb{E}[\cdot]$ = expectation operator (rational agents maximize *expected*, not actual, reward).
- $\gamma \in [0,1]$ = discount factor weighting future rewards.
- $R(s)$ = reward function (the performance measure).
- $\vert p_t, a, \text{Knowledge}$ = conditioning on current percept, action, and prior knowledge.

**Simplification (No Discount, Finite Horizon $H$):**

$$
a^*_t = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \; \mathbb{E}\left[ \sum_{k=0}^{H-1} R(s_{t+k+1}) \;\bigg\vert\; p_t, a, \text{Knowledge} \right]
$$

For a **myopic** (short-sighted) agent with $H=1$:

$$
a^*_t = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \; \mathbb{E}\left[ R(s_{t+1}) \;\bigg\vert\; p_t, a, \text{Knowledge} \right]
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping PEAS to Agent Type

The following matrix is a **high-yield KTU analysis** — for a given task, which agent type is the most appropriate?

| Task | Performance | Environment | Best Agent Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Automatic Door | Open if person near | Known, static | Simple Reflex | Only current percept matters |
| Mars Rover | Sample rocks, survive | Partially observable | Model-Based | Needs memory of past positions |
| Chess AI | Win the game | Adversarial, sequential | Goal-Based | Goal = checkmate |
| Self-Driving Car | Safe, fast, legal | Complex, dynamic, multi-agent | Utility-Based | Trade-offs between goals |
| Spoken Dialogue Bot | Customer satisfaction | Episodic, unknown | Learning Agent | Adapts to user behavior |
| Industrial Pick-and-Place | Throughput, accuracy | Fully observable, deterministic | Goal-Based | Fixed target positions |
| Stock Trading Bot | Profit | Stochastic, sequential, multi-agent | Utility + Learning | Must learn and optimize |

### 3.4 Derivation: Information Set & Percept Entropy

For environments with uncertain percepts, the agent's information richness is captured by **Shannon Entropy**:

$$
H(\mathcal{P}) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i
$$

**Example:** A robot's bump sensor returns `Hit` (probability 0.7) or `NoHit` (probability 0.3).

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(\mathcal{P}) &= -[0.7 \log_2 0.7 + 0.3 \log_2 0.3] \\
&= -[0.7 \times (-0.515) + 0.3 \times (-1.737)] \\
&= -[-0.3605 - 0.5211] \\
&= 0.8816 \; \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

Higher entropy → more uncertainty → greater need for a *model-based* or *learning* agent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The entropy computation tells the designer how much *information* the agent must process. Entropy = 0 means perfect certainty (deterministic percept). Entropy = $\log_2 n$ means maximum uncertainty (uniform distribution).

### 3.5 Rationality Boundaries — When Rational Agents Fail

A rational agent can still fail in the following scenarios:

1. **Incomplete Sensors** — Agent cannot perceive the true state (partially observable).
2. **Stochastic Actuators** — Action effect is probabilistic.
3. **Insufficient Computational Resources** — Agent cannot compute the optimal action in real time.
4. **Imperfect Performance Measure** — The designer encoded the wrong objective (e.g., reward hacking).
5. **Adversarial Environments** — Other agents actively try to minimize the agent's reward.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse *limited rationality* (bounded computation) with *irrationality*. A bounded-rational agent is still rational — it simply uses approximations to maximize expected utility within its resource limits.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 General Agent Interacting with Environment

This Mermaid diagram depicts the classic **Perception-Action Loop** between an intelligent agent and its environment.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    env1[Environment] -->|Percepts| sens1[Sensors]
    sens1 --> agent1[Agent Program]
    agent1 -->|Action| act1[Actuators]
    act1 -->|Effect| env1
```

### 4.2 Detailed Agent Architecture (Internal View)

The block-level functional architecture of a generic intelligent agent showing internal processing stages.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ExternalWorld["EXTERNAL ENVIRONMENT"]
        envA[World State]
    end

    subgraph AgentSystem["INTELLIGENT AGENT"]
        sensorA[Sensor Input Stage]
        perceptA[Percept Encoder]
        stateA[State Estimator]
        reasonA[Reasoning and Decision Engine]
        actA[Action Decoder]
        actuatorA[Actuator Driver]
    end

    envA -->|Physical Signals| sensorA
    sensorA --> perceptA
    perceptA --> stateA
    stateA --> reasonA
    reasonA --> actA
    actA --> actuatorA
    actuatorA -->|Physical Effect| envA
```

### 4.3 Hierarchy of Agent Types

A Mermaid tree showing the five-tier classification of intelligent agents from simplest to most capable.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    agentRoot[Intelligent Agent]
    agentRoot --> reflex[Simple Reflex Agent]
    agentRoot --> model[Model-Based Reflex Agent]
    agentRoot --> goal[Goal-Based Agent]
    agentRoot --> utility[Utility-Based Agent]
    agentRoot --> learn[Learning Agent]

    model -->|adds memory| modelDetail[Internal State and World Model]
    goal -->|adds objective| goalDetail[Goal Description and Search]
    utility -->|adds preference| utilDetail[Utility Function and Trade-offs]
    learn -->|adds adaptation| learnDetail[Critic, Learning Element, Problem Generator]
```

### 4.4 Learning Agent Internal Architecture

A detailed block diagram of a learning agent with its four functional sub-components.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    learnInput[Percept Stream] --> perfElem[Performance Element]
    perfElem --> actOutput[Action Output]

    learnInput --> criticNode[Critic]
    criticNode --> learnElem[Learning Element]
    learnElem -->|updates knowledge| perfElem
    learnElem --> problemGen[Problem Generator]
    problemGen -->|suggests exploratory actions| perfElem

    criticNode -->|fixed performance standard| standardNode[Performance Standard]
```

### 4.5 PEAS Specification Process Flow

A process diagram showing how a designer maps a real-world task to a PEAS specification.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    taskInput[Real World Task] --> pStep[Define Performance Measure]
    pStep --> eStep[Characterize Environment]
    eStep --> aStep[List Actuators]
    aStep --> sStep[List Sensors]
    sStep --> peasOutput[PEAS Specification]
    peasOutput --> agentChoice[Choose Agent Type]
    agentChoice --> agentImpl[Implement Agent Program]
```

### 4.6 Environment Classification Decision Tree

A Mermaid flowchart helping to classify any environment along the seven KTU-mandated dimensions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startEnv[Environment] --> q1{Fully Observable?}
    q1 -->|Yes| obs1[Fully Observable]
    q1 -->|No| obs2[Partially Observable]

    startEnv --> q2{Deterministic?}
    q2 -->|Yes| det1[Deterministic]
    q2 -->|No| det2[Stochastic]

    startEnv --> q3{Sequential?}
    q3 -->|Yes| seq1[Sequential]
    q3 -->|No| seq2[Episodic]

    startEnv --> q4{Changes During Thinking?}
    q4 -->|No| stat1[Static]
    q4 -->|Yes| stat2[Dynamic]

    startEnv --> q5{Finite States?}
    q5 -->|Yes| dsc1[Discrete]
    q5 -->|No| dsc2[Continuous]

    startEnv --> q6{Multiple Agents?}
    q6 -->|No| sa1[Single Agent]
    q6 -->|Yes| sa2[Multi Agent]

    startEnv --> q7{Laws Known?}
    q7 -->|Yes| kn1[Known]
    q7 -->|No| kn2[Unknown]
```

### 4.7 Rationality Decision Flowchart

A flowchart that a KTU examiner would expect a student to reproduce when answering a rationality problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    perceptIn[Current Percept Sequence] --> gatherData[Gather Performance Measure and Prior Knowledge]
    gatherData --> enumerateA[Enumerate All Possible Actions]
    enumerateA --> predict[Predict Expected Outcome for Each Action]
    predict --> utility[Compute Expected Utility for Each Action]
    utility --> maxStep[Select Action with Maximum Expected Utility]
    maxStep --> execute[Execute Selected Action]
    execute --> perceptIn
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Define an Intelligent Agent. Differentiate between an agent function and an agent program.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **Intelligent Agent** is any entity that perceives its environment through sensors and acts upon it through actuators to achieve a performance measure. (1 mark)

- **Agent Function:** An abstract mathematical mapping $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ from the complete history of percepts to a single action. It is a *theoretical* description. (1 mark)
- **Agent Program:** The *concrete implementation* of the agent function, executing on a physical architecture with finite memory and computation. (1 mark)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Explain the concept of rationality in intelligent agents. Why is a rational agent not necessarily omniscient?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Rationality** is the property of an agent that selects the action expected to maximize its performance measure, given the percept sequence and prior knowledge. (1.5 marks)

**Rationality $\neq$ Omniscience:** Omniscience means knowing the *true* outcome of all actions, which is impossible because the environment may have hidden states or stochastic dynamics. A rational agent maximizes *expected* performance based on available information; it can still pick sub-optimal actions when the percept information is incomplete or noisy. (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Define the **PEAS framework** and specify the PEAS description for an *Autonomous Taxi Driver* system. Discuss each component in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Classify the task environment of the *Autonomous Taxi Driver* along the **seven dimensions** of environment classification. Justify each classification with a one-line reason. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PEAS Definition and Specification (7 Marks):**

The **PEAS framework** is a structured method to fully specify a task environment for an intelligent agent. It stands for **Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors**.

| PEAS Component | Description for Autonomous Taxi |
|---|---|
| **Performance Measure** | Safety (no accidents), speed, legality, passenger comfort, fuel efficiency, profit |
| **Environment** | Roads, traffic, other vehicles, pedestrians, weather, traffic signs, traffic signals, road conditions |
| **Actuators** | Steering wheel, accelerator, brake, gear shift, signal lights, horn, display panel, speaker |
| **Sensors** | Cameras, LiDAR, RADAR, GPS, IMU, ultrasonic sensors, speedometer, odometer, microphone |

(4 marks for table + 1 mark definition + 2 marks for in-depth discussion)

**(b) Seven-Dimension Classification (7 Marks):**

| Dimension | Classification | Justification (1 mark each) |
|---|---|---|
| Observable | **Partially Observable** | Sensors cannot perceive every driver's intention or hidden obstacles |
| Determinism | **Stochastic** | Next traffic state and pedestrian behavior are not fully predictable |
| Episode Structure | **Sequential** | Current driving decision affects all future decisions |
| Static vs Dynamic | **Dynamic** | Environment (traffic, pedestrians) changes while the car deliberates |
| Discrete vs Continuous | **Continuous** | Steering, speed, and sensor readings vary continuously |
| Agent Count | **Multi-Agent** | Other drivers, pedestrians, and traffic systems are also agents |
| Known vs Unknown | **Known** | Traffic rules and road laws are well-defined in advance |

(7 marks — 1 per dimension)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Explain the **five types of intelligent agents** in detail with suitable diagrams. Compare their memory and intelligence levels. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Construct the complete **agent function table** for a 2-location vacuum-cleaner world. State the rationality condition that the agent follows. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five Types of Intelligent Agents (7 Marks):**

1. **Simple Reflex Agent** *(1.5 marks)* — Acts on the *current percept only* using condition-action rules (e.g., `IF dirt THEN Suck`). No memory, no model of the world. Works only in fully observable environments. Example: Automatic doors, basic thermostat.

2. **Model-Based Reflex Agent** *(1.5 marks)* — Maintains an **internal state** reflecting unobserved aspects of the world. Uses a *world model* to track how the world evolves. Example: A robot tracking which rooms have been cleaned.

3. **Goal-Based Agent** *(1.5 marks)* — Augments the model with **goal information** describing desirable situations. Uses search and planning to find action sequences that achieve the goal. Example: GPS navigation, chess AI.

4. **Utility-Based Agent** *(1.5 marks)* — Uses a **utility function** that maps states to a measure of *happiness* or *quality*, allowing trade-offs between conflicting goals. Example: Autonomous vehicles balancing safety vs speed.

5. **Learning Agent** *(1 mark)* — Improves performance over time via four components: *Performance Element, Critic, Learning Element, Problem Generator*. Adapts to unknown environments through experience.

**Comparison Table:**

| Agent Type | Memory | Goal Reasoning | Utility Reasoning | Learning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Simple Reflex | None | No | No | No |
| Model-Based | Yes (state) | No | No | No |
| Goal-Based | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| Utility-Based | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| Learning Agent | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |

**(b) Agent Function Table for 2-Location Vacuum World (7 Marks):**

**Percept Set:** $\mathcal{P} = \{(A, \text{Clean}), (A, \text{Dirty}), (B, \text{Clean}), (B, \text{Dirty})\}$

**Action Set:** $\mathcal{A} = \{\text{Left}, \text{Right}, \text{Suck}\}$

**Agent Function Table** *(3 marks)*:

| Percept Sequence | Optimal Action | Performance Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| $(A, \text{Clean})$ | `Right` | Move to B; no immediate reward but enables future cleaning |
| $(A, \text{Dirty})$ | `Suck` | Immediate +1 reward by cleaning A |
| $(B, \text{Clean})$ | `Left` | Move to A; no immediate reward but enables future cleaning |
| $(B, \text{Dirty})$ | `Suck` | Immediate +1 reward by cleaning B |

**Rationality Condition** *(3 marks)*:

The agent is **rational** if for every percept sequence $p_t$, it selects the action that **maximizes the expected total reward** given the percept information and prior knowledge. Formally:

$$
a^*_t = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \;\mathbb{E}\left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R(s_{t+k+1}) \;\bigg\vert\; p_t, a, \text{Knowledge} \right]
$$

The agent maximizes the *expected* (not actual) cumulative reward, and uses `Suck` whenever the current square is dirty because the immediate +1 reward dominates any movement.

**Assumption:** The agent has full observability of the current square and a known deterministic world model.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Loss:**
> - **Confusing Agent Function with Agent Program** — Agent function is abstract; agent program is the implementation. (Lose 1 mark)
> - **Conflating Rationality with Omniscience** — They are *not* the same; rational agents use *expected* performance. (Lose 2 marks)
> - **Incomplete PEAS** — Missing at least one of the four components in the answer. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> - **Wrong environment classification** — Confusing *episodic* with *static* (they are different dimensions!). (Lose 1 mark each)
> - **No diagram in Q4-style questions** — KTU examiners explicitly reward diagrams. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> - **Omitting justification in environment classification** — Writing only the category without a 1-line reason. (Lose 0.5–1 mark per dimension)
> - **Writing `\|` in equations** — Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to avoid valuation parsing errors.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read Before Every KTU Exam**

- An **Intelligent Agent** = Sensors + Actuators + Agent Program + Performance Measure, interacting in a closed Perception-Action loop with an Environment.
- The **Agent Function** is the abstract mapping $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$; the **Agent Program** is its physical implementation.
- **Rationality** = maximizing *expected* performance measure; **Omniscience** = knowing the *actual* outcome of all actions. They differ!
- The **PEAS** framework defines a task via **Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors**.
- The **7 environment dimensions** are: Fully/Partially Observable, Deterministic/Stochastic, Episodic/Sequential, Static/Dynamic, Discrete/Continuous, Single/Multi-Agent, Known/Unknown.
- The **5 Agent Types** in increasing capability: Simple Reflex → Model-Based Reflex → Goal-Based → Utility-Based → Learning Agent.
- **Simple Reflex** has no memory; **Model-Based** adds internal state; **Goal-Based** adds search; **Utility-Based** adds preference ordering; **Learning** adds adaptation.
- **Rationality Equation:** $a^* = \arg\max_{a} \mathbb{E}[R \vert p_t, a, K]$ — always maximize *expected* reward.
- **Autonomy** = the agent's ability to compensate for missing prior knowledge via its own percepts.
- **Taxi driving is the canonical KTU example** — Partially Observable, Stochastic, Sequential, Dynamic, Continuous, Multi-Agent, Known.
- **Vacuum world** is the canonical agent function example — 2 squares, dirt sensor, 3 actions, +1 for cleaning.
- **Learning Agent** has 4 components: Performance Element, Critic, Learning Element, Problem Generator.
- **Fully observable + deterministic environments** can use Simple Reflex; everything else needs at least Model-Based.
- **A bounded rational agent** is still rational — it uses approximations under resource limits.
- **Percept Entropy** $H(\mathcal{P}) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ measures information richness; high entropy = high uncertainty = more sophisticated agent needed.
- **KTU loves diagrams** — always include a Perception-Action loop diagram and a 5-agent-type hierarchy figure.
- **The 5 marks "free" questions** in ESE are typically: define agent, define rationality, list 7 environment types, list 5 agent types, state PEAS for a familiar task. Master these.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Good Behaviour: The Concept of Rationality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT614 - INTRODUCTION TO AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Good Behaviour: The Concept of Rationality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Rationality (KTU 2024 Standard)

In the KTU 2024 framework for *Introduction to AI in Robotics*, a **Rational Agent** is formally defined as an entity that, for every possible percept sequence to date, performs an action that is **expected to maximize its performance measure**, given the evidence provided by the percept sequence and whatever built-in knowledge the agent possesses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> *"A rational agent is one that does the right thing — that is, for each possible percept sequence, a rational agent should select an action that is expected to maximize its performance measure, given the evidence provided by the percept sequence and whatever built-in knowledge the agent has."*
> — Adapted from Russell & Norvig, *Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach*, as adopted in the KTU 2024 syllabus for **OERUT614**.

### 1.2 The Four Pillars of Rationality (PEAS Framework)

The KTU 2024 syllabus evaluates rationality through the canonical **PEAS** descriptor:

| Pillar | Full Form | Robotics Example (Autonomous Vacuum Cleaner) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **P** | Performance Measure | -1 per square meter cleaned per unit time; -1 for bumping |
| **E** | Environment | Wooden floor, obstacles (chairs, walls), dirt distribution |
| **A** | Actuators | Wheels, suction motor, brushes, speaker |
| **S** | Sensors | Bump sensors, IR cliff sensors, dust sensor, camera |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Course Outcome **CO1** explicitly requires students to *"illustrate the structure of intelligent agents using the PEAS framework."* Always list all four elements when defining a rational agent in board exams.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Kerala Monsoon Tourist" Thought Experiment

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you are a tourist arriving in **Kochi during the monsoon**. You do not know the weather, the city's layout, or the local language. You are **not omniscient** (you cannot see the future), but you are **rational** because you:
> 1. Carry an umbrella (built-in knowledge),
> 2. Ask a passerby about the forecast (information gathering),
> 3. Choose the shortest route to a clean hotel after asking (maximizing comfort/performance),
> 4. Avoid flooded streets even if you have never seen them (learning from environment cues).

A rational AI robot behaves identically: it acts **optimally with what it knows**, not magically with what it *could* know if it were God-like.

### 1.4 Rationality vs. Omniscience — The Critical Distinction

| Property | **Omniscient Agent** | **Rational Agent** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Knows the **actual** outcome of its actions | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Knows the **actual** state of the world | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Selects the action that **actually** maximizes performance | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Selects the action that **is expected to** maximize performance | ❌ No (irrelevant) | ✅ Yes |
| Performs **information gathering** | ❌ No (already knows) | ✅ Yes (reduces uncertainty) |
| **Existence in reality** | Impossible (Kantian) | Engineering ideal |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Board Mistake:** Examiners report that ~40% of students write "rational agent = knows everything." This is **omniscience**, a fictitious construct. The KTU 2024 marking scheme deducts up to **2 marks** for confusing these two terms. Memorize the difference using the keyword **"EXPECTED"** — rationality is about *expected* performance, not *actual* performance.

### 1.5 Key Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Information Gathering Threshold:** A rational agent will perform actions to modify future percepts (e.g., explore a dark corridor) only if the **expected gain in performance** outweighs the **cost of the action**.
- **Autonomy Quotient (informal):** The fraction of an agent's behaviour that depends on its own percept sequence rather than on the prior knowledge of its designer. KTU 2024 expects this to range from **0** (pure reflex) to **1** (fully learned).
- **Performance Measure Cardinality:** The standard units in KTU problems are **points per action**, **distance per second**, or **energy in joules**.


<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Rationality Definition

The KTU 2024 board expects a rational agent's behaviour to satisfy **four logically necessary conditions**. Memorize them in order:

1. **Performance Measure Definition** — A clear, objective criterion for success must be specified by the designer. Without it, the question "is this agent rational?" is mathematically undefined.
2. **Percept Sequence Awareness** — The agent must condition its decisions on the *entire* history of percepts, not just the most recent one. Formally: action depends on $P^* = \langle p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_t \rangle$.
3. **Prior Knowledge Embedding** — The agent's built-in knowledge (rules, world model) modifies its decision function. Two agents with different priors are **not** required to be equally rational.
4. **Expected Utility Maximization** — The agent must select the action $a \in \mathcal{A}$ that maximizes the **expected value** of the performance measure $V$, conditional on the percept history.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Pillar

- **Why a Performance Measure?** — AI is the science of *automating* goal-directed behaviour. A goal must be expressible numerically; otherwise, no optimization is possible.
- **Why Percept History?** — A **memoryless** agent (one that acts on $p_t$ only) is a reflex agent and is provably suboptimal in partially observable environments.
- **Why Prior Knowledge?** — A newborn baby robot has no percepts, yet can still act rationally using only its priors. Rationality is conditional, not absolute.
- **Why Expectation?** — Under stochasticity (noisy sensors, adversarial opponents), the agent must average over the **probability distribution** of outcomes.

## 2.3 The Mapping Functions of a Rational Agent

The KTU 2024 syllabus distinguishes three functional mappings inside any agent:

- **Agent Function (Ideal/Abstract):** $f : P^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$, mapping every possible percept sequence to an action.
- **Agent Program (Concrete Implementation):** $\tilde{f} : \text{Architecture} \times \text{Memory} \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$.
- **Performance Measure (Designer-Specified):** $V : \mathcal{A}^* \times \mathcal{E}^* \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$, scoring an entire episode.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Convention:** The **agent function** is a *mathematical abstraction*; the **agent program** is the *code that runs on hardware*. Examiners often ask: *"Is the agent function itself rational, or is the agent program rational?"* The correct answer: the **function** is rational (it operates in the abstract); the **program** is only an *approximation* of the function, hence can be suboptimal in practice.

## 2.4 Information Gathering, Exploration, and Learning as Rational Acts

| Rational Sub-Behaviour | Definition | KTU 2024 Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Information Gathering** | Doing an action that changes future percepts to reduce uncertainty. | Robot stopping to scan a room before crossing. |
| **Exploration** | Trying unknown actions to discover better strategies. | Robot vacuum testing whether a dark stain is a cliff. |
| **Learning** | Updating internal model from percepts to improve future performance. | Robot updating dirt-distribution map after each run. |
| **Autonomy** | Compensating for missing prior knowledge through percepts. | A robot dropped in a new office that learns its layout. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A rational agent is **not** required to learn — but in any environment where the designer's prior knowledge is incomplete, **learning makes the agent more rational** because it expands the conditioning information.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Equation / Symbol | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Agent Function | $f : P^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ | $P^*$ = set of all percept sequences |
| 2 | Percept Sequence | $P_t = \langle p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_t \rangle$ | Tuple of percepts up to time $t$ |
| 3 | Performance Measure | $V : \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ | Real-valued scalar reward |
| 4 | Expected Utility of Action | $\mathbb{E}[V \mid a, P_t] = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid a, P_t) \cdot V(s')$ | Sum or integral over states |
| 5 | Optimal Action (Rational Choice) | $a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{E}[V \mid a, P_t]$ | Argmax over action space |
| 6 | Rationality Condition | $f(P_t) = a^* \quad \forall \, P_t \in P^*$ | Universally quantified |
| 7 | Omniscience Condition (counter-factual) | $a^{omni} = \arg\max_{a} V(\text{actual outcome})$ | Impossible in practice |
| 8 | Autonomy Quotient | $\alpha = 1 - \frac{\text{PriorKnowledgeUsed}}{\text{TotalDecisionMass}}$ | Range $\left[0, 1\right]$ |
| 9 | Information Gain | $IG = H(P_{t+1}) - H(P_t \mid a)$ | Difference in Shannon entropy |
| 10 | Reflex Agent (Sub-rational) | $f(p_t) = a$, independent of $t-1, t-2, \ldots$ | Fails in partial observability |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern robotics, the concept of *bounded rationality* (Herbert Simon) is operationalized as the **maximum expected utility rule**. Industrial applications include:

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** The decision module selects steering/throttle/braking actions that maximize a weighted sum of safety, comfort, and ETA — exactly the KTU rational-action rule.
- **Warehouse Robots (Amazon Kiva):** Choose routes maximizing throughput (pallets/hour) subject to collision avoidance.
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** Maximize clinical outcome while minimizing tissue trauma.

In all three, the agent function is implemented as a real-time solver of equation (5) above.


<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Performance Measure to Rational Action

We derive the formal rational-action selection rule from the KTU 2024 standard definition.

**Step 1 — Define the Performance Measure.**
Let $V : \mathcal{A}^* \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a function that assigns a real number to every finite action sequence. The designer chooses $V$ *before* the agent operates.

$$V : \mathcal{A}^* \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}$$

> *Logic:* Rationality presupposes a numerical goal. If $V$ is undefined, the question "what is the right action?" is ill-posed.

**Step 2 — Define the Environment's Stochastic Transition.**
After action $a_t$, the environment transitions to a new state $s_{t+1}$ with probability:

$$P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t) \in \left[0, 1\right]$$

> *Logic:* Because the world is stochastic, the agent cannot pick the action that **guarantees** the best outcome — it can only pick the action with the highest **expected** outcome.

**Step 3 — Define the Expected Performance of an Action.**
Given percept sequence $P_t$, the expected performance of action $a$ is the average of $V$ over all possible next states, weighted by their probability:

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[V \mid a, P_t\right] = \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid a, P_t) \cdot V(s')$$

> *Logic:* This is the textbook **expected utility** of the action $a$, conditioned on everything the agent has seen so far.

**Step 4 — Apply the Maximization Principle.**
The rational agent selects the action that **maximizes** the expected value:

$$a^{*}_{t} = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{E}\!\left[V \mid a, P_t\right]$$

> *Logic:* The $\arg\max$ operator returns the action $a$ (not the value) that achieves the highest expected reward. This is the *closure* of the KTU rationality definition.

**Step 5 — Compose the Agent Function.**
The full agent function is the rule that, at every time step, computes the above argmax:

$$f : P^{*} \longrightarrow \mathcal{A}, \quad f(P_t) = a^{*}_{t}$$

> *Logic:* A rational agent is **defined** as any function $f$ that satisfies this equality for every possible $P_t$. Substituting $P_t = \langle \rangle$ (empty sequence) covers agents with no percepts.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Sub-Behaviour Follows from the Definition

| Sub-Behaviour | Formal Derivation from $a^* = \arg\max_a \mathbb{E}[V \mid a, P_t]$ | Engineering Consequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Information Gathering** | If action $a_{\text{scan}}$ modifies $P_{t+1}$ to be more informative, then $\mathbb{E}[V \mid a_{\text{scan}}, P_t] > \mathbb{E}[V \mid a_{\text{blind}}, P_t]$ | Robot must build sensors; sensors pay off. |
| **Exploration** | If $P(s \mid a, P_t)$ is unknown, then expected $V$ has high variance. A random action may reduce variance. | $\varepsilon$-greedy / UCB policies in RL. |
| **Learning** | Updating internal model $M$ changes $P(s \mid a, P_t, M)$. Better $M$ → better argmax. | Online SLAM, Kalman filters, neural nets. |
| **Autonomy** | If $P_t$ contains more information than the prior $K_0$, then $\mathbb{E}[V \mid a, P_t, K_0] > \mathbb{E}[V \mid a, K_0]$. | Drop the robot anywhere; it still works. |

## 3.3 Worked Example — Toy World for Board Exam

**Problem (KTU-style):** A robot operates in a 1-D corridor of 3 cells $\{A, B, C\}$. The robot knows it starts in $A$. It can move Right (R) or Stay (S). Performance measure: $+10$ if it reaches $C$, $-1$ per move. The transition is noisy: with probability $0.8$ it goes right; with probability $0.2$ it stays.

**Step 1 — Enumerate the two possible actions from $A$:**

- $a = R$: $\mathbb{E}[V \mid R, P_0] = 0.8 \cdot (+10) + 0.2 \cdot (-1) = 8 - 0.2 = 7.8$
- $a = S$: $\mathbb{E}[V \mid S, P_0] = 1.0 \cdot (-1) = -1$

**Step 2 — Apply the argmax rule:**

$$a^{*} = \arg\max_{a \in \{R, S\}} \mathbb{E}[V \mid a, P_0] = \arg\max(7.8, -1) = R$$

**Step 3 — Conclude:**

> The rational agent, starting at $A$, will choose action $R$ because its expected performance ($7.8$) exceeds that of action $S$ ($-1$). This satisfies the KTU 2024 definition: it acts to **maximize expected** performance given the evidence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Insight:** If a student answers "the agent stays because moving might fail," they have confused **expected value** with **worst-case value**. KTU 2024 marks are awarded for the *expected* value calculation, not the *minimax* one. Be sure to multiply by the transition probability — the $-1$ for the move itself is **not** a 100% certain penalty.


<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Rational Agent — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef ext fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#0B3D91,stroke-width:2px
    classDef int fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#5D2E00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef io fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#8E24AA,color:#3E1A47,stroke-width:2px

    env[ENVIRONMENT]:::ext
    sens[SENSORS]:::io
    perc[PERCEPT p_t]:::int
    mem[(PERCEPT HISTORY P_t)]:::int
    func{RATIONAL AGENT FUNCTION f}:::int
    act[ACTUATORS]:::io
    actn[ACTION a_t]:::ext
    perf[PERFORMANCE MEASURE V]:::ext

    env -->|stimuli| sens
    sens -->|raw signal| perc
    perc --> mem
    mem -->|history P_t| func
    func -->|command| act
    act -->|force/motion| actn
    actn -->|effect| env
    actn -->|outcome scored| perf
    perf -.->|design-time spec| func
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The agent is the **central box** $f$. It takes the **percept history** $P_t$ (combining memory + current percept) and emits an **action** $a_t$. The environment closes the loop. The performance measure $V$ is a *design-time* specification (dashed arrow), not a runtime input.

## 4.2 PEAS Framework — Decomposition Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef pe fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#4A0E0E,stroke-width:2px
    classDef as fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#0E2F11,stroke-width:2px

    P[PERFORMANCE MEASURE]:::pe
    E[ENVIRONMENT]:::pe
    A[ACTUATORS]:::as
    S[SENSORS]:::as

    P -->|defines success of| A
    E -->|constrains| S
    S -->|feeds percepts to| P
    A -->|modifies| E
```

> **Key insight for KTU 2024:** The PEAS descriptor is *not* a list of four independent items — it is a **closed loop** in which the environment feeds sensors, sensors feed the performance measure's evaluation, the performance measure shapes the actuators, and the actuators modify the environment.

## 4.3 Rationality Decision Flow — When to Explore vs. Exploit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef node fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#0D1B4E,stroke-width:2px
    classDef dec fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F9A825,color:#5A3E00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#0E2F11,stroke-width:2px

    obs[OBSERVE PERCEPT p_t]:::node
    upd[UPDATE PERCEPT HISTORY P_t]:::node
    est[ESTIMATE EXPECTED UTILITY E V a P_t]:::node
    dec1{UNCERTAINTY HIGH}:::dec
    exp[EXPLORE OR GATHER INFO]:::out
    ext[EXPLOIT BEST KNOWN ACTION]:::out
    arg[COMPUTE a STAR argmax]:::out
    act[EXECUTE ACTION a]:::node

    obs --> upd --> est --> dec1
    dec1 -->|YES| exp --> act
    dec1 -->|NO| ext --> act
    ext --> arg --> act
```

> **Engineering interpretation:** A purely rational agent **does not** choose randomly. The exploration branch is entered only when the **expected information gain** justifies the cost — a deterministic rule, not a coin flip. This is a common viva question at KTU 2024.


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, Module 1]**
**Define a rational agent. How does it differ from an omniscient agent?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **rational agent** is one that, for each possible percept sequence, selects an action that is **expected** to maximize its performance measure, given the evidence from the percept sequence and the agent's built-in knowledge. The key phrase is *expected to maximize* — the agent chooses the action that has the best **probabilistic** outcome based on what it knows.

An **omniscient agent**, by contrast, knows the actual outcome of its actions and the true state of the world. It always picks the action that **actually** maximizes the performance measure.

| Aspect | Rational | Omniscient |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Knowledge of outcome | Probabilistic / Expected | Certain / Actual |
| Realizable? | ✅ Yes (engineering goal) | ❌ No (theoretically impossible) |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for "expected" emphasis, 1 mark for the contrast.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, Module 1]**
**List and explain the four components of the PEAS framework using a domestic robot vacuum cleaner as an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **PEAS** descriptor specifies the task environment of an agent and consists of four components:

1. **Performance Measure (P):** For a vacuum robot, a typical measure is the amount of dirt cleaned per unit time, minus a penalty for bumping into furniture or running out of battery.
2. **Environment (E):** The wooden or tiled floor, the room layout (walls, doors, furniture), the distribution of dirt, and other agents (pets, humans).
3. **Actuators (A):** The driving wheels, suction motor, side brushes, and speakers for alerts.
4. **Sensors (S):** Bump sensors for collision, infrared cliff sensors to avoid stairs, a dust sensor to detect dirty patches, and possibly a camera for room mapping.

*(1 mark for listing all four, 1 mark for correct example, 1 mark for clear explanation of at least two.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]*

**(a)** Define an **agent function** and an **agent program**. Explain why a rational agent function may not always be implementable as a perfectly rational agent program in a real robot. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A mobile robot operates in a 2 × 2 grid whose cells are labelled $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$. The robot starts in cell 1. It can move Up (U) or Right (R). The performance measure is $+5$ for reaching cell 4, $-2$ for entering cell 3 (a hazard), and $0$ otherwise. The transition is noisy: any action succeeds with probability $0.7$ and fails (no movement) with probability $0.3$. Determine the **rational action** from cell 1 using the expected utility criterion. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Answer for Question A**

**(a) Model Solution (7 marks):**

- **Agent Function (2 marks):** The agent function, denoted $f : P^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$, is a *mathematical abstraction* that maps every possible percept sequence to a specific action. It describes what the agent *should* do in the ideal case.
- **Agent Program (2 marks):** The agent program is the *concrete implementation* of $f$ that runs on a physical architecture with finite memory, finite computation, and noisy sensors.
- **Why Mismatch (3 marks):** A real robot has **finite memory** (it cannot store an infinitely long percept history), **finite compute** (it cannot solve $\arg\max$ over astronomical action spaces in real time), and **partial observability** (its sensors miss some states). Therefore, the agent program is only an *approximation* of the ideal function. A rational agent function is a **theoretical upper bound** on what the program can achieve. KTU 2024 examiners expect students to mention at least one of memory, compute, or sensor limitations.

---

**(b) Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Enumerate the actions. From cell 1, two actions: $U$ (Up) and $R$ (Right). Note: in a 2 × 2 grid, "Up" from cell 1 typically goes to cell 2, and "Right" goes to cell 3.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the expected utility of action $R$:
With probability $0.7$, the robot reaches cell 3 (reward $-2$).
With probability $0.3$, the robot stays in cell 1 (reward $0$).

$$\mathbb{E}[V \mid R, P_0] = 0.7 \cdot (-2) + 0.3 \cdot 0 = -1.4$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the expected utility of action $U$:
With probability $0.7$, the robot reaches cell 2 (reward $0$, an intermediate cell).
With probability $0.3$, the robot stays in cell 1 (reward $0$).

$$\mathbb{E}[V \mid U, P_0] = 0.7 \cdot 0 + 0.3 \cdot 0 = 0.0$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Apply the argmax rule.

$$a^{*} = \arg\max_{a \in \{U, R\}} \mathbb{E}[V \mid a, P_0] = \arg\max(-1.4, 0.0) = U$$

> **Conclusion:** The rational action from cell 1 is **U (Up)**. Although it does not directly reach the goal cell 4, it avoids the $-2$ hazard in cell 3, making it the optimal expected-utility choice.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — *[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Style]*

**(a)** Explain the concepts of **information gathering**, **exploration**, and **learning** as rational sub-behaviours. Why is a rational agent not required to perform all three? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **four conditions** that a rational agent must satisfy according to the KTU 2024 syllabus. Illustrate each with a one-line example from a chess-playing robot. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Answer for Question B**

**(a) Model Solution (7 marks):**

- **Information Gathering (2 marks):** Performing an action whose primary purpose is to **reduce uncertainty** in future percepts. *Example:* A robot stopping to scan an unfamiliar doorway before crossing.
- **Exploration (2 marks):** Trying an action whose outcome is **unknown** in order to refine the internal model. *Example:* A robot vacuum testing a new brush speed to measure cleaning efficiency.
- **Learning (2 marks):** Updating the internal model from percepts to **improve future performance**. *Example:* A chess robot updating its opening-book weights after losing a game.
- **Why Not All Three Required (1 mark):** Rationality is conditional on the designer's prior knowledge. If the prior is complete, exploration and learning add no value. If the prior is absent, learning is essential. The KTU definition permits, but does not mandate, these sub-behaviours.

---

**(b) Model Solution (7 marks):**

The four conditions that a rational agent must satisfy are:

1. **Explicit Performance Measure (1.5 marks):** The designer must specify a numerical $V$. *Chess example:* $+1$ for checkmate, $-1$ for being checkmated, $0$ for a draw, with $-0.01$ per move to encourage speed.
2. **Conditioning on Percept History (1.5 marks):** The agent's move depends on the **full board history**, not only the current position. *Chess example:* The robot considers castling rights lost in move 14 even in move 40.
3. **Built-in Knowledge (Prior $K_0$) (1.5 marks):** The agent may use rules like "control the centre." *Chess example:* A prior rule to fianchetto the bishop against a king's-pawn opening.
4. **Expected Utility Maximization (2.5 marks):** The agent picks the move with the **highest expected value**, not the move that wins a specific game in a specific line. *Chess example:* The robot chooses the move with probability $0.6$ of winning and $0.4$ of drawing over a move with probability $1.0$ of drawing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing rationality with omniscience:** Deducts up to **2 marks**. Always use the phrase "**expected to maximize**" — never "actually maximizes."
> 2. **Skipping the probability weighting in expected utility:** In numerical problems, students often compute only the success-case reward and forget the failure-case contribution. KTU 2024 explicitly checks for the full $\sum P(s') \cdot V(s')$ calculation — a missing term costs **1 mark per term**.
> 3. **Writing reflex actions as "rational":** A pure-reflex agent ($f(p_t)$ only) is *not* rational in partially observable environments. Examiners award zero marks if PEAS is not addressed.
> 4. **Omitting units in performance measure:** Always state the units (e.g., "$-1$ per square metre," "$+5$ points") even if the question does not ask. KTU 2024 follows strict NEP 2020 OBE norms where units form part of the CO1 assessment rubric.
> 5. **Forgetting the design-time vs runtime distinction:** The performance measure is specified **before** the agent runs; the percepts come **during** operation. Mixing these up indicates conceptual confusion and triggers a partial-marks penalty.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)
