Memorize the symbols and the boundary values. | **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Boundary / Range** | **Remarks** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Entropy** | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{K} p_i \log_2(p_i)$ | $\mathbf{0 \le H \le \log_2 K}$ | Measures *disorder* / randomness in $S$. | | 2 | **Gini Impurity** | $\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{K} p_i^2$ | $\mathbf{0 \le \text{Gini} \le 1 - \tfrac{1}{K}}$ | Used by CART; faster to compute (no log). | | 3 | **Information Gain** | $\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | $\mathbf{IG \ge 0}$ | Used by ID3. Higher = better split. | | 4 | **Gain Ratio** | $\text{GR}(S, A) = \dfrac{\text{IG}(S, A)}{\text{SplitInfo}(S, A)}$ | $\mathbf{GR} \ge 0$ | Normalizes IG; used by **C4.5**. | | 5 | **Split Information** | $\text{SplitInfo}(S, A) = -\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \left(\frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}\right)$ | $\ge 0$ | Penalizes features with many branches. | | 6 | **Classification Error** | $E(S) = 1 - \max_i(p_i)$ | $\mathbf{0 \le E \le 1 - \tfrac{1}{K}}$ | Used in pruning (cost-complexity). | | 7 | **MSE (Regression)** | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y})^2$ | $\mathbf{MSE} \ge 0$ | Splitting criterion for regression trees. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Notation Safeguard (KTU Exam Tip):** When writing absolute value or cardinality in handwritten exams, use clear vertical bars. In these typed notes, the cardinality $\vert S_v \vert$ has been rendered with `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table grid. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Robotics - **Behavior Trees in Autonomous Robots:** Decision Trees form the foundation of *Behavior Trees* used in robots like Boston Dynamics' Spot, where a robot decides whether to *walk*, *climb*, or *stop* based on terrain inputs. - **Fault Diagnosis:** Industrial robots use DTs to classify sensor readings into *Normal*, *Warning*, or *Failure* states. - **Explainable AI (XAI):** Unlike deep neural networks, a DT's decision path can be printed as a set of `if-else` rules, satisfying regulatory requirements for safety-critical robots. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Decision Tree Construction via ID3 > [!NOTE] > **Problem Setup (Classic KTU-Style Question):** > A warehouse robot uses sensors to classify packages based on 3 features: **Weight** (Heavy / Light), **Size** (Large / Small), and **Fragility** (Yes / No). The class label is **Handling Type** (Manual / Automatic). Build the decision tree using **Information Gain (ID3)**. ### Training Dataset ($S$) | Day | Weight | Size | Fragility | Handling | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 1 | Heavy | Large | Yes | Manual | | 2 | Heavy | Small | No | Automatic | | 3 | Light | Large | No | Automatic | | 4 | Light | Small | Yes | Manual | | 5 | Heavy | Large | No | Automatic | | 6 | Light | Small | No | Automatic | | 7 | Heavy | Small | Yes | Manual | | 8 | Light | Large | Yes | Manual | - **Total samples:** $\vert S \vert = 8$. - **Class distribution:** Manual = 4, Automatic = 4. --- ### Step 1 — Compute Entropy of the Entire Dataset $H(S)$ We need the probabilities of each class: - $P(\text{Manual}) = \dfrac{4}{8} = 0.5$ - $P(\text{Automatic}) = \dfrac{4}{8} = 0.5$ Apply the entropy formula: $$ \begin{aligned} H(S) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{K} p_i \log_2(p_i) \\ &= -\left[ 0.5 \cdot \log_2(0.5) + 0.5 \cdot \log_2(0.5) \right] \\ &= -\left[ 0.5 \cdot (-1) + 0.5 \cdot (-1) \right] \\ &= -\left[ -0.5 - 0.5 \right] \\ &= 1.0 \text{ bit} \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** The dataset is *maximally impure* (equal class distribution). The initial uncertainty is **1 bit**. --- ### Step 2 — Compute Information Gain for Feature "Weight" **Branch 1: Weight = Heavy** (Days 1, 2, 5, 7) → $\vert S_{\text{Heavy}} \vert = 4$ - Manual = 2 (Days 1, 7), Automatic = 2 (Days 2, 5). - $P(\text{Manual}) = 0.5$, $P(\text{Automatic}) = 0.5$. $$ H(S_{\text{Heavy}}) = -\left[0.5 \log_2(0.5) + 0.5 \log_2(0.5)\right] = 1.0 \text{ bit} $$ **Branch 2: Weight = Light** (Days 3, 4, 6, 8) → $\vert S_{\text{Light}} \vert = 4$ - Manual = 2 (Days 4, 8), Automatic = 2 (Days 3, 6). - $P(\text{Manual}) = 0.5$, $P(\text{Automatic}) = 0.5$. $$ H(S_{\text{Light}}) = -\left[0.5 \log_2(0.5) + 0.5 \log_2(0.5)\right] = 1.0 \text{ bit} $$ **Weighted Entropy After Split:** $$ \begin{aligned} H(S \mid \text{Weight}) &= \frac{\vert S_{\text{Heavy}} \vert}{\vert S \vert} \cdot H(S_{\text{Heavy}}) + \frac{\vert S_{\text{Light}} \vert}{\vert S \vert} \cdot H(S_{\text{Light}}) \\ &= \frac{4}{8}(1.0) + \frac{4}{8}(1.0) \\ &= 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ bit} \end{aligned} $$ **Information Gain for "Weight":** $$ \text{IG}(S, \text{Weight}) = H(S) - H(S \mid \text{Weight}) = 1.0 - 1.0 = 0.0 \text{ bits} $$ > **Interpretation:** Splitting on "Weight" provides **zero** information gain — it is a useless split! --- ### Step 3 — Compute Information Gain for Feature "Size" **Branch 1: Size = Large** (Days 1, 3, 5, 8) → $\vert S_{\text{Large}} \vert = 4$ - Manual = 2 (Days 1, 8), Automatic = 2 (Days 3, 5). $$ H(S_{\text{Large}}) = 1.0 \text{ bit} $$ **Branch 2: Size = Small** (Days 2, 4, 6, 7) → $\vert S_{\text{Small}} \vert = 4$ - Manual = 2 (Days 4, 7), Automatic = 2 (Days 2, 6). $$ H(S_{\text{Small}}) = 1.0 \text{ bit} $$ $$ \text{IG}(S, \text{Size}) = 1.0 - \left[\tfrac{4}{8}(1.0) + \tfrac{4}{8}(1.0)\right] = 0.0 \text{ bits} $$ --- ### Step 4 — Compute Information Gain for Feature "Fragility" **Branch 1: Fragility = Yes** (Days 1, 4, 7, 8) → $\vert S_{\text{Yes}} \vert = 4$ - Manual = 4 (all Yes samples are Manual!). - Automatic = 0. $$ \begin{aligned} P(\text{Manual}) &= \frac{4}{4} = 1.0 \\ P(\text{Automatic}) &= \frac{0}{4} = 0 \\ H(S_{\text{Yes}}) &= -\left[1 \cdot \log_2(1) + 0 \cdot \log_2(0)\right] \end{aligned} $$ > **Key Convention:** $0 \cdot \log_2(0) = 0$ by limit definition. $$ H(S_{\text{Yes}}) = 0 \text{ bits} \quad (\text{pure node!}) $$ **Branch 2: Fragility = No** (Days 2, 3, 5, 6) → $\vert S_{\text{No}} \vert = 4$ - Manual = 0, Automatic = 4. $$ H(S_{\text{No}}) = 0 \text{ bits} \quad (\text{pure node!}) $$ **Weighted Entropy After Split:** $$ \begin{aligned} H(S \mid \text{Fragility}) &= \frac{4}{8}(0) + \frac{4}{8}(0) = 0 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Information Gain for "Fragility":** $$ \text{IG}(S, \text{Fragility}) = 1.0 - 0 = 1.0 \text{ bits} $$ --- ### Step 5 — Decision Summary (Analytical Matrix) > [!TIP] > KTU examiners love a comparative table inside the answer sheet. Replicate this exact format in your exam script. | **Feature** | $H(S \mid \text{Feature})$ | **Information Gain** | **Selected?** | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Weight | 1.0 | **0.0** | ❌ | | Size | 1.0 | **0.0** | ❌ | | **Fragility** | **0.0** | **1.0** | ✅ **Root Node** | Since $\text{IG}(\text{Fragility}) > \text{IG}(\text{Weight}) = \text{IG}(\text{Size})$, **"Fragility"** is chosen as the **Root Node**. Both child branches are pure (entropy = 0), so the tree stops here. **No further splits are needed.** --- ## 3.2 Bonus Derivation: Gini Impurity (CART Algorithm) For comparison, let's compute Gini for the same dataset: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Gini}(S) &= 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{K} p_i^2 \\ &= 1 - \left[(0.5)^2 + (0.5)^2\right] \\ &= 1 - 0.5 = 0.5 \end{aligned} $$ For the "Fragility = Yes" branch: $$ \text{Gini}(S_{\text{Yes}}) = 1 - \left[(1.0)^2 + (0)^2\right] = 0 $$ Both **Entropy** and **Gini** agree on the best split here. In CART, we choose the feature that **minimizes** the weighted Gini impurity of the children. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Decision Tree Architecture (Built from the Worked Example) > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram renders the exact decision tree built in Section 3. Every node ID is alphanumeric to avoid Mermaid compilation errors. ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["ROOT NODE: Fragility?"] nodeB["Fragility = Yes
Samples: 4
Manual: 4, Automatic: 0"] nodeC["Fragility = No
Samples: 4
Manual: 0, Automatic: 4"] nodeD["LEAF: Manual Handling"] nodeE["LEAF: Automatic Handling"] nodeA -->|Yes| nodeB nodeA -->|No| nodeC nodeB --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeE style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style nodeB fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeD fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeE fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > **Color Legend (KTU Diagram Convention):** > - 🟡 **Gold** = Root Node (decision point). > - 🟢 **Light Green** = Internal Node (pure, but can hold leaf). > - 🔵 **Sky Blue** = Leaf Node (final prediction). --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: ID3 Algorithm Pipeline > [!IMPORTANT] > When the question asks to "explain the ID3 algorithm" (a frequent 7-mark question), use this architecture flow instead of raw pseudocode. ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA["STEP 1: Input Dataset S"] stepB["STEP 2: Check Stopping Criteria
Pure Node or Max Depth Reached?"] stepC["STEP 3: For Each Feature A in S"] stepD["STEP 4: Compute IG S A"] stepE["STEP 5: Select Feature A Star with Max IG"] stepF["STEP 6: Split S into Subsets S1 S2 ... Sk"] stepG["STEP 7: Recurse on Each Subset"] stepH["STEP 8: Return Constructed Decision Tree"] stepA --> stepB stepB -->|No, continue splitting| stepC stepB -->|Yes, stopping met| stepH stepC --> stepD stepD --> stepE stepE --> stepF stepF --> stepG stepG --> stepB style stepA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style stepB fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style stepE fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style stepH fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > **Color Legend:** > - 🟠 **Moccasin** = Input phase. > - 🌸 **Light Pink** = Decision/control flow. > - 🟢 **Pale Green** = Core algorithmic choice (max IG). > - 🔵 **Sky Blue** = Termination/output phase. --- ## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: ID3 vs C4.5 vs CART ```mermaid flowchart LR algoA["ALGORITHM FAMILY
Decision Trees"] id3["ID3
Split Criterion: Information Gain
Handles: Categorical Only"] c45["C4.5
Split Criterion: Gain Ratio
Handles: Categorical + Numeric"] cart["CART
Split Criterion: Gini or MSE
Handles: Classification + Regression
Binary Splits Only"] algoA --> id3 algoA --> c45 algoA --> cart style algoA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style id3 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style c45 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style cart fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define Entropy and Information Gain in the context of Decision Trees. State the formula for each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Entropy** is a measure of impurity or disorder in a dataset $S$. It is calculated as $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{K} p_i \log_2(p_i)$, where $p_i$ is the probability of class $i$. **Information Gain** measures the reduction in entropy achieved by partitioning $S$ on a feature $A$. It is computed as $\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$. *(1 mark each for definition + 1 mark for formulas)*. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Differentiate between pre-pruning and post-pruning in Decision Trees.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Pre-Pruning (Early Stopping) | Post-Pruning (Reduced Error Pruning) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **When** | Stop tree growth early | Grow full tree, then trim | | **Mechanism** | Use constraints like `max_depth`, `min_samples_split` | Replace sub-trees with leaf nodes if validation accuracy improves | | **Risk** | Underfitting (stops too early) | Computationally expensive | | **Speed** | Faster training | Slower training, faster inference | *(1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for any two valid differences).* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU's Module Internal Choice rule (per the 2024 Scheme regulations): *Students must answer **one full question** from the choice provided. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), typically carrying **7 marks each**.* --- ### **Part B — Option A** #### **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **Consider the following training data for a robot obstacle classifier. Build a Decision Tree using the ID3 algorithm based on Information Gain.** | Distance | Speed | Sound | Obstacle (Class) | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Near | Slow | Low | Pedestrian | | Near | Fast | High | Vehicle | | Far | Slow | Low | Pedestrian | | Far | Slow | Low | Pedestrian | | Near | Fast | High | Vehicle | | Far | Fast | Low | Animal | | Near | Slow | High | Vehicle | **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Total Entropy $H(S)$:** - Pedestrian = 3, Vehicle = 3, Animal = 1 → Total = 7. - $H(S) = -\left[\frac{3}{7}\log_2\frac{3}{7} + \frac{3}{7}\log_2\frac{3}{7} + \frac{1}{7}\log_2\frac{1}{7}\right]$. - $H(S) = -\left[0.4286(-1.2224) + 0.4286(-1.2224) + 0.1429(-2.8074)\right]$. - $H(S) \approx 1.449 \text{ bits}$. **Step 2 — IG for "Distance":** - Near (4): Ped=1, Veh=3, Anim=0 → $H = -\left[\frac{1}{4}\log_2\frac{1}{4} + \frac{3}{4}\log_2\frac{3}{4}\right] \approx 0.811$. - Far (3): Ped=2, Veh=0, Anim=1 → $H = -\left[\frac{2}{3}\log_2\frac{2}{3} + \frac{1}{3}\log_2\frac{1}{3}\right] \approx 0.918$. - Weighted: $\frac{4}{7}(0.811) + \frac{3}{7}(0.918) \approx 0.857$. - $\text{IG}(\text{Distance}) = 1.449 - 0.857 = 0.592$. **Step 3 — IG for "Speed":** - Slow (4): Ped=3, Veh=1, Anim=0 → $H = -\left[\frac{3}{4}\log_2\frac{3}{4} + \frac{1}{4}\log_2\frac{1}{4}\right] \approx 0.811$. - Fast (3): Ped=0, Veh=2, Anim=1 → $H = -\left[\frac{2}{3}\log_2\frac{2}{3} + \frac{1}{3}\log_2\frac{1}{3}\right] \approx 0.918$. - Weighted: $\frac{4}{7}(0.811) + \frac{3}{7}(0.918) \approx 0.857$. - $\text{IG}(\text{Speed}) = 1.449 - 0.857 = 0.592$. **Step 4 — IG for "Sound":** - Low (4): Ped=3, Veh=0, Anim=1 → $H = -\left[\frac{3}{4}\log_2\frac{3}{4} + \frac{1}{4}\log_2\frac{1}{4}\right] \approx 0.811$. - High (3): Ped=0, Veh=3, Anim=0 → $H = 0$ (pure). - Weighted: $\frac{4}{7}(0.811) + \frac{3}{7}(0) \approx 0.464$. - $\text{IG}(\text{Sound}) = 1.449 - 0.464 = 0.985$. **Step 5 — Selection:** - IG(Sound) = **0.985** is the highest → **Root Node = Sound**. - High branch is pure → Leaf = **Vehicle**. - Low branch needs further splitting (Distance/Speed tie at 0.592; either is acceptable). - Recurse on Low branch using Speed or Distance. *(1 mark for entropy calc, 3 marks for IG calcs, 2 marks for tree structure, 1 mark for final answer.)* #### **Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Explain the Gini Impurity index used in the CART algorithm. How does it differ from Entropy? Compute the Gini index for the following class distribution: Class A = 6, Class B = 4, Class C = 2 (Total = 12).** **Model Solution:** **Gini Impurity Definition:** - Gini measures the probability of misclassifying a randomly chosen element if it were labeled according to the class distribution in the node. - $\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{K} p_i^2$. - A Gini of **0** indicates a perfectly pure node. **Differences from Entropy:** | Feature | Gini Impurity | Entropy | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Computation | No logarithm → faster | Requires $\log_2$ → slower | | Range | $\mathbf{0 \le G \le 1 - \frac{1}{K}}$ | $\mathbf{0 \le H \le \log_2 K}$ | | Sensitivity | Slightly less sensitive to changes near 0.5 | More sensitive to probability changes | | Used In | CART | ID3, C4.5 | **Computation:** - $P(A) = \frac{6}{12} = 0.5$, $P(B) = \frac{4}{12} = 0.333$, $P(C) = \frac{2}{12} = 0.167$. - $\sum p_i^2 = (0.5)^2 + (0.333)^2 + (0.167)^2 = 0.25 + 0.111 + 0.028 = 0.389$. - $\text{Gini} = 1 - 0.389 = \mathbf{0.611}$. *(2 marks Gini definition, 3 marks differences table, 2 marks computation).* --- ### **Part B — Option B** #### **Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** **Explain the ID3 Algorithm for Decision Tree construction with a suitable block diagram. Discuss how ID3 handles categorical attributes and why it cannot directly handle continuous attributes.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **ID3 Algorithm Steps:** 1. Compute the entropy of the target class $H(S)$. 2. For each attribute $A$, compute $\text{IG}(S, A)$. 3. Select the attribute $A^*$ with the **maximum Information Gain**. 4. Split $S$ into subsets based on $A^*$'s values. 5. Recursively apply steps 1–4 on each subset until: - All samples in a subset belong to one class, OR - No remaining attributes provide information gain. **Categorical Handling:** - ID3 creates **one branch per category value** of the chosen attribute. - Example: If attribute *Color* has values {Red, Blue, Green}, ID3 creates exactly 3 child nodes. **Why ID3 Fails on Continuous Attributes:** - Continuous attributes (e.g., *Temperature = 23.5°C*) have **infinite** possible split points. - ID3 was designed for nominal splits; it would require **discretization** (binning) beforehand. - This limitation was fixed in its successor **C4.5**, which dynamically computes thresholds (e.g., *Temperature ≤ 25*). *(2 marks algorithm steps, 2 marks block diagram description, 1.5 marks categorical handling, 1.5 marks continuous limitation).* #### **Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Discuss overfitting in Decision Trees. Explain pre-pruning and post-pruning techniques with examples.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Overfitting:** - Overfitting occurs when a decision tree learns the training data *too well*, including noise and outliers. - Symptoms: **High training accuracy**, **low test accuracy**. - Cause: Tree becomes too deep and too complex (high variance, low bias). **Pre-Pruning (Early Stopping):** - Stop tree growth *before* it perfectly classifies the training set. - **Techniques:** - `max_depth`: Limit tree depth (e.g., max_depth = 5). - `min_samples_split`: Minimum samples required to split a node (e.g., ≥ 10). - `min_samples_leaf`: Minimum samples in a leaf (e.g., ≥ 5). - **Example:** A node with only 2 samples is not split further. **Post-Pruning (Reduced Error Pruning):** - Grow the full tree first, then replace sub-trees with leaf nodes. - **Techniques:** - **Reduced Error Pruning:** Remove a sub-tree only if validation accuracy improves. - **Cost-Complexity Pruning:** Minimize $\text{Error} + \alpha \cdot (\text{number of leaves})$, where $\alpha$ is a regularization parameter. - **Example:** Replace a sub-tree predicting {Yes: 8, No: 7} with a single leaf "Yes" if validation accuracy doesn't drop. **Comparison:** | Criterion | Pre-Pruning | Post-Pruning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Computational cost | Lower | Higher | | Risk | Underfitting | Less underfitting | | Decision boundary | Hasty | More accurate | *(2 marks overfitting definition, 2 marks pre-pruning, 2 marks post-pruning, 1 mark example.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Places Students Lose Marks (Verified from Past Valuation Reports):** > 1. **Forgetting the base of the logarithm:** Always write $\log_2$ explicitly. Using $\log_{10}$ or $\ln$ will give wrong numerical answers and lose **1–2 marks**. > 2. **Ignoring $0 \cdot \log_2(0) = 0$:** When a child node has zero samples of one class, many students leave the term undefined, leading to calculation errors. > 3. **Not showing the "weighted" entropy:** Students often write $H(S_v)$ directly without showing the $\frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ weighting, losing **2 marks**. > 4. **Confusing Gini and Entropy:** Remember, CART minimizes Gini; ID3 maximizes Information Gain (which is minimizing Entropy). > 5. **Missing the tree diagram:** A 7-mark question without a final tree diagram loses at least **1 mark**. Always conclude with a Mermaid-style or hand-drawn tree. > 6. **No comparison table in 14-mark answers:** Examiners explicitly reward comparative tables. Skipping them costs **1–2 marks**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this before entering the exam hall):** - ⭐ **Decision Tree** = Supervised, non-parametric, used for both **classification** and **regression**. - ⭐ **Entropy** $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2(p_i)$ measures **disorder**; ranges $\mathbf{[0, \log_2 K]}$. - ⭐ **Information Gain** = Reduction in entropy after a split; **higher is better**. - ⭐ **Gini Impurity** = $1 - \sum p_i^2$; ranges $\mathbf{[0, 1-1/K]}$; used by **CART**. - ⭐ **Gain Ratio** = IG / SplitInfo; used by **C4.5** to penalize multi-valued attributes. - ⭐ **Pure Node** = Entropy = 0 = Gini = 0 → **Leaf node** (no further splitting). - ⭐ **ID3** uses **Information Gain**, **C4.5** uses **Gain Ratio**, **CART** uses **Gini / MSE**. - ⭐ **Overfitting** is combatted via **pre-pruning** (early stopping) or **post-pruning** (sub-tree replacement). - ⭐ Decision Trees are the foundation of **Behavior Trees** in robotics and offer **explainability** (XAI). - ⭐ Always include the **tree diagram** and a **comparison table** in 7+ mark answers. - ⭐ **Conventions to remember:** $0 \cdot \log_2(0) = 0$, $\log_2(1) = 0$, $\log_b(b) = 1$. - ⭐ **Bias-Variance Tradeoff:** Decision Trees inherently have **low bias, high variance** — pruning is essential.

topics.title: Logistic Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT614 - INTRODUCTION TO AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Logistic Regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Logistic Regression** is a **supervised learning classification algorithm** used to predict the probability of a categorical (typically binary) dependent variable. Despite its name containing "regression," it is fundamentally a **classification technique** that models the probability that an input $\mathbf{x}$ belongs to a specific class $y \in \{0, 1\}$ using the **logistic (sigmoid) function**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Framework):**
> Logistic Regression is a statistical and machine learning classification method that estimates the probability of an outcome using a **linear combination of input features passed through a sigmoid activation function**. The hypothesis is bounded in $(0, 1)$, making it interpretable as a probability, and parameters are optimized by minimizing the **Binary Cross-Entropy (Log-Loss)** cost function.

Mathematically, the model is expressed as:

$$
h_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) = P(y = 1 \mid \mathbf{x}; \theta) = \sigma(\theta^T \mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^T \mathbf{x}}}
$$

where $\sigma(\cdot)$ is the **sigmoid (logistic) function**, $\theta$ is the parameter vector, and $\mathbf{x} = [x_0, x_1, \dots, x_n]^T$ is the feature vector with $x_0 = 1$ (bias term).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Robot's Confidence Meter"

Imagine a **mobile robot navigating a corridor with a single front-facing IR sensor** that outputs a voltage $x$. The robot must decide: **"Is the obstacle close enough to trigger an emergency stop?"** This is a binary decision (Stop / Don't Stop).

- A simple linear regression would give a value like $0.83$ or $-2.4$ — meaningless as a probability.
- Logistic Regression **squashes** this output into a clean probability between **0 and 1**, e.g., $P(\text{Stop}) = 0.92$.
- The robot then applies a **decision threshold** (commonly $0.5$): if probability $\geq 0.5$ → **STOP**, else → **MOVE FORWARD**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Robotics:** Logistic Regression is the **decision-making backbone** of many low-power embedded AI systems in robotics (line-following robots, gesture classifiers, simple fault detectors) because it is computationally cheap, interpretable, and easily deployable on microcontrollers.

## 1.3 Why Logistic Regression in Robotics?

| Robotic Application | Classification Task | Why Logistic Regression? |
|---|---|---|
| Obstacle Avoidance | Obstacle / Free-Path | Fast inference, low memory footprint |
| Terrain Classification | Traversable / Non-Traversable | Probability output enables fuzzy control |
| Human-Robot Interaction | Human-Present / Absent | Works well with small sensor datasets |
| Battery Fault Detection | Healthy / Degraded | Calibrated probability triggers alarms |
| Gesture Recognition | Static Hand Pose Classes | Forms the base of multinomial extensions |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis:** The syllabus highlights Logistic Regression as a **linear classifier** — meaning it can only create **linear decision boundaries** in the original feature space. To handle non-linear robotic sensor data, **feature engineering** (polynomial features, interaction terms) or **kernel extensions** are required.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

The Logistic Regression pipeline can be decomposed into the following sequential stages:

1. **Feature Assembly** — Collect input feature vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_0, x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$ where $x_0 = 1$ (bias). For a robot, these could be sensor readings (distance, voltage, current, IMU values).
2. **Linear Combination** — Compute the *logit* (also called *log-odds*) $z = \theta^T \mathbf{x} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \dots + \theta_n x_n$.
3. **Sigmoid Transformation** — Apply the logistic function $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ to squash $z \in (-\infty, +\infty)$ into a probability $h \in (0, 1)$.
4. **Threshold Decision** — Apply a threshold $\tau$ (default $\tau = 0.5$) to assign the class label: $\hat{y} = 1$ if $h \geq \tau$, else $\hat{y} = 0$.
5. **Loss Computation** — Quantify prediction error using **Binary Cross-Entropy (Log-Loss)**.
6. **Parameter Update** — Optimize $\theta$ via **Gradient Descent** to minimize the loss.
7. **Convergence Check** — Stop when loss change $\leq \epsilon$ or maximum iterations reached.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Stage

- **Why the Sigmoid?** It is the **canonical inverse of the logit function** $\text{logit}(p) = \ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)$. Using sigmoid ensures the model's output is a **valid probability distribution** that sums correctly for the two classes.
- **Why Log-Loss (and not MSE)?** The Mean Squared Error cost function creates a **non-convex** surface when applied to the sigmoid, causing gradient descent to get stuck in local minima. The **Log-Loss is provably convex** for Logistic Regression, guaranteeing convergence to the **global optimum**.
- **Why Threshold of 0.5?** This is derived from the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** decision rule assuming **equal class priors** $P(y=0) = P(y=1) = 0.5$. For imbalanced robotic datasets (e.g., rare fault detection), the threshold is shifted.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Range / Units** | **Engineering Role** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sigmoid (Logistic) Function | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | $(0, 1)$ — dimensionless | Probability mapping |
| Hypothesis | $h_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\theta^T \mathbf{x})$ | $(0, 1)$ | Predicted probability |
| Logit (Log-Odds) | $\text{logit}(p) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{p}{1-p}\right)$ | $(-\infty, +\infty)$ | Inverse of sigmoid |
| Decision Rule | $\hat{y} = \mathbb{1}[h_\theta(\mathbf{x}) \geq \tau]$ | $\{0, 1\}$ | Class label assignment |
| Log-Loss (Single Sample) | $L = -\!\left[ y \ln(h) + (1-y)\ln(1-h) \right]$ | $[0, +\infty)$ | Per-sample cost |
| Cost Function (Batch) | $J(\theta) = -\dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} \!\left[ y^{(i)}\ln(h^{(i)}) + (1-y^{(i)})\ln(1-h^{(i)}) \right]$ | $[0, +\infty)$ | Aggregate cost |
| Gradient of $J$ | $\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} (h^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$ | scalar | Parameter update direction |
| Gradient Descent Update | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$ | scalar | Learning step |
| Multinomial Softmax (extension) | $P(y=k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{e^{\theta_k^T \mathbf{x}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{\theta_j^T \mathbf{x}}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | K-class generalization |
| Odds Ratio | $\text{Odds} = \dfrac{p}{1-p}$ | $[0, +\infty)$ | Interpretability metric |
| L2 Regularization Term | $\dfrac{\lambda}{2m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta_j^2$ | scalar | Prevents overfitting |
| Regularized Gradient | $\dfrac{\partial J_{\text{reg}}}{\partial \theta_j} = \dfrac{1}{m}\sum (h^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)} + \dfrac{\lambda}{m}\theta_j$ | scalar | Stable training |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention:** All probabilities and sigmoid outputs are **dimensionless** quantities in $[0, 1]$. The logit and log-loss are dimensionless but measured in **nats** (natural log base) or **bits** (log base 2). The learning rate $\alpha$ is dimensionless.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern **robotic systems**, Logistic Regression is the foundational building block for:
- **Edge AI Deployment:** Runs on microcontrollers (Arduino, ESP32, STM32) consuming < 1 KB memory.
- **Sensor Fusion Decisions:** Acts as a binary gate deciding whether to trust a noisy sensor reading.
- **Probabilistic Path Planning:** Outputs feed into **Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs)** as observation likelihoods.
- **Safety Classifiers in Human-Robot Collaboration (HRC):** Determines whether a human is within the robot's safety zone using proximity sensors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 KTU scheme specifically tests the **convexity property** of the Log-Loss and the **linear decision boundary** property of Logistic Regression. These are high-frequency board exam topics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Sigmoid Function from the Logit

The logit function transforms a probability $p \in (0,1)$ into a real-valued log-odds:

$$
\text{logit}(p) = \ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = z
$$

**Step 1:** Exponentiate both sides of the equation $z = \ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)$:

$$
e^z = \frac{p}{1-p}
$$

**Step 2:** Cross-multiply to isolate $p$:

$$
e^z (1 - p) = p \implies e^z - e^z p = p
$$

**Step 3:** Collect $p$ terms on one side:

$$
e^z = p + e^z p = p(1 + e^z)
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $p$:

$$
p = \frac{e^z}{1 + e^z} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
$$

**Final Result:** This is the **sigmoid (logistic) function**, denoted $\sigma(z)$. The derivation confirms that sigmoid is the **canonical inverse** of the logit — a bijection from $\mathbb{R} \to (0,1)$.

$$
\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sigmoid Derivative (Critical for Backpropagation)

The gradient of the sigmoid function has an elegant closed-form that simplifies gradient computations enormously.

**Step 1:** Start with the definition:

$$
\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} = (1 + e^{-z})^{-1}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the chain rule $\frac{d}{dz}[f(g(z))] = f'(g(z)) \cdot g'(z)$ where $f(u) = u^{-1}$ and $g(z) = 1 + e^{-z}$:

$$
\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = -1 \cdot (1 + e^{-z})^{-2} \cdot (-e^{-z}) = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^2}
$$

**Step 3:** Rewrite using the identity $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ and $1 - \sigma(z) = \frac{e^{-z}}{1 + e^{-z}}$:

$$
\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} \cdot \frac{e^{-z}}{1 + e^{-z}} = \sigma(z)\bigl(1 - \sigma(z)\bigr)
$$

**Final Elegant Result:**

$$
\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\bigl(1 - \sigma(z)\bigr)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** This compact form is why logistic regression is computationally efficient — gradient computations avoid expensive exponentials, making it ideal for **real-time robotic inference loops** running at $> 100$ Hz.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Log-Loss Cost Function from Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)

We derive the cost function from the **principle of maximum likelihood** for a Bernoulli distribution.

**Step 1:** Model the class label as a Bernoulli random variable:

$$
P(y \mid \mathbf{x}; \theta) = h^y (1 - h)^{1-y}, \quad \text{where } h = h_\theta(\mathbf{x})
$$

**Step 2:** Form the **likelihood function** over $m$ independent training samples:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(y^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}^{(i)}; \theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} (h^{(i)})^{y^{(i)}} (1 - h^{(i)})^{1 - y^{(i)}}
$$

**Step 3:** Take the natural logarithm to obtain the **log-likelihood** (monotonic transformation preserves the argmax):

$$
\ell(\theta) = \ln \mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \ln h^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \ln (1 - h^{(i)}) \right]
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to a **minimization problem** by negating, and scale by $\frac{1}{m}$ to obtain the average cost:

$$
J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \ell(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \ln h^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \ln (1 - h^{(i)}) \right]
$$

**Final Result — The Binary Cross-Entropy Cost Function:**

$$
J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \ln(h^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \ln(1 - h^{(i)}) \right]
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** Always present the negative sign in the cost function explicitly. Many students drop it and confuse **maximization of likelihood** with **minimization of cost** — this is a **2-mark deduction** pattern observed in past evaluations.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Gradient of the Cost Function

We derive $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$ which is essential for the gradient descent update rule.

**Step 1:** Recall $h^{(i)} = \sigma(z^{(i)})$ where $z^{(i)} = \theta^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$. Write the cost for a single sample as:

$$
J^{(i)} = -\left[ y^{(i)} \ln h^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \ln (1 - h^{(i)}) \right]
$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to $z^{(i)}$ using the chain rule and the sigmoid derivative derived above:

$$
\frac{\partial J^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}} = -\left[ y^{(i)} \frac{1}{h^{(i)}} \cdot h^{(i)}(1 - h^{(i)}) - (1 - y^{(i)}) \frac{1}{1 - h^{(i)}} \cdot h^{(i)}(1 - h^{(i)}) \right]
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify — the $(1 - h^{(i)})$ and $h^{(i)}$ terms cancel beautifully:

$$
\frac{\partial J^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}} = -\left[ y^{(i)}(1 - h^{(i)}) - (1 - y^{(i)}) h^{(i)} \right] = h^{(i)} - y^{(i)}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the chain rule with $\frac{\partial z^{(i)}}{\partial \theta_j} = x_j^{(i)}$:

$$
\frac{\partial J^{(i)}}{\partial \theta_j} = (h^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) \, x_j^{(i)}
$$

**Step 5:** Average over all $m$ samples:

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) \, x_j^{(i)}
$$

**Final Result — Gradient Vector:**

$$
\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m} \mathbf{X}^T (\mathbf{h} - \mathbf{y})
$$

where $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times (n+1)}$ is the design matrix and $\mathbf{h}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ are prediction and label vectors.

## 3.5 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem:** A warehouse robot uses a single distance sensor $x_1$ (in meters) to classify whether an obstacle is present ($y=1$) or absent ($y=0$). After training, the model has $\theta_0 = -3$ and $\theta_1 = 2$. Compute the probability of an obstacle when $x_1 = 1.5$ m. State the robot's decision using threshold $\tau = 0.5$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the logit (linear combination):

$$
z = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 = -3 + 2 \times 1.5 = -3 + 3 = 0
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the sigmoid function:

$$
h = \sigma(0) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{0}} = \frac{1}{1 + 1} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the decision rule with $\tau = 0.5$:

$$
\hat{y} = \mathbb{1}[0.5 \geq 0.5] = 1
$$

**Final Answer:** The robot predicts an **obstacle is present** with probability **$0.5$** (i.e., the sensor reading is at the **decision boundary**).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Logistic Regression — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following block diagram maps the complete computational pipeline of a Logistic Regression classifier as deployed in a robotic decision system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[ROBOTIC INPUT SENSORS]:::inputNode
    node2[FEATURE VECTOR ASSEMBLY x1 x2 ... xn]:::processNode
    node3[BIAS AUGMENTATION x0 equals 1]:::processNode
    node4[LINEAR COMBINATION z equals theta transpose x]:::mathNode
    node5[SIGMOID ACTIVATION sigma z]:::mathNode
    node6[PROBABILITY OUTPUT h in 0 1]:::outputNode
    node7[THRESHOLD COMPARATOR tau equals 0.5]:::decisionNode
    node8[CLASS LABEL y hat equals 0]:::outputNode
    node9[CLASS LABEL y hat equals 1]:::outputNode
    node10[ACTUATOR COMMAND ROBOT ACTION]:::actionNode
    node11[GROUND TRUTH y]:::truthNode
    node12[LOG LOSS COMPUTATION]:::lossNode
    node13[COST FUNCTION J theta]:::lossNode
    node14[GRADIENT DESCENT OPTIMIZER]:::optimNode
    node15[PARAMETER UPDATE theta]:::optimNode

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8
    node7 --> node9
    node8 --> node10
    node9 --> node10
    node6 --> node12
    node11 --> node12
    node12 --> node13
    node13 --> node14
    node14 --> node15
    node15 --> node4

    classDef inputNode fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef processNode fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0891b2,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef mathNode fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#9a3412,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef outputNode fill:#166534,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef decisionNode fill:#a16207,stroke:#ca8a04,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef actionNode fill:#be123c,stroke:#e11d48,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef truthNode fill:#4c1d95,stroke:#5b21b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef lossNode fill:#7e22ce,stroke:#9333ea,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef optimNode fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0d9488,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Decision Boundary Geometry — Linear Classifier Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[FEATURE SPACE x1 x2]:::spaceNode
    nodeB[LINEAR BOUNDARY theta0 plus theta1 x1 plus theta2 x2 equals 0]:::boundaryNode
    nodeC[REGION y equals 0 NEGATIVE CLASS]:::regionA
    nodeD[REGION y equals 1 POSITIVE CLASS]:::regionB
    nodeE[NORMAL VECTOR TO BOUNDARY]:::vectorNode

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE

    classDef spaceNode fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef boundaryNode fill:#a16207,stroke:#ca8a04,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef regionA fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef regionB fill:#14532d,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef vectorNode fill:#4c1d95,stroke:#5b21b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Training Loop Flowchart — Gradient Descent Optimization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[INITIALIZE theta RANDOM OR ZEROS]:::initNode
    stepB[COMPUTE HYPOTHESIS h theta x]:::forwardNode
    stepC[COMPUTE COST J theta]:::costNode
    stepD[CHECK CONVERGENCE J CHANGE BELOW EPSILON]:::checkNode
    stepE[COMPUTE GRADIENT dJ BY d theta]:::gradNode
    stepF[UPDATE theta theta minus alpha times gradient]:::updateNode
    stepG[OUTPUT OPTIMAL theta STAR]:::finalNode
    stepH[TERMINATE]:::stopNode
    stepI[CONTINUE TRAINING]:::loopNode

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepH
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepI
    stepI --> stepB
    stepH --> stepG

    classDef initNode fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0891b2,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef forwardNode fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#9a3412,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef costNode fill:#7e22ce,stroke:#9333ea,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef checkNode fill:#a16207,stroke:#ca8a04,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef gradNode fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0d9488,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef updateNode fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef finalNode fill:#166534,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef stopNode fill:#be123c,stroke:#e11d48,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef loopNode fill:#4c1d95,stroke:#5b21b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The arrow from `PARAMETER UPDATE theta` back to `LINEAR COMBINATION` in the first diagram represents the **iterative closed-loop nature** of the training process — the updated $\theta$ is fed back into the forward pass until convergence is achieved.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Logistic Regression. Why is it preferred over Linear Regression for classification tasks in robotics?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Logistic Regression is a **supervised learning classification algorithm** that models the probability of a binary outcome $y \in \{0, 1\}$ given input features $\mathbf{x}$, using the **sigmoid activation function** $\sigma(\theta^T \mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^T \mathbf{x}}}$. **(1 Mark)**

It is preferred over Linear Regression for classification because:
- Linear Regression outputs **unbounded real values**, which cannot be interpreted as probabilities. **(1 Mark)**
- Logistic Regression outputs are **bounded in $(0, 1)$**, providing a valid probabilistic interpretation essential for robotic decision thresholds (e.g., obstacle confidence). **(1 Mark)**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of the sigmoid function in Logistic Regression. State its key properties.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The sigmoid function $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ maps the linear combination $z = \theta^T \mathbf{x}$ (which can take any real value) into a probability in the range $(0, 1)$. **(1 Mark)**

**Key Properties:** **(2 Marks — 1 each)**
1. **Range Bounded:** $\sigma(z) \in (0, 1)$ for all $z \in \mathbb{R}$, ensuring valid probability outputs.
2. **Differentiable and Smooth:** Its derivative $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ is analytically tractable, enabling efficient gradient-based learning on robotic hardware.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Pattern:** Students must answer **ONE** of the two alternatives. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**(a)** Derive the **Binary Cross-Entropy (Log-Loss) cost function** for Logistic Regression starting from the Bernoulli likelihood assumption. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 2D robotic terrain classification problem, the trained logistic regression has parameters $\theta_0 = 0$, $\theta_1 = 1$, $\theta_2 = -1$. A new sensor reading gives $\mathbf{x} = [1, 2, 1]^T$. Compute the probability of class 1 and the robot's classification decision. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Bernoulli Likelihood Setup** *(1 Mark)*: Model the label as Bernoulli:

$$
P(y \mid \mathbf{x}; \theta) = h^y (1 - h)^{1-y}, \quad h = \sigma(\theta^T \mathbf{x})
$$

**Step 2 — Form Likelihood over $m$ Samples** *(1 Mark)*:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} (h^{(i)})^{y^{(i)}} (1 - h^{(i)})^{1 - y^{(i)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Take Natural Logarithm** *(1 Mark)*: Monotonicity of $\ln$ preserves the argmax:

$$
\ell(\theta) = \ln \mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \ln h^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \ln(1 - h^{(i)}) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Negate and Normalize** *(1 Mark)*: Convert to minimization and average over $m$:

$$
J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \ln h^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \ln(1 - h^{(i)}) \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $h = \sigma(\theta^T \mathbf{x})$** *(1 Mark)*:

$$
J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \ln \sigma(\theta^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \ln(1 - \sigma(\theta^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)})) \right]
$$

**Step 6 — State the Convexity Property** *(1 Mark)*: The function $J(\theta)$ is **convex** in $\theta$, guaranteeing that gradient descent converges to the **global minimum**.

**Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation** *(1 Mark)*: For robotic applications, this convexity means the model is **guaranteed to converge during training** — a critical property for deterministic, reproducible safety-critical classifiers.

#### Model Solution for Part A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — State the Given Data** *(1 Mark)*: $\theta = [0, 1, -1]^T$, $\mathbf{x} = [1, 2, 1]^T$.

**Step 2 — Compute the Linear Combination (Logit)** *(2 Marks)*:

$$
z = \theta^T \mathbf{x} = (0)(1) + (1)(2) + (-1)(1) = 0 + 2 - 1 = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Sigmoid Function** *(2 Marks)*:

$$
h = \sigma(1) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-1}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.3679} = \frac{1}{1.3679} \approx 0.7311
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Decision Rule** *(1 Mark)*: With threshold $\tau = 0.5$:

$$
\hat{y} = \mathbb{1}[0.7311 \geq 0.5] = 1
$$

**Step 5 — Robotic Decision Statement** *(1 Mark)*: The robot classifies the terrain as **Class 1 (Traversable)** with probability **$0.7311$** and proceeds with motion.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**(a)** Explain the **Gradient Descent algorithm** for optimizing the Logistic Regression cost function. Derive the gradient expression $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$ step-by-step. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **concept of overfitting in Logistic Regression** and explain how **L2 Regularization** addresses it. Provide the modified cost function and gradient. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — State Gradient Descent Update Rule** *(1 Mark)*:

$$
\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate**.

**Step 2 — Write Per-Sample Cost** *(1 Mark)*:

$$
J^{(i)} = -\left[ y^{(i)} \ln h^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \ln(1 - h^{(i)}) \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $z^{(i)}$** *(2 Marks)*: Using the sigmoid derivative and chain rule:

$$
\frac{\partial J^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}} = h^{(i)} - y^{(i)}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Chain Rule to $\theta_j$** *(1 Mark)*: Since $z^{(i)} = \theta^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$ and $\frac{\partial z^{(i)}}{\partial \theta_j} = x_j^{(i)}$:

$$
\frac{\partial J^{(i)}}{\partial \theta_j} = (h^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) \, x_j^{(i)}
$$

**Step 5 — Average Across $m$ Samples** *(1 Mark)*:

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) \, x_j^{(i)}
$$

**Step 6 — Vectorized Form and Convergence Note** *(1 Mark)*:

$$
\nabla_\theta J = \frac{1}{m} \mathbf{X}^T (\mathbf{h} - \mathbf{y})
$$

The algorithm converges when $J(\theta)$ plateaus; the learning rate $\alpha$ controls step size — too large causes divergence, too small causes slow convergence.

#### Model Solution for Part B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define Overfitting** *(1 Mark)*: Overfitting occurs when the model **memorizes training noise** and fails to generalize to new robotic sensor data, indicated by **low training error but high validation error**.

**Step 2 — Why Logistic Regression Overfits** *(1 Mark)*: With many features (e.g., high-dimensional sensor arrays), $\theta$ magnitudes grow large to fit noise, creating overly complex decision boundaries.

**Step 3 — L2 Regularization Concept** *(1 Mark)*: Add a **penalty term** proportional to the squared magnitude of parameters, weighted by regularization strength $\lambda$:

$$
J_{\text{reg}}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \ln h^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \ln(1 - h^{(i)}) \right] + \frac{\lambda}{2m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta_j^2
$$

**Step 4 — Modified Gradient** *(2 Marks — 1 each)*:

For $j = 0$ (bias), the gradient is **unregularized**:

$$
\frac{\partial J_{\text{reg}}}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) x_0^{(i)}
$$

For $j \geq 1$ (feature weights), the regularized gradient is:

$$
\frac{\partial J_{\text{reg}}}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)} + \frac{\lambda}{m} \theta_j
$$

**Step 5 — Effect of Regularization** *(1 Mark)*: The penalty **shrinks $\theta_j$** toward zero, reducing model complexity and improving generalization to unseen robotic scenarios.

**Step 6 — Engineering Trade-off** *(1 Mark)*: The hyperparameter $\lambda$ must be tuned via **cross-validation**; large $\lambda$ causes **underfitting** (high bias), small $\lambda$ causes **overfitting** (high variance).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to **exclude $\theta_0$ (bias) from regularization**. The bias term is **not penalized** in standard L2 regularization — penalizing it introduces systematic errors.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Writing the cost function with a **positive sign** instead of **negative sign** in front of the log-loss term. The Log-Loss must be **negated** because we minimize negative log-likelihood.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing **log-loss with MSE**. MSE is **non-convex** for logistic regression; only **log-loss guarantees convexity**.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Using **$e^{-z}$ vs $e^{z}$** in the sigmoid — both are equivalent, but mixing them in a single derivation loses **1 mark** for notational inconsistency.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Forgetting the **$\frac{1}{m}$ normalization** in the batch cost function. This causes an $\alpha$-dependent scale mismatch in gradient descent updates.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Logistic Regression (KTU Module 2)**

- **Logistic Regression is a CLASSIFICATION algorithm**, not a regression algorithm — despite the name. **(High-Frequency Trap)**
- The **sigmoid function** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ maps $\mathbb{R} \to (0, 1)$, producing valid probabilities. Output is strictly bounded away from 0 and 1.
- The **decision boundary** is defined by $\theta^T \mathbf{x} = 0$, which is a **linear hyperplane** in feature space.
- The **default decision threshold** is $\tau = 0.5$, derived from the MAP rule with **equal class priors**. Shift $\tau$ for imbalanced robotic datasets.
- The **Log-Loss (Binary Cross-Entropy)** cost function is the **negative log-likelihood** of a Bernoulli distribution and is **provably convex**.
- The **gradient** has the elegant form $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum (h^{(i)} - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)}$ — note the symmetry with linear regression.
- The **sigmoid derivative** simplifies to $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$, making backpropagation computationally efficient.
- **L2 Regularization** adds $\frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum \theta_j^2$ to the cost and $\frac{\lambda}{m}\theta_j$ to the gradient (for $j \geq 1$ only — **not** for bias $\theta_0$).
- **Multiclass extension** uses the **Softmax function** in place of the sigmoid, generalizing logistic regression to $K$-class problems.
- The **odds ratio** $\frac{p}{1-p} = e^{\theta^T \mathbf{x}}$ provides an interpretable metric: a unit increase in $x_j$ multiplies the odds by $e^{\theta_j}$.
- Logistic Regression requires **feature scaling** (standardization) for fast gradient descent convergence in high-dimensional robotic sensor data.
- **Limitations:** Cannot capture non-linear relationships without explicit feature engineering (polynomial features, kernel tricks, or interaction terms).
- **Regularization strength $\lambda$** is tuned via **k-fold cross-validation**; the optimal value balances **bias-variance trade-off**.
- **Evaluation metrics** for classification in robotics: **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, ROC-AUC, Confusion Matrix** — never rely on accuracy alone for imbalanced data.
- For **real-time robotic deployment**, the trained model is a simple matrix-vector multiplication followed by a sigmoid — **O(n) complexity** per inference, suitable for embedded microcontrollers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Support Vector Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT614 - INTRODUCTION TO AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Support Vector Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a **supervised machine learning algorithm** developed by *Vladimir N. Vapnik* and *Alexey Ya. Chervonenkis* (Boser–Guyon–Vapnik, 1992) used for **classification, regression, and outlier detection**. It constructs an **optimal hyperplane** (decision boundary) in an N-dimensional feature space that **maximizes the margin** between different class data points.

In the context of **AI in Robotics**, SVMs are widely deployed for:
- **Object recognition** in robot vision systems
- **Gesture classification** for human–robot interaction
- **Terrain classification** for autonomous ground vehicles
- **Fault diagnosis** in robotic manipulators

The fundamental objective of an SVM is to find the **Maximum Margin Hyperplane (MMH)** that separates classes while being maximally distant from the nearest training data points (called **support vectors**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Quick-Glance**
> - **Hyperplane**: The decision boundary that separates classes. In 2D it is a line, in 3D it is a plane, and in higher dimensions it is a hyperplane.
> - **Support Vectors**: The data points closest to the hyperplane. They are the *only* points that influence its position and orientation.
> - **Margin**: The perpendicular distance between the separating hyperplane and the nearest data point of any class.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **wide street** running between two rows of buildings on either side. The street represents the **margin**, and the kerb stones of the buildings closest to the street represent the **support vectors**.

If you were an urban planner, you would want to build the **widest possible street** so that:
- Any new building (new data point) is clearly identified as belonging to the left or right side.
- Small errors in building positions (noise) do not cause misclassification.
- The street is equidistant from the kerbs of both sides (maximum margin principle).

The **two rows of buildings** = two different classes (e.g., *obstacle* vs. *free space* in robotic path planning).
The **widest street you can place** = the **optimal hyperplane** chosen by the SVM.

> [!TIP]
> Think of SVMs as a **fair referee** standing exactly in the middle of two opposing teams, maximizing the gap to ensure no player from either team accidentally crosses the line.

## 1.3 Why SVMs Matter in Robotics

| Robotic Application | Role of SVM |
|---|---|
| Visual object detection | Classifies image patches as *target* / *non-target* |
| SLAM loop closure | Binary classification of candidate image pairs |
| Speech/gesture recognition | Multi-class classification with one-vs-rest SVM |
| Anomaly detection in actuators | One-class SVM for outlier identification |
| Grasp stability prediction | Classifies grasp attempts as *stable* / *unstable* |

The robustness of SVM against the **Curse of Dimensionality** makes it especially suited for high-dimensional sensor data (LiDAR, depth cameras) used in modern robotic platforms.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Linearly Separable Case — Hard Margin SVM

Given a training dataset:
$$
\{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}, \quad \mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d, \quad y_i \in \{-1, +1\}
$$

The decision hyperplane is defined as:
$$
\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the **weight (normal) vector** perpendicular to the hyperplane
- $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **bias term**

For correct classification of all training points, the following constraints must hold:
$$
y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, N
$$

### 2.1.1 Margin Calculation

The perpendicular distance from a point $\mathbf{x}_i$ to the hyperplane is:
$$
d_i = \frac{\vert \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}
$$

For the nearest support vectors, $\vert \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b \vert = 1$, hence the **total margin** between the two classes is:
$$
M = \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}
$$

### 2.1.2 Optimization Objective

Maximizing the margin $M$ is equivalent to **minimizing** $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2
$$

subject to:
$$
y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is a mathematical convenience that simplifies differentiation and does not affect the optimal solution.

## 2.2 Soft Margin SVM (Non-Separable Case)

In real-world robotics data, perfect linear separation is rare. The **Soft Margin SVM** (Cortes & Vapnik, 1995) introduces **slack variables** $\xi_i \geq 0$:

$$
y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0
$$

The primal optimization problem becomes:
$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i
$$

where **$C > 0$** is the **regularization parameter (penalty hyperparameter)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Large $C$** → heavy penalty on misclassification → narrower margin, fewer errors (risk of overfitting).
> - **Small $C$** → lighter penalty → wider margin, more errors allowed (better generalization).

## 2.3 Dual Formulation (Lagrangian Approach)

Using **Lagrange multipliers** $\alpha_i \geq 0$, the primal problem is converted into the **dual problem**, which is computationally superior and enables the **kernel trick**.

### 2.3.1 Lagrangian for Hard Margin

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \right]
$$

### 2.3.2 Dual Optimization Problem

$$
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j)
$$

subject to:
$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0, \quad \alpha_i \geq 0
$$

The final decision function becomes:
$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x} + b \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Only data points with $\alpha_i > 0$ are **support vectors** — they alone define the hyperplane. All other points have $\alpha_i = 0$ and are irrelevant at inference time.

## 2.4 The Kernel Trick

For **non-linearly separable** data, SVM projects the input into a higher-dimensional feature space $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ where a linear separator exists. To avoid the prohibitive cost of explicit projection, the **kernel function** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$ is computed directly.

### 2.4.1 Standard Kernels (KTU-Exam Essentials)

| Kernel Type | Mathematical Form $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | Key Parameters | Typical Use in Robotics |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ | None | High-dimensional sensor data (e.g., raw images) |
| **Polynomial** | $(\gamma \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j + r)^d$ | degree $d$, coef0 $r$ | Image processing, polynomial features |
| **RBF (Gaussian)** | $\exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2)$ | $\gamma > 0$ | Non-linear terrain/obstacle classification |
| **Sigmoid** | $\tanh(\gamma \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j + r)$ | $\gamma$, $r$ | Neural-net-like behaviour |

> [!WARNING]
> - The **RBF kernel** is the most commonly used in robotic perception because it can handle arbitrarily complex, non-linear decision boundaries.
> - $\gamma$ controls the **influence radius** of each training point — large $\gamma$ → overfitting.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hyperplane Equation | $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | $d$-dimensional input |
| 2 | Geometric Margin | $M = \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Total width of the margin |
| 3 | Distance from point to hyperplane | $d = \frac{\vert \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Scalar, units of $\mathbf{x}$ |
| 4 | Primal Objective (Hard) | $\min \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ | Subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i+b) \geq 1$ |
| 5 | Primal Objective (Soft) | $\min \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum \xi_i$ | $\xi_i \geq 0$ |
| 6 | Dual Objective | $\max \sum \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | $\alpha_i \geq 0$ |
| 7 | Decision Function | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\left( \sum \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b \right)$ | Output $\in \{-1, +1\}$ |
| 8 | RBF Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2)$ | $\gamma > 0$ |
| 9 | Polynomial Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\gamma \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j + r)^d$ | $d \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| 10 | KKT Complementarity | $\alpha_i [y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1] = 0$ | Identifies support vectors |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern robotic AI pipelines (e.g., **ROS 2 + MoveIt + OpenCV**), SVMs serve as lightweight, deterministic classifiers for embedded systems where deep neural networks are too computationally expensive. SVMs offer:
- **Strong mathematical guarantees** (global optimum via convex optimization).
- **Memory efficiency** (only support vectors need to be stored).
- **Robustness** to high-dimensional data typical of LiDAR/point-cloud inputs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Margin Width from Geometric Distance

### 3.1.1 Statement
Show that the total margin width between the two support hyperplanes is $M = \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the two supporting hyperplanes (one for each class).**

For the positive class ($y_i = +1$) and negative class ($y_i = -1$), the two parallel support hyperplanes are:
$$
H_+ : \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = +1
$$
$$
H_- : \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = -1
$$

**Step 2 — Identify a representative point on each hyperplane.**

Let $\mathbf{x}_+$ be any point on $H_+$ and $\mathbf{x}_-$ be any point on $H_-$:
$$
\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_+ + b = +1
$$
$$
\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_- + b = -1
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the two equations to relate the points.**

Subtracting the second from the first:
$$
\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_+ - \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_- = 1 - (-1)
$$
$$
\mathbf{w}^\top (\mathbf{x}_+ - \mathbf{x}_-) = 2
$$

**Step 4 — Project the difference vector onto the normal $\mathbf{w}$.**

The margin width is the projection of $(\mathbf{x}_+ - \mathbf{x}_-)$ onto the unit normal $\frac{\mathbf{w}}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$:
$$
M = (\mathbf{x}_+ - \mathbf{x}_-) \cdot \frac{\mathbf{w}}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{\mathbf{w}^\top (\mathbf{x}_+ - \mathbf{x}_-)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the result from Step 3.**

$$
M = \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Minimizing $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ directly maximizes the margin $M$. This is the geometric foundation of the SVM optimization objective.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Converting the Primal to the Dual Problem

### 3.2.1 Statement
Derive the dual form of the hard-margin SVM optimization problem using the Lagrangian method.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the primal problem.**

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2
$$
subject to:
$$
g_i(\mathbf{w}, b) = 1 - y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \leq 0, \quad \forall i
$$

**Step 2 — Construct the Lagrangian.**

Introduce Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$:
$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Set partial derivatives to zero (stationarity conditions).**

Partial derivative w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$:
$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i = 0
$$
$$
\Rightarrow \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i \quad \text{(Eq. A)}
$$

Partial derivative w.r.t. $b$:
$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$
$$
\Rightarrow \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \quad \text{(Eq. B)}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the Lagrangian to obtain the dual.**

Substituting Eq. (A) and Eq. (B) into $\mathcal{L}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_D &= \frac{1}{2} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i \right)^\top \left( \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_j y_j \mathbf{x}_j \right) - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i \left( \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_j y_j \mathbf{x}_j \right)^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b y_i - 1 \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j) - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j) - b \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Eq. (B) to eliminate the $b$ term.**

Since $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$, the $b$ term vanishes:
$$
\mathcal{L}_D = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j) + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i
$$

**Step 6 — Final dual optimization problem.**

Maximizing $\mathcal{L}_D$ is equivalent to:
$$
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j)
$$
subject to:
$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0, \quad \alpha_i \geq 0
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why the dual?** The dual form depends only on **inner products** $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$. This is what unlocks the **kernel trick** — we can replace the inner product with any valid kernel function $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ without ever computing $\phi(\mathbf{x})$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Kernel Trick — Polynomial Kernel Expansion

### 3.3.1 Statement
Show that the polynomial kernel $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = (\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z} + 1)^2$ corresponds to an explicit feature map $\phi(\cdot)$ in a higher-dimensional space.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation (for 2-D inputs)

**Step 1 — Expand the kernel for $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)$ and $\mathbf{z} = (z_1, z_2)$.**

$$
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = (x_1 z_1 + x_2 z_2 + 1)^2
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the square.**

$$
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = x_1^2 z_1^2 + 2 x_1 z_1 x_2 z_2 + x_2^2 z_2^2 + 2 x_1 z_1 + 2 x_2 z_2 + 1
$$

**Step 3 — Re-arrange as inner product of two explicit feature vectors.**

Group terms to identify the components of $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ and $\phi(\mathbf{z})$:

$$
\phi(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} x_1^2 \\ \sqrt{2} x_1 x_2 \\ x_2^2 \\ \sqrt{2} x_1 \\ \sqrt{2} x_2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \phi(\mathbf{z}) = \begin{bmatrix} z_1^2 \\ \sqrt{2} z_1 z_2 \\ z_2^2 \\ \sqrt{2} z_1 \\ \sqrt{2} z_2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the inner product equals the kernel.**

$$
\phi(\mathbf{x})^\top \phi(\mathbf{z}) = x_1^2 z_1^2 + 2 x_1 x_2 z_1 z_2 + x_2^2 z_2^2 + 2 x_1 z_1 + 2 x_2 z_2 + 1 = K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance:** A 2-D input is implicitly mapped to a **6-D feature space** without ever performing the transformation explicitly — saving huge computational cost. This is the essence of the **kernel trick**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SVM Block-Level Functional Architecture (Robotics Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sensor Data\nLiDAR / Camera / IMU] --> B[Pre-processing\nNoise Filtering & Normalization]
    B --> C[Feature Extraction\nHOG / SIFT / Point Cloud Descriptors]
    C --> D[Training Dataset\nLabelled Samples for each Class]
    D --> E[Kernel Selection\nLinear / RBF / Polynomial]
    E --> F[SVM Optimization Engine\nConvex QP Solver]
    F --> G{Convergence?}
    G -- No --> F
    G -- Yes --> H[Compute Support Vectors\nand Bias Term]
    H --> I[Trained SVM Model\nStored as alpha_i, x_i, y_i]
    I --> J[Real-Time Robot Inference]
    J --> K{Decision Function Output}
    K -- Class +1 --> L[Action: Move Forward\nFree Space]
    K -- Class -1 --> M[Action: Stop or Reroute\nObstacle Detected]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The diagram traces a typical robotic vision pipeline — from raw sensor ingestion, through SVM-based classification, to action selection. The optimization engine solves a Quadratic Programming (QP) problem iteratively until convergence is achieved.

---

## 4.2 Maximum Margin Hyperplane — Geometric View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FS[Feature Space R2]
        direction LR
        S1((x1)) --- SV1[/Positive\nSupport Vector/]
        SV2[/Negative\nSupport Vector/] --- S2((x2))
        HP[Optimal Hyperplane\nw transpose x + b = 0]
        H1[H+ : w transpose x + b = +1]
        H2[H- : w transpose x + b = -1]
        SV1 -. lies on .-> H1
        SV2 -. lies on .-> H2
        HP --- M[Margin Width M = 2 over norm of w]
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> - The **two outer dashed lines** $H_+$ and $H_-$ touch the support vectors.
> - The **central solid line** is the optimal hyperplane.
> - The **margin** $M$ is the gap between $H_+$ and $H_-$.

---

## 4.3 SVM Algorithm Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start]) --> Q1{Is data linearly\nseparable?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1[Use Hard Margin SVM\nNo slack variables]
    Q1 -- No --> A2[Use Soft Margin SVM\nIntroduce slack xi_i and penalty C]
    A2 --> Q2{Do you need a\nnon-linear boundary?}
    Q2 -- No --> A3[Use Linear Kernel\nDot product only]
    Q2 -- Yes --> A4[Apply Kernel Trick]
    A4 --> Q3{Choose Kernel Type}
    Q3 -- RBF --> K1[Gaussian Kernel]
    Q3 -- Polynomial --> K2[Polynomial Kernel]
    Q3 -- Sigmoid --> K3[Sigmoid Kernel]
    K1 --> Solve[Solve Dual QP Problem]
    K2 --> Solve
    K3 --> Solve
    A1 --> Solve
    A3 --> Solve
    Solve --> Output[Output: Optimal w, b\nand Support Vectors]
    Output --> End([End])
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define the term "Support Vector" in the context of Support Vector Machines. Why are they important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **support vector** is a data point from the training set that lies closest to the decision hyperplane — i.e., it satisfies $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$. These are the *only* data points whose **Lagrange multiplier $\alpha_i > 0$** in the dual formulation.
>
> **Importance (2 marks):**
> 1. They **define the position and orientation** of the optimal hyperplane.
> 2. Removing or relocating any other non-support training point does **not** change the decision boundary.
> 3. They determine the **margin width** $M = 2 / \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between Hard Margin and Soft Margin SVM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Hard Margin SVM | Soft Margin SVM |
|---|---|---|
| Assumption | Data is **perfectly linearly separable** | Data may have **overlap / noise** |
| Slack variables | Not used ($\xi_i = 0$) | Introduced $\xi_i \geq 0$ |
| Penalty term | None | $C \sum \xi_i$ added to objective |
| Misclassification | **Not allowed** | **Allowed** with penalty |
| Use case | Clean toy / synthetic data | Real-world robotic sensor data |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat diagram, explain the working of a Support Vector Machine for a two-class linearly separable classification problem. Derive the expression for the margin width.**  **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Problem Setup (2 marks):**
Given training data $\{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, SVM seeks a hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ that separates the two classes with **maximum margin**.

**2. Diagram (2 marks):**
[Show two parallel support hyperplanes $H_+$ and $H_-$ touching the nearest data points, with the optimal hyperplane midway between them. The margin $M$ is the distance between $H_+$ and $H_-$.]

**3. Margin Derivation (3 marks):**

The two support hyperplanes are:
$$
H_+ : \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = +1
$$
$$
H_- : \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = -1
$$

For any $\mathbf{x}_+ \in H_+$ and $\mathbf{x}_- \in H_-$:
$$
\mathbf{w}^\top (\mathbf{x}_+ - \mathbf{x}_-) = 2
$$

The margin is the projection onto the unit normal:
$$
M = \frac{\mathbf{w}^\top (\mathbf{x}_+ - \mathbf{x}_-)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}
$$

The optimization becomes $\min \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Kernel Trick in SVM. List any three commonly used kernel functions with their mathematical expressions.**  **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Concept of Kernel Trick (3 marks):**

The **kernel trick** is a mathematical technique that enables SVMs to learn **non-linear decision boundaries** without explicitly transforming data into a high-dimensional feature space.

For any two points $\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j$, instead of computing $\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$ (which is expensive for high-dimensional $\phi$), we compute a **kernel function** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ directly.

The dual objective becomes:
$$
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)
$$

**2. Three Common Kernels (4 marks = 1.5 + 1.5 + 1):**

| Kernel | Expression |
|---|---|
| **Linear Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ |
| **Polynomial Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\gamma \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j + r)^d$ |
| **RBF (Gaussian) Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2)$ |

> The RBF kernel is the most popular for robotic perception tasks because it can map data into an **infinite-dimensional** feature space.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Formulate the primal optimization problem for Soft Margin SVM. Explain the role of the regularization parameter $C$.**  **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Primal Formulation (4 marks):**

For non-separable data, slack variables $\xi_i \geq 0$ are introduced:
$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i
$$
subject to:
$$
y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0, \quad \forall i
$$

- The first term $\frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ → **maximizes the margin**.
- The second term $C \sum \xi_i$ → **penalizes misclassifications / margin violations**.

**2. Role of Regularization Parameter $C$ (3 marks):**

- **$C$ is a user-defined positive constant** that controls the **trade-off** between margin width and training error.
- **Large $C$** → strict penalty on $\xi_i$ → model tries to classify every point correctly → may **overfit** (narrow margin).
- **Small $C$** → tolerant of misclassifications → wider margin → better **generalization** (risk of underfitting).
- $C = \infty$ recovers the **hard-margin** SVM.

> [!NOTE]
> In robotic vision, $C$ is typically tuned via **cross-validation** to balance classification accuracy with robustness to noisy sensor data.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Derive the dual form of the hard-margin SVM optimization problem using Lagrange multipliers.**  **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Primal Problem (1 mark):**
$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1
$$

**2. Lagrangian Construction (2 marks):**
$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \right], \quad \alpha_i \geq 0
$$

**3. Stationarity Conditions (2 marks):**
$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i
$$
$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = 0 \Rightarrow \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

**4. Dual Problem (2 marks):**

Substituting back:
$$
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j)
$$
subject to $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$, $\alpha_i \geq 0$.

The decision function becomes:
$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}) + b \right)
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $\frac{1}{2}$** in the primal objective — it is mathematically required for clean differentiation.
> 2. **Writing the constraint as $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$** instead of $\geq 1$ for non-support points — the equality holds **only** for support vectors.
> 3. **Confusing primal and dual** — students often write the dual objective as minimization; it is always a **maximization** in canonical SVM form.
> 4. **Missing the constraint $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$** in the dual — this is a mandatory equality constraint.
> 5. **Wrong signs in Lagrangian** — the inequality constraint $g_i \leq 0$ must use a **minus** sign: $-\alpha_i g_i$.
> 6. **Conflating "support vector" with "any training point"** — only points with $\alpha_i > 0$ are support vectors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Support Vector Machines**
>
> - **Goal:** Find the **maximum-margin hyperplane** that separates two classes.
> - **Key geometric object:** $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ with margin $M = 2 / \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$.
> - **Support vectors** are the *closest* data points to the hyperplane; they uniquely define $\mathbf{w}$ and $b$.
> - **Hard Margin SVM** assumes perfect linear separability; uses primal $\min \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$.
> - **Soft Margin SVM** introduces slack variables $\xi_i$ and penalty $C \sum \xi_i$ for real-world noisy data.
> - **Lagrange multipliers** $\alpha_i \geq 0$ convert the primal to the dual, exposing inner products.
> - **Kernel Trick** replaces $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ with $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ to learn **non-linear** boundaries.
> - **Linear Kernel:** $K = \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$.
> - **Polynomial Kernel:** $K = (\gamma \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j + r)^d$.
> - **RBF Kernel:** $K = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2)$ — most common in robotics.
> - **$C$ (regularization):** Large $C$ → strict / overfit; Small $C$ → tolerant / underfit.
> - **$\gamma$ (RBF width):** Large $\gamma$ → narrow influence / overfit; Small $\gamma$ → wide influence / underfit.
> - **KKT conditions** identify support vectors: $\alpha_i [y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1] = 0$.
