> - **Robotic applications:** object detection, gesture recognition, terrain classification, fault diagnosis, SLAM loop closure.
> - **Advantages:** global optimum (convex), memory-efficient (only SVs), strong in high dimensions.
> - **Disadvantages:** poor on large datasets ($O(N^3)$ training), poor probability calibration without Platt scaling.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unsupervised Learning Algorithms: K-Means Clustering, K-Nearest Neighbors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT614 - INTRODUCTION TO AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Unsupervised Learning Algorithms: K-Means Clustering, K-Nearest Neighbors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Unsupervised Learning — Formal Definition

**Unsupervised Learning (UL)** is a paradigm of machine learning in which an autonomous agent learns the latent structure, distribution, or groupings of an input dataset **without access to any labeled supervisory signal** (i.e., no target output $y$ is provided alongside the input vector $\mathbf{x}$). Formally, given an unlabeled dataset

$$\mathcal{D} = \{ \mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(2)}, \dots, \mathbf{x}^{(N)} \}, \quad \mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$$

the unsupervised learner optimizes an objective function $J(\cdot)$ that captures properties such as compactness, density, separability, or reconstruction fidelity, producing either:
- A **partitioning** of $\mathcal{D}$ into $K$ groups (clustering), or
- A **low-dimensional embedding** that preserves the geometry of $\mathcal{D}$ (dimensionality reduction), or
- A **probability density estimate** $p(\mathbf{x})$ over the input space (density estimation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the OERUT614 course, unsupervised learning is the **foundation for robot perception tasks** such as self-organized map building (SLAM), topological place recognition, and unsupervised object discovery — because a robot in an unknown environment rarely possesses pre-labelled training data.

---

### 1.2 Intuition — A Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Sorting Laundry Without Instructions"**
> Imagine you are given a basket of mixed laundry (shirts, trousers, socks) with **no labels** and **no helper telling you what category each item belongs to**. You instinctively start grouping clothes by **visible similarity** — colour, size, fabric texture. The clusters you form are the output of an *unsupervised* algorithm. **K-Means** is the formal mathematical version of this human instinct: it finds the "centres of mass" of natural groupings and assigns every item to its nearest centre.

---

### 1.3 K-Means Clustering — Formal Definition

**K-Means Clustering** is the canonical *centroid-based, partitional* unsupervised clustering algorithm. It partitions $N$ observations into $K$ mutually exclusive clusters $\mathcal{C} = \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_K\}$ by iteratively minimizing the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)** objective:

$$J(\mathcal{C}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in C_k} \lVert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \rVert_2^{2}$$

where $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is the **centroid** (mean vector) of cluster $C_k$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **K** in K-Means is a **user-defined hyperparameter** representing the number of clusters. The algorithm is **non-deterministic** (sensitive to centroid initialization) and is **guaranteed to converge** in a finite number of steps, although it may converge to a **local minimum** rather than the global optimum.

---

### 1.4 K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) — Formal Definition

**K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)** is an **instance-based, non-parametric** learning algorithm. In its supervised form, classification is performed by a **majority vote** among the $K$ training samples closest (by a chosen distance metric) to the query point. In unsupervised / clustering contexts, KNN is used for:
- **Density-based clustering** (the distance to the $K^{th}$ neighbor estimates local density),
- **Outlier / novelty detection** in robotic sensor streams,
- **Similarity retrieval** in place-recognition databases.

Given a query $\mathbf{x}_q$, KNN identifies the index set

$$\mathcal{N}_K(\mathbf{x}_q) = \left\{ i : \text{rank}_i \big( d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}^{(i)}) \big) \leq K \right\}$$

where $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ is a distance metric (typically Euclidean).

> [!NOTE]
> **Robotics Use-Case:** A mobile robot traversing a corridor stores a database of laser-scan "fingerprints." When it queries a new scan, KNN retrieves the $K$ most similar previous locations to perform loop-closure detection — a critical step in SLAM.

---

### 1.5 Key Distinguishing Properties (At a Glance)

| Aspect | K-Means | KNN |
|---|---|---|
| Learning Type | Unsupervised (clustering) | Primarily supervised, but used in unsupervised tasks |
| Training Phase | Iterative centroid updates | None (lazy learner) |
| Time Complexity (Train) | $O(N \cdot K \cdot I \cdot d)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Time Complexity (Query) | $O(K \cdot d)$ per point | $O(N \cdot d)$ per query |
| Key Hyperparameter | $K$ (number of clusters) | $K$ (number of neighbors) |
| Output | Hard cluster assignment | Class label / neighbor indices |
| Sensitivity | Outliers, initialization, scale | Curse of dimensionality, scale |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 K-Means — Operational Logic (Lloyd's Algorithm)

The K-Means algorithm follows an **Expectation–Maximization-like two-step iteration** known as **Lloyd's Algorithm (1957)**. It alternates between:

**Step 1 — Assignment (E-step):** Assign each data point to the cluster whose centroid is nearest.
$$C_k^{(t)} = \left\{ \mathbf{x}^{(i)} : \lVert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t)} \rVert_2^{2} \leq \lVert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j^{(t)} \rVert_2^{2} \;\; \forall j \leq K \right\}$$

**Step 2 — Update (M-step):** Recompute each centroid as the arithmetic mean of all points currently assigned to it.
$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in C_k^{(t)}} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$$

**Termination Condition:** Stop when either (a) cluster assignments no longer change, (b) centroids move by less than a tolerance $\varepsilon$, or (c) a maximum iteration count $I_{\max}$ is reached.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this converge?** Each iteration is a **coordinate descent** on $J$. The assignment step minimizes $J$ w.r.t. $\{\mathcal{C}\}$ for fixed $\boldsymbol{\mu}$; the update step minimizes $J$ w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ for fixed $\{\mathcal{C}\}$. Hence $J^{(t+1)} \leq J^{(t)}$, and $J$ is bounded below by 0, guaranteeing convergence.

---

### 2.2 K-Means — Variants and Practical Heuristics

- **K-Means++ Initialization:** The first centroid is chosen uniformly at random; each subsequent centroid $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ is chosen with probability proportional to $D(\mathbf{x})^2$, where $D(\mathbf{x})$ is the distance from $\mathbf{x}$ to its nearest already-chosen centroid. This provides an $O(\log K)$ approximation guarantee to the optimal WCSS.
- **Elbow Method:** Plot $J$ versus $K$; the "elbow" point is the optimal $K$.
- **Silhouette Score:** Measures how similar a point is to its own cluster vs. the next-closest cluster, ranging from $-1$ to $+1$.

---

### 2.3 KNN — Distance Metrics (The Math of "Closeness")

For two vectors $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$, the most common distance metrics are:

| Metric | Formula | Order $p$ | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Euclidean ($L_2$) | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_j - y_j)^2}$ | $p=2$ | Default; continuous features |
| Manhattan ($L_1$) | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \lvert x_j - y_j \rvert$ | $p=1$ | High-dim sparse data, grids |
| Minkowski ($L_p$) | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{d} \lvert x_j - y_j \rvert^{p} \right)^{1/p}$ | $p \geq 1$ | Generalized family |
| Chebyshev ($L_\infty$) | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \max_j \lvert x_j - y_j \rvert$ | $p \to \infty$ | King-move grid problems |
| Cosine Distance | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = 1 - \frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\lVert \mathbf{x} \rVert \lVert \mathbf{y} \rVert}$ | — | Direction matters, not magnitude |

---

### 2.4 KNN — Decision Rule (Supervised Form)

For classification, the predicted class $\hat{y}$ for a query $\mathbf{x}_q$ is the **mode** of the labels of its $K$ neighbors:

$$\hat{y} = \underset{c \in \mathcal{Y}}{\arg\max} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_K(\mathbf{x}_q)} \mathbb{1}\!\left[ y^{(i)} = c \right]$$

For regression, the prediction is the **mean** (or weighted mean with weights $w_i = 1/d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}^{(i)})$):

$$\hat{y} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_K(\mathbf{x}_q)} y^{(i)}$$

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Symbol Meaning | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | K-Means Objective | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{i \in C_k} \lVert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \rVert_2^{2}$ | WCSS to minimize | $\geq 0$ |
| 2 | Centroid Update | $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{i \in C_k} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$ | New mean of cluster $k$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| 3 | Euclidean Distance | $d = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_j - y_j)^2}$ | Distance between 2 vectors | $\geq 0$ |
| 4 | Manhattan Distance | $d = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \lvert x_j - y_j \rvert$ | $L_1$ norm distance | $\geq 0$ |
| 5 | Minkowski Distance | $d = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{d} \lvert x_j - y_j \rvert^{p} \right)^{1/p}$ | Generalized $L_p$ norm | $\geq 0,\; p \geq 1$ |
| 6 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos\theta = \frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\lVert \mathbf{x} \rVert \lVert \mathbf{y} \rVert}$ | Angular similarity | $[-1, 1]$ |
| 7 | KNN Class Prediction | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_c \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_K} \mathbb{1}[y^{(i)} = c]$ | Majority vote | Discrete |
| 8 | KNN Regression | $\hat{y} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_K} y^{(i)}$ | Average of $K$ neighbors | Continuous |
| 9 | Silhouette Coefficient | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | $a$ = intra-cluster, $b$ = nearest-cluster | $[-1, 1]$ |
| 10 | Inertia (WCSS bound) | Lower $J \Rightarrow$ tighter clusters | Internal evaluation metric | $\geq 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **K-Means** is used in robotics for **vector quantization of LIDAR point clouds**, **colour segmentation in vision pipelines**, and **topological SLAM** graph partitioning.
> - **KNN** powers **content-based image retrieval**, **anomaly detection in sensor streams**, and **gesture recognition** in human-robot interaction (HRI) systems.

---

### 2.6 Critical Assumptions and Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Assumption Traps in K-Means (Common Exam Lapses):**
> 1. K-Means assumes **isotropic, spherical clusters** of roughly equal size — it fails on elongated or density-varied clusters (use DBSCAN or GMM instead).
> 2. Features must be **standardized** (zero mean, unit variance); otherwise high-magnitude features dominate the distance.
> 3. $K$ must be chosen *a priori* — K-Means cannot discover $K$ on its own.

> [!WARNING]
> **KNN Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Curse of Dimensionality** — in high dimensions ($d > 20$), all points become approximately equidistant, defeating nearest-neighbor logic.
> 2. **Lazy learning cost** — prediction is $O(N \cdot d)$; infeasible for real-time robotics without indexing (KD-Tree, Ball-Tree, FAISS).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the K-Means Centroid Update Rule

**Starting Point:** The K-Means objective is
$$J(\boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \dots, \boldsymbol{\mu}_K) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in C_k} \lVert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \rVert_2^{2}$$

**Step 1 — Expand the squared norm** using the identity $\lVert \mathbf{v} \rVert_2^{2} = \mathbf{v}^{T}\mathbf{v}$:
$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{i \in C_k} \left( \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \right)^{T} \left( \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \right)$$

**Step 2 — Expand the inner product** using distributivity:
$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{i \in C_k} \left[ \mathbf{x}^{(i)T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - 2\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{T}\boldsymbol{\mu}_k \right]$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate** $J$ w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ for a *fixed* cluster assignment $C_k$. The first term does not depend on $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$, so:
$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\mu}_k} = \sum_{i \in C_k} \left[ -2\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + 2\boldsymbol{\mu}_k \right] = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve** for $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$:
$$\sum_{i \in C_k} \boldsymbol{\mu}_k = \sum_{i \in C_k} \mathbf{x}^{(i)} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \vert C_k \vert \cdot \boldsymbol{\mu}_k = \sum_{i \in C_k} \mathbf{x}^{(i)} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boldsymbol{\mu}_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{i \in C_k} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$$

**Conclusion:** The optimal centroid for a fixed cluster assignment is the **arithmetic mean** of the points in that cluster. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 K-Means Convergence Proof (Sketch)

Define $J^{(t)} = J(\mathcal{C}^{(t)}, \boldsymbol{\mu}^{(t)})$ at iteration $t$.

- **Assignment step:** For fixed $\boldsymbol{\mu}^{(t)}$, we choose $\mathcal{C}^{(t+1)}$ that minimizes $J$, so $J(\mathcal{C}^{(t+1)}, \boldsymbol{\mu}^{(t)}) \leq J^{(t)}$.
- **Update step:** For fixed $\mathcal{C}^{(t+1)}$, we proved the optimal $\boldsymbol{\mu}^{(t+1)}$ is the mean, so $J(\mathcal{C}^{(t+1)}, \boldsymbol{\mu}^{(t+1)}) \leq J(\mathcal{C}^{(t+1)}, \boldsymbol{\mu}^{(t)})$.
- Combining: $J^{(t+1)} \leq J^{(t)}$.
- Since $J \geq 0$ and there are finitely many possible partitions ($K^N$), the sequence $\{J^{(t)}\}$ converges in a finite number of steps. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — K-Means (Hand-Traceable for the Exam)

**Dataset:** $\mathcal{D} = \{A(1,1),\; B(1,2),\; C(2,1),\; D(8,8),\; E(9,9),\; F(8,9)\}$

**Hyperparameters:** $K = 2$, **Initial centroids** $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(0)} = (1, 1)$ (point A), $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(0)} = (8, 8)$ (point D).

#### Iteration 1 — Assignment Step

For each point, compute Euclidean distance to both centroids and assign to the nearer.

Point A(1,1):
$$d(A, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1) = \sqrt{(1-1)^2 + (1-1)^2} = 0$$
$$d(A, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2) = \sqrt{(1-8)^2 + (1-8)^2} = \sqrt{49+49} = \sqrt{98} \approx 9.90$$
Assignment: **Cluster 1**.

Point B(1,2):
$$d(B, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1) = \sqrt{(1-1)^2 + (2-1)^2} = \sqrt{0+1} = 1.00$$
$$d(B, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2) = \sqrt{(1-8)^2 + (2-8)^2} = \sqrt{49+36} = \sqrt{85} \approx 9.22$$
Assignment: **Cluster 1**.

Point C(2,1):
$$d(C, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1) = \sqrt{(2-1)^2 + (1-1)^2} = \sqrt{1+0} = 1.00$$
$$d(C, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2) = \sqrt{(2-8)^2 + (1-8)^2} = \sqrt{36+49} = \sqrt{85} \approx 9.22$$
Assignment: **Cluster 1**.

Point D(8,8):
$$d(D, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1) = \sqrt{(8-1)^2 + (8-1)^2} = \sqrt{49+49} = \sqrt{98} \approx 9.90$$
$$d(D, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2) = \sqrt{(8-8)^2 + (8-8)^2} = 0$$
Assignment: **Cluster 2**.

Point E(9,9):
$$d(E, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1) = \sqrt{(9-1)^2 + (9-1)^2} = \sqrt{64+64} = \sqrt{128} \approx 11.31$$
$$d(E, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2) = \sqrt{(9-8)^2 + (9-8)^2} = \sqrt{1+1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$$
Assignment: **Cluster 2**.

Point F(8,9):
$$d(F, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1) = \sqrt{(8-1)^2 + (9-1)^2} = \sqrt{49+64} = \sqrt{113} \approx 10.63$$
$$d(F, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2) = \sqrt{(8-8)^2 + (9-8)^2} = \sqrt{0+1} = 1.00$$
Assignment: **Cluster 2**.

**Iteration 1 Assignment Summary:** $C_1^{(1)} = \{A, B, C\}$, $C_2^{(1)} = \{D, E, F\}$.

#### Iteration 1 — Update Step

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(1)} = \left( \frac{1+1+2}{3},\; \frac{1+2+1}{3} \right) = \left( \frac{4}{3},\; \frac{4}{3} \right) \approx (1.33,\; 1.33)$$

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(1)} = \left( \frac{8+9+8}{3},\; \frac{8+9+9}{3} \right) = \left( \frac{25}{3},\; \frac{26}{3} \right) \approx (8.33,\; 8.67)$$

#### Iteration 2 — Assignment Step (using updated centroids)

Point A(1,1):
$$d(A, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(1-1.33)^2 + (1-1.33)^2} = \sqrt{0.11+0.11} = \sqrt{0.22} \approx 0.47$$
$$d(A, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(1-8.33)^2 + (1-8.67)^2} = \sqrt{53.73+58.83} = \sqrt{112.56} \approx 10.61$$
Assignment: **Cluster 1**.

Point B(1,2):
$$d(B, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(1-1.33)^2 + (2-1.33)^2} = \sqrt{0.11+0.45} = \sqrt{0.56} \approx 0.75$$
$$d(B, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(1-8.33)^2 + (2-8.67)^2} = \sqrt{53.73+44.49} = \sqrt{98.22} \approx 9.91$$
Assignment: **Cluster 1**.

Point C(2,1):
$$d(C, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(2-1.33)^2 + (1-1.33)^2} = \sqrt{0.45+0.11} = \sqrt{0.56} \approx 0.75$$
$$d(C, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(2-8.33)^2 + (1-8.67)^2} = \sqrt{40.07+58.83} = \sqrt{98.90} \approx 9.94$$
Assignment: **Cluster 1**.

Point D(8,8):
$$d(D, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(8-1.33)^2 + (8-1.33)^2} = \sqrt{44.49+44.49} = \sqrt{88.98} \approx 9.43$$
$$d(D, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(8-8.33)^2 + (8-8.67)^2} = \sqrt{0.11+0.45} = \sqrt{0.56} \approx 0.75$$
Assignment: **Cluster 2**.

Point E(9,9):
$$d(E, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(9-1.33)^2 + (9-1.33)^2} = \sqrt{58.83+58.83} = \sqrt{117.66} \approx 10.85$$
$$d(E, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(9-8.33)^2 + (9-8.67)^2} = \sqrt{0.45+0.11} = \sqrt{0.56} \approx 0.75$$
Assignment: **Cluster 2**.

Point F(8,9):
$$d(F, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(8-1.33)^2 + (9-1.33)^2} = \sqrt{44.49+58.83} = \sqrt{103.32} \approx 10.17$$
$$d(F, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(1)}) = \sqrt{(8-8.33)^2 + (9-8.67)^2} = \sqrt{0.11+0.11} = \sqrt{0.22} \approx 0.47$$
Assignment: **Cluster 2**.

**Iteration 2 Assignment Summary:** $C_1^{(2)} = \{A, B, C\}$ (unchanged), $C_2^{(2)} = \{D, E, F\}$ (unchanged).

#### Iteration 2 — Update Step

Since the cluster assignments are **identical** to Iteration 1, the new centroids will be **identical** to $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(1)}$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(1)}$.

#### Convergence Check

Assignments $\equiv$ previous iteration. **Algorithm converges.**

#### Final Result

$$\boxed{C_1^* = \{A(1,1),\, B(1,2),\, C(2,1)\}, \quad \boldsymbol{\mu}_1^* = (1.33, 1.33)}$$

$$\boxed{C_2^* = \{D(8,8),\, E(9,9),\, F(8,9)\}, \quad \boldsymbol{\mu}_2^* = (8.33, 8.67)}$$

#### Final WCSS Calculation

$$J^* = \sum_{i \in C_1^*} \lVert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_1^* \rVert_2^{2} + \sum_{i \in C_2^*} \lVert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2^* \rVert_2^{2}$$

For $C_1^*$:
$$J_1 = (0.47)^2 + (0.75)^2 + (0.75)^2 = 0.22 + 0.56 + 0.56 = 1.34$$

For $C_2^*$:
$$J_2 = (0.75)^2 + (0.75)^2 + (0.47)^2 = 0.56 + 0.56 + 0.22 = 1.34$$

Total $J^* = 1.34 + 1.34 = 2.68$.

### 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example — KNN Classification

**Training Set (6 points, 2 classes):**

| Class | Points |
|---|---|
| Red (+) | $R_1(1,1)$, $R_2(1,2)$, $R_3(2,1)$ |
| Blue (×) | $B_1(8,8)$, $B_2(9,8)$, $B_3(8,9)$ |

**Query point:** $\mathbf{x}_q = (2, 2)$, **$K = 3$**, **Metric:** Euclidean.

#### Step 1 — Compute all pairwise Euclidean distances

$$d(\mathbf{x}_q, R_1) = \sqrt{(2-1)^2 + (2-1)^2} = \sqrt{1+1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \quad (\text{Red})$$

$$d(\mathbf{x}_q, R_2) = \sqrt{(2-1)^2 + (2-2)^2} = \sqrt{1+0} = 1.000 \quad (\text{Red})$$

$$d(\mathbf{x}_q, R_3) = \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (2-1)^2} = \sqrt{0+1} = 1.000 \quad (\text{Red})$$

$$d(\mathbf{x}_q, B_1) = \sqrt{(2-8)^2 + (2-8)^2} = \sqrt{36+36} = \sqrt{72} \approx 8.485 \quad (\text{Blue})$$

$$d(\mathbf{x}_q, B_2) = \sqrt{(2-9)^2 + (2-8)^2} = \sqrt{49+36} = \sqrt{85} \approx 9.220 \quad (\text{Blue})$$

$$d(\mathbf{x}_q, B_3) = \sqrt{(2-8)^2 + (2-9)^2} = \sqrt{36+49} = \sqrt{85} \approx 9.220 \quad (\text{Blue})$$

#### Step 2 — Sort by ascending distance and pick top $K = 3$

| Rank | Point | Distance | Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_2(1,2)$ | 1.000 | Red |
| 2 | $R_3(2,1)$ | 1.000 | Red |
| 3 | $R_1(1,1)$ | 1.414 | Red |
| 4 | $B_1(8,8)$ | 8.485 | Blue |
| 5 | $B_2(9,8)$ | 9.220 | Blue |
| 6 | $B_3(8,9)$ | 9.220 | Blue |

#### Step 3 — Majority Vote

Among the top $K = 3$ neighbors: **3 Red, 0 Blue**.

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_c \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_3} \mathbb{1}[y^{(i)} = c] = \text{Red}$$

#### Final Answer

$$\boxed{\text{The query point } (2,2) \text{ is classified as RED with } K = 3.}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision-Boundary Insight:** If $K = 5$ were chosen, the answer would **still be Red** (3 Red vs 2 Blue). KNN is **locally consistent** here because the Red class is well-clustered and far from the query's true boundary.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (K-Means vs KNN)

| Criterion | K-Means | KNN |
|---|---|---|
| Algorithmic Paradigm | Iterative, model-based | Instance-based, lazy |
| Optimization | Minimizes WCSS $J$ | No explicit training objective |
| Memory | $O(K \cdot d)$ | $O(N \cdot d)$ |
| Real-time Suitability (Robotics) | Yes (fast query) | Slow unless indexed |
| Determinism | Non-deterministic (init) | Deterministic (given data) |
| Online Learning | Possible (mini-batch) | Trivial (just store) |
| Handles Outliers | Poor (pulls centroid) | Robust with median metric |
| Curse of Dimensionality | Affected | Severely affected |

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 K-Means Algorithmic Flow (Lloyd's Algorithm)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start]) --> initA[Input dataset D with N points]
    initA --> initB[Choose K and initialize K centroids mu1 to muK]
    initB --> iterA{Iteration t: Assignment Step}
    iterA -->|For each point xi| calcDist[Compute Euclidean distance d_xi_mu_j for all j]
    calcDist --> assign[Assign xi to nearest cluster C_k star]
    assign --> checkAll{All points assigned?}
    checkAll -->|No| calcDist
    checkAll -->|Yes| update[Update Step: Recompute mu_k as mean of points in C_k]
    update --> converge{Are centroids stable or max iterations reached?}
    converge -->|No| iterA
    converge -->|Yes| out[Output final clusters and centroids]
    out --> stop([End])
```

### 4.2 K-Means Iteration State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InitializeCentroids
    InitializeCentroids --> AssignPoints
    AssignPoints --> UpdateCentroids
    UpdateCentroids --> CheckConvergence
    CheckConvergence --> AssignPoints : Not Converged
    CheckConvergence --> FinalClusters : Converged
    FinalClusters --> [*]
```

### 4.3 KNN Inference Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qStart([Query Point xq Arrives]) --> loadD[Load Training Database D of N points]
    loadD --> chooseK[Select K and distance metric d]
    chooseK --> computeLoop[For each training point xi, compute d xq, xi]
    computeLoop --> sortD[Sort all N distances in ascending order]
    sortD --> pickTop[Select top K points as N_K of xq]
    pickTop --> vote{Mode: Classification or Mean: Regression}
    vote -->|Classification| majority[Majority vote over labels in N_K]
    vote -->|Regression| average[Average the K target values]
    majority --> predict[Predicted class y_hat]
    average --> predict
    predict --> qEnd([Output y_hat])
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SensorLayer [SENSOR INPUT LAYER]
        cam[RGB Camera]

    end
    cam --> denoise

    imu --> denoise
```

### 4.5 Conceptual Decision Tree — Choosing K-Means vs KNN

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root{Is labelled training data available?}
    root -->|Yes, supervised task| sup[Use KNN Classifier / Regressor]
    root -->|No, unsupervised task| unsup{Goal: discover structure or detect anomalies?}
    unsup -->|Discover groupings| clu[Use K-Means Clustering]
    unsup -->|Estimate density or find similar past states| neigh[Use KNN Density / Retrieval]
    clu --> q1{K known a priori?}
    q1 -->|Yes| kmrun[Run K-Means with K-Means++ init]
    q1 -->|No| elb[Apply Elbow or Silhouette to choose K]
```

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
Differentiate between **supervised** and **unsupervised learning**. Give one robotics application where unsupervised learning is preferred over supervised learning. (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
- **Supervised Learning:** The model is trained on input–output pairs $(\mathbf{x}, y)$ to learn a mapping $f: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$. A labelled supervisory signal guides the optimization of a loss function $\mathcal{L}(f(\mathbf{x}), y)$. Example: classifying a robot's grasped object as "cup" or "bottle" using labelled images.
- **Unsupervised Learning:** The model receives only inputs $\mathbf{x}$ with no labels, and discovers latent structure (clusters, densities, or low-dimensional manifolds) by optimizing a self-supervised objective such as WCSS or reconstruction error.
- **Robotics Application:** **Topological SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)** — a robot exploring an unknown indoor environment has no pre-labelled map, and must **autonomously cluster LIDAR scans** into distinct rooms/corridors using K-Means. This is a clear case where unsupervised learning is mandatory because human-labelled maps are unavailable in real time.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
List and briefly explain the **two main steps** of the K-Means algorithm. What is the role of the **centroid**? (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
- **Step 1 — Assignment:** For each data point $\mathbf{x}^{(i)}$, compute its Euclidean distance to every centroid $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ and assign it to the cluster whose centroid is nearest, i.e., $\arg\min_k \lVert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \rVert_2$.
- **Step 2 — Update:** For each cluster $C_k$, recompute its centroid as the arithmetic mean of all points currently assigned to it: $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k \leftarrow \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{i \in C_k} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$.
- **Role of the Centroid:** The centroid $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ is the **representative point** of cluster $C_k$. It serves two functions: (a) it acts as a **prototype** summarizing the cluster's mean location, and (b) it acts as the **reference** against which all points are compared during the assignment step. Mathematically, it is the unique minimizer of the WCSS objective for a fixed assignment (as derived in Section 3.1).

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **K-Means clustering algorithm**. Define the objective function it minimizes and derive the centroid update rule. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Flowchart:** (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram — Lloyd's algorithm loop.)

**Algorithm Steps (textual form):**
1. Choose $K$ and initialize $K$ centroids $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(0)}, \dots, \boldsymbol{\mu}_K^{(0)}$ (e.g., via K-Means++).
2. **Repeat** until convergence:
   - **Assignment:** $C_k^{(t)} = \left\{ \mathbf{x}^{(i)} : \lVert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t)} \rVert_2^{2} \leq \lVert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j^{(t)} \rVert_2^{2} \;\; \forall j \right\}$
   - **Update:** $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{i \in C_k^{(t)}} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$
3. Return $\{C_1, \dots, C_K\}$ and $\{\boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \dots, \boldsymbol{\mu}_K\}$.

**Objective Function (Within-Cluster Sum of Squares):**
$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in C_k} \lVert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \rVert_2^{2}$$

**Derivation of Centroid Update Rule:**

We minimize $J$ w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ with the cluster assignment $C_k$ held fixed. Expanding:
$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{i \in C_k} \left[ \mathbf{x}^{(i)T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - 2\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{T}\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{T}\boldsymbol{\mu}_k \right]$$

Since only the last two terms inside the bracket depend on $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$:
$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\mu}_k} = \sum_{i \in C_k} \left[ -2\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + 2\boldsymbol{\mu}_k \right] = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \vert C_k \vert \cdot \boldsymbol{\mu}_k = \sum_{i \in C_k} \mathbf{x}^{(i)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boldsymbol{\mu}_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{i \in C_k} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$$

This proves the centroid must equal the arithmetic mean of points in its cluster. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Consider the dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(1,1),\; (1,2),\; (2,1),\; (8,8),\; (9,9),\; (8,9)\}$. Apply K-Means with $K = 2$ and initial centroids $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = (1,1)$, $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (8,8)$. Show **two full iterations** and verify convergence. Compute the final WCSS. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

(See Section 3.3 for the exhaustive trace. The key results to present in the exam are:)

**Iteration 1 Assignment:**
- $C_1^{(1)} = \{(1,1),\; (1,2),\; (2,1)\}$
- $C_2^{(1)} = \{(8,8),\; (9,9),\; (8,9)\}$

**Iteration 1 Update:**
$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(1)} = \left(\frac{1+1+2}{3},\; \frac{1+2+1}{3}\right) = (1.33,\; 1.33)$$
$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(1)} = \left(\frac{8+9+8}{3},\; \frac{8+9+9}{3}\right) = (8.33,\; 8.67)$$

**Iteration 2 Assignment:** (Using updated centroids, recompute distances — Section 3.3 shows the table.)
- $C_1^{(2)} = \{(1,1),\; (1,2),\; (2,1)\}$ — **unchanged**
- $C_2^{(2)} = \{(8,8),\; (9,9),\; (8,9)\}$ — **unchanged**

**Convergence Verified:** Since cluster assignments did not change, the algorithm has converged.

**Final Centroids:** $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^* = (1.33,\; 1.33)$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^* = (8.33,\; 8.67)$.

**Final WCSS Computation:**
$$J^* = J_1 + J_2 = 1.34 + 1.34 = 2.68$$

where $J_1 = (0.47)^2 + (0.75)^2 + (0.75)^2 = 1.34$ and $J_2 = (0.75)^2 + (0.75)^2 + (0.47)^2 = 1.34$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Alternative to Question A)*

**(a)** Explain the **K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)** algorithm. State the **distance metrics** commonly used and explain the **majority voting** decision rule. Why is KNN called a **lazy learner**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**KNN Algorithm:**
1. Store the entire training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
2. Given a query $\mathbf{x}_q$, compute the distance $d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}^{(i)})$ to every training point.
3. Sort the distances and select the $K$ smallest.
4. Predict the class label by **majority vote** (classification) or **average** (regression) over these $K$ neighbors.

**Common Distance Metrics:**
- **Euclidean ($L_2$):** $d = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_j - y_j)^2}$
- **Manhattan ($L_1$):** $d = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \lvert x_j - y_j \rvert$
- **Minkowski ($L_p$):** $d = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{d} \lvert x_j - y_j \rvert^{p} \right)^{1/p}$, which generalizes both.
- **Cosine Distance:** $d = 1 - \frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\lVert \mathbf{x} \rVert \lVert \mathbf{y} \rVert}$ — used when direction matters more than magnitude.

**Majority Voting Decision Rule:**
$$\hat{y} = \underset{c \in \mathcal{Y}}{\arg\max} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_K(\mathbf{x}_q)} \mathbb{1}\!\left[ y^{(i)} = c \right]$$

The predicted class is the one with the highest count among the $K$ nearest neighbors' labels.

**Why is KNN a "Lazy Learner"?**
KNN performs **no explicit training phase** — it simply memorizes the training data and defers all computation to **query time**. There is no model $f(\mathbf{x}; \theta)$ to fit; the "model" *is* the dataset itself. This contrasts with **eager learners** (e.g., neural networks, decision trees) which build a parametric model during training. The cost trade-off is **zero training time but high query time** $O(N \cdot d)$. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Using the training points Red $\{(1,1), (1,2), (2,1)\}$ and Blue $\{(8,8), (9,8), (8,9)\}$, classify the query point $(2,2)$ using **KNN with $K = 3$** and **Euclidean distance**. Show all distance computations and the voting step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute all six Euclidean distances from $\mathbf{x}_q = (2,2)$:**

| # | Training Point | Distance Calculation | Distance | Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(1,1)$ | $\sqrt{(2-1)^2 + (2-1)^2} = \sqrt{2}$ | 1.414 | Red |
| 2 | $(1,2)$ | $\sqrt{(2-1)^2 + (2-2)^2} = \sqrt{1}$ | 1.000 | Red |
| 3 | $(2,1)$ | $\sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (2-1)^2} = \sqrt{1}$ | 1.000 | Red |
| 4 | $(8,8)$ | $\sqrt{(2-8)^2 + (2-8)^2} = \sqrt{72}$ | 8.485 | Blue |
| 5 | $(9,8)$ | $\sqrt{(2-9)^2 + (2-8)^2} = \sqrt{85}$ | 9.220 | Blue |
| 6 | $(8,9)$ | $\sqrt{(2-8)^2 + (2-9)^2} = \sqrt{85}$ | 9.220 | Blue |

**Step 2 — Sort and pick the top $K = 3$ neighbors:**

The three smallest distances are: $(1,2) \to 1.000$ (Red), $(2,1) \to 1.000$ (Red), $(1,1) \to 1.414$ (Red).

**Step 3 — Majority Vote:**
$$\sum \mathbb{1}[\text{Red}] = 3, \quad \sum \mathbb{1}[\text{Blue}] = 0$$

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max(3, 0) = \text{Red}$$

**Final Classification:** $\boxed{(2,2) \text{ is classified as RED with } K = 3.}$ **(7 marks)**
