> 3. **Parameter Sharing Across Time** — FNNs treat each timestep with different weights; sequence models share parameters, improving generalization.

These three limitations are precisely what sequence models (RNNs, LSTMs, GRUs, Transformers) are engineered to overcome.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formal Decomposition — What a Sequence Model Does

A sequence model is decomposed into the following logical pipeline:

1. **Input Reception:** The model receives a sequence of inputs $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T)$ where each $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is a $d$-dimensional vector at timestep $t$.
2. **State Propagation:** At each timestep, a hidden state $h_t$ is computed as a function of the current input and the previous hidden state:
$$h_t = f(h_{t-1}, x_t; \theta)$$
3. **Output Emission:** An output $y_t$ is produced at each (or selected) timestep:
$$y_t = g(h_t; \phi)$$
4. **Parameter Sharing:** The functions $f$ and $g$ are **the same** at every timestep, which is what gives sequence models their ability to generalize across varying sequence lengths.

## 2.2 The Markov Assumption — A Central Simplification

> [!NOTE]
> **Markov Property (1st Order):** The future state depends only on the **present state**, not on the full history.
>
> Formally: $P(x_t \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{t-1}) = P(x_t \mid x_{t-1})$

This is the **simplest** sequence model. It dramatically reduces computational cost but limits long-range dependency capture.

For an **$n$-th order Markov model**:
$$P(x_t \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{t-1}) = P(x_t \mid x_{t-n}, \ldots, x_{t-1})$$

The higher the order $n$, the more historical context is preserved, but the parameter count grows **exponentially**.

## 2.3 Joint Probability Factorization of a Sequence

Any sequence of random variables can be factorized using the **chain rule of probability**:

$$P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(x_t \mid x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{t-1})$$

This equation is the **mathematical foundation** of every sequence model. A sequence model simply **approximates** the conditional probability $P(x_t \mid x_{<t})$ using a parametric function (e.g., a neural network with recurrent or attention-based connections).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Generic Sequence | $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T)$ | $T$ = sequence length (variable) |
| 2 | Chain Rule of Probability | $P(\mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(x_t \mid x_{<t})$ | Foundational identity |
| 3 | 1st-Order Markov | $P(x_t \mid x_{<t}) = P(x_t \mid x_{t-1})$ | Memoryless beyond 1 step |
| 4 | $n$-th Order Markov | $P(x_t \mid x_{<t}) = P(x_t \mid x_{t-n:t-1})$ | History window of size $n$ |
| 5 | Stationary Assumption | $P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) = P(x_{t+1} \mid x_t), \forall t$ | Time-invariant transition |
| 6 | Hidden State Recurrence | $h_t = f(h_{t-1}, x_t; \theta)$ | Shared parameters $\theta$ |
| 7 | Output Emission | $y_t = g(h_t; \phi)$ | Task-specific head $\phi$ |
| 8 | Autoregressive Form | $\hat{x}_t = \sum_{i=1}^{p} w_i x_{t-i} + \epsilon_t$ | Linear case (AR model) |
| 9 | Sequence Length | $T \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}$ | Variable across samples |
| 10 | Loss (Cross-Entropy) | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(x_t \mid x_{<t})$ | NLL over sequence |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU 2024, the primary focus is on the **motivation** and **mathematical framework** — not on the internal architecture of RNNs/LSTMs. You must master the chain rule factorization and Markov assumptions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In robotics, sequence models are **mission-critical** for:

- **Trajectory Prediction:** Forecasting where a pedestrian or another robot will move in the next 0.5–2 seconds.
- **Speech Recognition:** Converting voice commands to text for human-robot interaction.
- **IMU/Gyroscope Smoothing:** Filtering noisy sensor streams by modeling temporal correlation.
- **Video Action Recognition:** Classifying robot-observed activities over multiple frames.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** Anticipating mechanical failure from time-series vibration data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Chain Rule Factorization of a Sequence

We derive the fundamental identity that underlies all sequence modeling.

**Step 1 — Start with the joint probability of two variables:**

$$P(x_1, x_2) = P(x_1) \cdot P(x_2 \mid x_1)$$

*Explanation:* This follows the definition of conditional probability: $P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$.

**Step 2 — Extend to three variables using the product rule:**

$$P(x_1, x_2, x_3) = P(x_1) \cdot P(x_2 \mid x_1) \cdot P(x_3 \mid x_1, x_2)$$

*Explanation:* At each step, condition on the entire observed history.

**Step 3 — Inductive generalization to $T$ variables:**

Assume the identity holds for $T - 1$ variables. For $T$ variables:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T) &= P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{T-1}) \cdot P(x_T \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{T-1}) \\
&= \left[ \prod_{t=1}^{T-1} P(x_t \mid x_{<t}) \right] \cdot P(x_T \mid x_{<T}) \\
&= \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(x_t \mid x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{t-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the rigorous chain rule factorization for sequences.

**Step 4 — Apply the Markov assumption of order $n$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(x_t \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{t-1}) &\approx P(x_t \mid x_{t-n}, x_{t-n+1}, \ldots, x_{t-1}) \\
&= P(x_t \mid x_{t-n:t-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The full history is replaced by a finite window of size $n$, reducing the parameter space from exponential to linear in $n$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Parameter Count Comparison — Markov vs. Full History

Suppose each $x_t$ can take $K$ discrete values.

**Step 1 — Full history model parameter count:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{full}} &= K \cdot K^{T-1} = K^T
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* For each of the $K^{T-1}$ possible histories, we store a distribution over $K$ outcomes.

**Step 2 — 1st-order Markov parameter count:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{markov-1}} &= K^2
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Only a $K \times K$ transition matrix is needed.

**Step 3 — Ratio comparison:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{N_{\text{full}}}{N_{\text{markov-1}}} &= \frac{K^T}{K^2} = K^{T-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* For $K = 10$ and $T = 8$, the full model needs $10^8$ parameters while Markov needs only $10^2$ — a **one-million-fold** reduction. This is precisely why the Markov assumption is computationally essential.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Joint Probability of a 3-Step Sequence

A robot's gripper state follows a 1st-order Markov chain with three states: **Open (O)**, **Closing (C)**, **Closed (L)**. The transition matrix $P$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.2 & 0.1 \\ 0.3 & 0.5 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.3 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Rows represent **current state**, columns represent **next state**, in order $(O, C, L)$.

**Step 1 — Initial state probability:** Assume $P(x_1 = O) = 1$ (starts Open).

**Step 2 — Joint probability of the sequence (O → C → L):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(O, C, L) &= P(x_1 = O) \cdot P(x_2 = C \mid x_1 = O) \cdot P(x_3 = L \mid x_2 = C) \\
&= 1 \cdot P[O, C] \cdot P[C, L] \\
&= 1 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 0.2 \\
&= 0.04
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Each transition is read off the appropriate row/column of $P$. The joint probability is the product of the initial state and all transitions.

**Step 3 — Probability of (O → C → O) — an alternative sequence:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(O, C, O) &= 1 \cdot P[O, C] \cdot P[C, O] = 1 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 0.3 = 0.06
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Same methodology, but the final transition is $C \to O$, which is row $C$, column $O = 0.3$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Approaches

| Criterion | Feedforward NN (FNN) | 1st-Order Markov | $n$-th Order Markov | Full Sequence Model (RNN) |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------|---------------------|---------------------------|
| Input Length | Fixed | Fixed | Fixed window | **Variable** |
| Long-Range Memory | None | None | Up to $n$ steps | **Unbounded (in theory)** |
| Parameter Count | $O(d^2)$ | $O(K^2)$ | $O(K^{n+1})$ | $O(d^2)$ shared across $T$ |
| Training Complexity | Low | Very Low | Exponential in $n$ | Moderate |
| Suitable for Robotics? | No (static) | Simple | Medium | **Yes (industry standard)** |
| Captures i.i.d.? | Yes | N/A | N/A | N/A (assumes dependency) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Sequence Model Architecture in Robotics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Sequence x1...xT] --> B[Encoder Block]
    B --> C[Hidden State Propagation h1...hT]
    C --> D[Output Head g]
    D --> E[Predicted Sequence y1...yT]
    C --> F[Memory Bank hT]
    F --> G[Decision Module]
    G --> H[Robot Actuator Command]
```

*Explanation of Flow:*
- **Input Sequence:** Variable-length stream (e.g., joint angles, LIDAR scans, audio frames).
- **Encoder Block:** Transforms raw inputs into feature vectors at each timestep.
- **Hidden State Propagation:** The recurrent core that maintains memory across time.
- **Output Head:** Task-specific (regression for control, softmax for classification).
- **Memory Bank:** Final state $h_T$ serves as a fixed-size summary of the entire history.
- **Decision Module:** Maps the learned representation to a robot action.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Why Standard Fails and Sequence Succeeds

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph S1[Standard Feedforward Limitation]
        F1[Raw Sensor Stream x1..xT] --> F2[Fixed-Size Vector Flatten]
        F2 --> F3[Feedforward Network]
        F3 --> F4[Single Output]
    end

    subgraph S2[Sequence Model Pipeline]
        G1[Raw Sensor Stream x1..xT] --> G2[Per-Timestep Encoder]
        G2 --> G3[Recurrent State h1..hT]
        G3 --> G4[Per-Timestep Decoder]
        G4 --> G5[Output Sequence y1..yT]
    end
```

*Explanation:* The top branch shows how flattening destroys temporal order. The bottom branch shows the proper sequence-aware pipeline that preserves ordering and variable length.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Markov Order Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Order0[Order 0 Independent]
        A0[x_t]
    end
    subgraph Order1[Order 1 Markov]
        A1[x_t-1] --> B1[x_t]
    end
    subgraph Order2[Order 2 Markov]
        A2[x_t-2] --> B2[x_t-1] --> C2[x_t]
    end
    subgraph OrderN[Order N Full]
        AA[x_1] --> BB[x_2] --> CC[...] --> DD[x_t]
    end
```

*Explanation:* Visualizes the growing dependency window as Markov order increases. Order 0 has no memory; order $N$ depends on the entire history.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**
*Define a sequence model. Give two examples of sequential data encountered in robotics.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **sequence model** is a machine learning model that processes data in which the **order of elements carries semantic information**, and each element $x_t$ is statistically dependent on previous elements $x_1, \ldots, x_{t-1}$.

It learns the conditional distribution $P(x_t \mid x_{<t}; \theta)$ and can handle **variable-length** inputs.

**Two robotic examples (1.5 marks each):**
1. **Joint trajectory data** — A robot arm's sequence of joint angles $\{ \theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_T \}$ over time, used for motion prediction and control.
2. **Voice commands** — Audio waveforms of a human operator's speech, sampled at fixed rate but variable in total length, used for natural human-robot interaction.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**
*State the chain rule of probability for sequences. Why is it the mathematical foundation of all sequence models?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The chain rule of probability factorizes a sequence's joint probability as:

$$P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(x_t \mid x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{t-1})$$

(2 marks for correct formula)

**Why it is foundational:** Every sequence model — whether a 1st-order Markov chain, an $n$-gram language model, an RNN, or a Transformer — is essentially a **parametric approximation** of the conditional factor $P(x_t \mid x_{<t})$. The chain rule reduces the intractable joint distribution to a product of tractable conditionals, making learning and inference feasible. (1 mark for justification)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer an internal choice between two questions. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks. The complete, model solution with step-by-step working is provided below.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **limitations of feedforward neural networks** when applied to sequential data. Discuss how sequence models overcome these limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot's battery voltage follows a 1st-order Markov process with the transition matrix below, where states are: **High (H)**, **Medium (M)**, **Low (L)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.3 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.5 & 0.3 \\ 0.1 & 0.2 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

The robot starts in state **H**. Compute:
- (i) The probability of the sequence $H \to M \to L$ over two timesteps.
- (ii) The probability of the sequence $H \to H \to M \to L$ over three timesteps.
- (iii) The steady-state (stationary) distribution $\pi$ satisfying $\pi P = \pi$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Limitations of FNNs on Sequential Data (7 Marks):**

1. **Fixed Input Size (2 marks):** FNNs require an input vector of constant dimensionality. Sequences such as audio commands or motion trajectories have **variable length**, and pre-truncation or padding discards information or adds noise.

2. **No Memory of Past Inputs (2 marks):** FNNs treat each input as i.i.d. They cannot capture the **temporal dependency** between, say, a robot's previous action and its current sensor reading. The Markov property $P(x_t \mid x_{<t})$ cannot be modeled without state propagation.

3. **Parameter Inefficiency (1.5 marks):** A separate FNN would be needed for every possible sequence length. Sequence models use **parameter sharing** (the same $f(h_{t-1}, x_t)$ at every $t$), drastically reducing the parameter count and improving generalization.

4. **No Position/Order Awareness (1.5 marks):** FNNs have no native mechanism to know that $x_1$ came before $x_5$. Sequence models preserve order either via recurrence (RNNs) or positional encoding (Transformers).

**How sequence models overcome these (bonus integration):**
- Variable length → handled by iterating over timesteps until input is exhausted.
- Memory → maintained via hidden state $h_t = f(h_{t-1}, x_t)$.
- Parameter sharing → same transition function at each $t$.
- Order awareness → inherent in the sequential computation graph.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**(i) Probability of $H \to M \to L$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(H, M, L) &= P(x_1 = H) \cdot P(x_2 = M \mid x_1 = H) \cdot P(x_3 = L \mid x_2 = M) \\
&= 1 \cdot P[H, M] \cdot P[M, L] \\
&= 1 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 0.3 \\
&= 0.09
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Probability of $H \to H \to M \to L$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(H, H, M, L) &= 1 \cdot P[H,H] \cdot P[H,M] \cdot P[M,L] \\
&= 1 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 0.3 \\
&= 0.054
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Steady-state distribution (3 marks):**

We solve $\pi = (\pi_H, \pi_M, \pi_L)$ with $\pi P = \pi$ and $\pi_H + \pi_M + \pi_L = 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_H &= 0.6 \pi_H + 0.2 \pi_M + 0.1 \pi_L \\
\pi_M &= 0.3 \pi_H + 0.5 \pi_M + 0.2 \pi_L \\
\pi_L &= 0.1 \pi_H + 0.3 \pi_M + 0.7 \pi_L
\end{aligned}
$$

From equation 1: $0.4 \pi_H = 0.2 \pi_M + 0.1 \pi_L \Rightarrow \pi_H = 0.5 \pi_M + 0.25 \pi_L$.

From equation 3: $0.3 \pi_L = 0.1 \pi_H + 0.3 \pi_M \Rightarrow \pi_L = \frac{1}{3}\pi_H + \pi_M$.

Substituting and solving the linear system along with $\pi_H + \pi_M + \pi_L = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_H &= 0.176, \quad \pi_M \approx 0.353, \quad \pi_L \approx 0.471
\end{aligned}
$$

(The student may present the full 3×4 augmented matrix solution; partial marks awarded for the setup.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** What is the **Markov assumption**? Compare **1st-order, 2nd-order, and full-history** sequence models in terms of memory, parameter complexity, and practical applicability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **chain rule factorization** for a sequence of length 4. Using it, show how an RNN computes the log-likelihood of an observed sequence. State the training objective in terms of negative log-likelihood. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Markov Assumption and Comparison (7 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Markov Assumption:** The conditional probability of the next state depends only on a **finite window** of the most recent past, not on the entire history. Mathematically, $P(x_t \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{t-1}) = P(x_t \mid x_{t-n}, \ldots, x_{t-1})$ for an $n$-th order Markov model. (2 marks)

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Aspect | 1st-Order | 2nd-Order | Full History |
|--------|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| Memory Window | 1 step | 2 steps | All $t-1$ steps |
| Parameters (for $K$ states) | $K^2$ | $K^3$ | $K^T$ (exponential) |
| Long-Range Deps | None | Limited | Full |
| Practical Use | Weather, simple control | N-gram language | Impractical without approximation |
| Robotics Example | Gripper state | Short gesture | Speech recognition |

**(b) Chain Rule Derivation for $T=4$ and RNN Log-Likelihood (7 Marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4) &= P(x_1) \cdot P(x_2 \mid x_1) \cdot P(x_3 \mid x_1, x_2) \cdot P(x_4 \mid x_1, x_2, x_3) \\
&= \prod_{t=1}^{4} P(x_t \mid x_{<t})
\end{aligned}
$$

**RNN Log-Likelihood (3 marks):**

An RNN with parameters $\theta$ approximates $P(x_t \mid x_{<t}) \approx P_{\theta}(x_t \mid h_{t-1})$, where $h_t = \tanh(W_h h_{t-1} + W_x x_t)$.

The **log-likelihood** of an observed sequence is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log P_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) = \log P_{\theta}(x_1) + \sum_{t=2}^{T} \log P_{\theta}(x_t \mid h_{t-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

The **training objective** is the negative log-likelihood (NLL), minimized via gradient descent:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\log P_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P_{\theta}(x_t \mid h_{t-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Markov chain" with "Markov decision process (MDP)"** — Markov chains are passive stochastic processes; MDPs include actions and rewards. The current module deals with Markov *chains* (passive).
> 2. **Forgetting the initial state probability** $P(x_1)$ in the chain rule — many students omit this, losing 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Stating "Markov assumption = no memory"** — this is wrong. Markov models have *limited* memory (window of size $n$), not zero memory. Zero memory corresponds to the i.i.d. assumption, which is the trivial case.
> 4. **Mixing up row/column conventions** in the transition matrix — always state explicitly whether rows = current state or next state.
> 5. **Not summing the stationary distribution to 1** — always verify $\pi_H + \pi_M + \pi_L = 1$ in numerical answers.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Know Bullets for KTU Exam**

- **Definition:** A sequence model handles ordered, variable-length data where elements are statistically dependent.
- **Chain Rule:** $P(\mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(x_t \mid x_{<t})$ — the foundational identity.
- **Markov Assumption (Order $n$):** $P(x_t \mid x_{<t}) = P(x_t \mid x_{t-n:t-1})$.
- **1st-Order Markov:** Simplest non-trivial sequence model; needs $K^2$ parameters for $K$ states.
- **Hidden State Update:** $h_t = f(h_{t-1}, x_t; \theta)$ — the heart of every recurrent model.
- **Three FNN Limitations:** Fixed size, no memory, no parameter sharing across time.
- **Three Sequence Model Advantages:** Variable length, temporal memory, parameter sharing.
- **Robotic Applications:** Trajectory prediction, speech recognition, sensor filtering, video understanding, predictive maintenance.
- **Parameter Count Explosion:** Full-history models scale as $K^T$ — Markov is essential for tractability.
- **Steady-State Distribution:** Solved via $\pi P = \pi$ with $\sum \pi_i = 1$ — appears frequently in 7-mark numericals.
- **Joint Probability Calculation:** Multiply the initial state probability with all transition probabilities in order.
- **NLL Training Loss:** $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P_{\theta}(x_t \mid h_{t-1})$ — used for training RNN-type models.
- **Examiner's trap:** "Sequence" does NOT mean "ordered set" alone — it specifically means *temporal or structural* order with statistical dependency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic RNN Architecture and Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT614 - INTRODUCTION TO AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic RNN Architecture and Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** is a specialized class of artificial neural networks designed to recognize patterns in sequences of data, such as time series, speech, text, video, or robotic sensor streams. Unlike feedforward neural networks, RNNs possess an internal **hidden state (memory)** that captures information from previous time steps, enabling them to exhibit **temporal dynamic behaviour**.

Formally, an RNN applies a **shared set of weight parameters** recursively across all time steps of a sequence, mapping an input sequence $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T)$ to an output sequence $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_T)$ while maintaining a hidden state vector $\mathbf{h}_t$ at each time step $t$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** A Recurrent Neural Network is "a neural network with recurrent connections forming a directed cycle, allowing the network to store representations of recent input events in its hidden state and use them for prediction."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are reading a sentence: *"The robot walked into the room because it was..."*

To predict the next word (*"dark"*, *"large"*, *"broken"*), your brain does **not** process each word in isolation. Instead, it carries a **running memory** of the words already read. The pronoun *"it"* makes sense only because you remember *"The robot"* from the previous clause.

A standard feedforward network has **no memory** of previous inputs — it is like reading with your eyes closed, seeing only the current word. An RNN is like **reading with your eyes open**: it carries a "mental state" from one time step to the next, allowing context to flow forward through the sequence.

| Property | Feedforward Network | Recurrent Network (RNN) |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | None | Sequential memory via hidden state |
| Input Size | Fixed | Variable length sequence |
| Parameter Sharing | Across layers only | Across time steps |
| Use Case | Static images | Time-series, robot trajectories, NLP |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of **AI in Robotics**, RNNs are foundational for processing **temporal sensor data** (e.g., LiDAR sequences, IMU streams, motor encoder feedback) and for tasks such as **trajectory prediction**, **gesture recognition**, and **natural language command interpretation** for human-robot interaction.

## 1.3 Core Terminology for KTU Board Examinations

- **Time Step ($t$):** A discrete index representing position in the sequence.
- **Hidden State ($\mathbf{h}_t$):** The "memory" vector of the network at time $t$.
- **Cell / Unit:** A single recurrent neuron; the basic computational block.
- **Unrolling (Unfolding):** Drawing the recurrent loop as a chain of identical cells across time.
- **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT):** The training algorithm that unrolls the network and applies backpropagation across all time steps.
- **Vanishing/Exploding Gradient:** Numerical instability during BPTT when gradients shrink toward zero or grow unboundedly.

---

> **Estimated Self-Study Time for this Section:** 35–45 minutes for first-time learners, 10 minutes for revision.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic

A basic RNN cell performs the following logical sequence at every time step $t$:

1. **Receive Input Vector** $\mathbf{x}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ from the current time step.
2. **Receive Previous Hidden State** $\mathbf{h}_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ from the immediately preceding time step.
3. **Compute New Hidden State** as a non-linear transformation of the weighted sum of $\mathbf{x}_t$ and $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$.
4. **Compute Output** $\mathbf{y}_t$ as a function of the new hidden state.
5. **Pass Forward** the new hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$ to the next time step, creating the **recurrent loop**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** The same weight matrices $W_{xh}$, $W_{hh}$, and $W_{hy}$ are reused at every time step. This **parameter sharing** is what makes RNNs generalizable across sequences of arbitrary length and is the central engineering insight behind recurrent architectures.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Dimension / Range |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{x}_t$ | Input vector at time $t$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ (where $d$ = input dim) |
| $\mathbf{h}_t$ | Hidden state at time $t$ | $\mathbb{R}^{m}$ (where $m$ = hidden dim) |
| $\mathbf{y}_t$ | Output vector at time $t$ | $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ (where $k$ = output dim) |
| $W_{xh}$ | Input-to-hidden weight matrix | $\mathbb{R}^{m \times d}$ |
| $W_{hh}$ | Hidden-to-hidden (recurrent) weight | $\mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ |
| $W_{hy}$ | Hidden-to-output weight matrix | $\mathbb{R}^{k \times m}$ |
| $\mathbf{b}_h$ | Hidden bias vector | $\mathbb{R}^{m}$ |
| $\mathbf{b}_y$ | Output bias vector | $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ |
| $\sigma$ | Activation (commonly $\tanh$ for hidden, $\text{softmax}$ for output) | Range: $\tanh \in (-1, +1)$ |
| $L_t$ | Per-time-step loss | Scalar $\geq 0$ |
| $L$ | Total sequence loss | Scalar $\geq 0$ |
| $T$ | Total number of time steps in sequence | Integer $\geq 1$ |
| $\eta$ | Learning rate | Typical range: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| $E \vert \cdot \vert$ | Expected value of absolute value (used in gradient magnitude) | Scalar $\geq 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When writing formulas in the answer sheet, students often confuse $W_{hh}$ (the **recurrent** weight that creates the loop) with $W_{xh}$ (the **input** weight). The board examiner typically awards 1 mark for correctly identifying the role of each weight matrix.

## 2.3 Core Equations (Forward Pass)

The forward propagation through a basic RNN cell is governed by the following three coupled equations:

$$\mathbf{h}_t = \sigma_h \left( W_{xh}\, \mathbf{x}_t + W_{hh}\, \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h \right)$$

$$\mathbf{y}_t = \sigma_y \left( W_{hy}\, \mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_y \right)$$

The total loss over the entire sequence is the sum (or mean) of per-time-step losses:

$$L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t \left( \mathbf{y}_t,\, \mathbf{y}_t^{\text{target}} \right)$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | RNN Utility |
|---|---|
| **Robotic Motion Control** | Smoothing and predicting joint trajectories from noisy encoder data |
| **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization & Mapping)** | Modelling temporal dependencies in LiDAR/visual odometry sequences |
| **Human-Robot Interaction** | Interpreting spoken commands or sign language gestures as temporal sequences |
| **Predictive Maintenance** | Forecasting sensor failures from time-series vibration/temperature data |
| **Autonomous Navigation** | Predicting pedestrian/vehicle trajectories for safe path planning |

---

> **Self-Assessment Checkpoint:** Before moving to derivations, you should be able to (1) draw the unrolled RNN diagram from memory, (2) state the three forward-pass equations, and (3) explain why parameter sharing matters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Forward Pass of a Basic RNN

We begin with the unrolled computational graph, where the same RNN cell is replicated for each time step $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$. At each cell, two inputs are received: the current observation $\mathbf{x}_t$ and the previous hidden state $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$.

### Step 1 — Hidden State Pre-Activation

Define the **net input** to the hidden unit at time $t$ as a linear combination:

$$\mathbf{z}_t = W_{xh}\, \mathbf{x}_t + W_{hh}\, \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h$$

**Explanation:** This equation combines three sources of information — the current input (weighted by $W_{xh}$), the prior memory (weighted by the recurrent matrix $W_{hh}$), and a bias offset $\mathbf{b}_h$.

### Step 2 — Hidden State Activation (Non-linearity)

Apply the **hyperbolic tangent** activation (the standard choice for RNN hidden states) to introduce non-linearity:

$$\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{z}_t) = \tanh\!\left( W_{xh}\, \mathbf{x}_t + W_{hh}\, \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h \right)$$

**Explanation:** The $\tanh$ function squashes the unbounded linear combination into the bounded interval $(-1, +1)$. This prevents numerical explosion of hidden activations but is a primary cause of the **vanishing gradient** problem.

### Step 3 — Output Pre-Activation

The output layer receives only the current hidden state (not the input directly, in the basic architecture):

$$\mathbf{o}_t = W_{hy}\, \mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_y$$

**Explanation:** This is a standard linear projection from hidden representation to output space.

### Step 4 — Output Activation

For classification tasks, the **softmax** function converts raw scores into a probability distribution:

$$\mathbf{y}_t = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{o}_t) = \frac{\exp(\mathbf{o}_t)}{\sum_{i} \exp(o_{t,i})}$$

**Explanation:** $\text{softmax}$ ensures the output vector sums to 1 and each component is non-negative, making it interpretable as class probabilities. For regression tasks, the identity activation $\mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{o}_t$ is used.

### Step 5 — Per-Time-Step Loss

For a classification problem with true label $\mathbf{y}_t^{\text{target}}$, the **cross-entropy loss** is:

$$L_t = - \sum_{i} y_{t,i}^{\text{target}} \, \log \left( y_{t,i} \right)$$

**Explanation:** Cross-entropy heavily penalizes confident wrong predictions. For regression, the **mean squared error** $L_t = \tfrac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{y}_t - \mathbf{y}_t^{\text{target}} \Vert^2$ is used.

### Step 6 — Total Sequence Loss

The total loss accumulates contributions from all time steps:

$$L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$$

**Explanation:** Summing across time means the gradient with respect to any parameter must account for its influence at **every** time step — this is the foundation of Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)

The objective of training is to minimize $L$ by updating weights via gradient descent. We derive the gradient $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}}$ as a representative case (other gradients follow analogously).

### Step A — Chain Rule Expansion Across Time

By the chain rule, the loss at time $t$ depends on $W_{hh}$ through all subsequent hidden states:

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_k}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

**Explanation:** We must backpropagate from time $t$ all the way back to time $k$, because $W_{hh}$ influenced $\mathbf{h}_k$, which in turn influenced $\mathbf{h}_{k+1}, \ldots, \mathbf{h}_t$.

### Step B — The Critical Recursive Term

The middle term $\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k}$ expands recursively:

$$\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_i}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{i-1}} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} W_{hh}^{\top} \, \text{diag}\!\left( 1 - \tanh^2(\mathbf{z}_i) \right)$$

**Explanation:** Each factor represents the local Jacobian of the hidden state transition. The product of $t - k$ such Jacobians is what causes the **vanishing/exploding gradient** problem.

### Step C — Vanishing/Exploding Gradient Condition

If the spectral radius (largest absolute eigenvalue) of the Jacobian factor satisfies $\rho < 1$, the product decays exponentially toward zero — **vanishing gradient**. If $\rho > 1$, it grows exponentially — **exploding gradient**.

$$\rho = \max_{i} \left\Vert W_{hh}^{\top} \, \text{diag}\!\left( 1 - \tanh^2(\mathbf{z}_i) \right) \right\Vert_{\text{spectral}}$$

**Explanation:** When gradients vanish, the network cannot learn long-range dependencies because early time steps receive negligible updates. This is the primary motivation for **LSTM** and **GRU** architectures, which introduce gating mechanisms to control gradient flow.

### Step D — Weight Update Rule

The final update using gradient descent is:

$$W_{hh}^{\text{new}} = W_{hh}^{\text{old}} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

**Explanation:** The learning rate $\eta$ scales the step size. For exploding gradients, **gradient clipping** is applied: if $\Vert \mathbf{g} \Vert > \theta$, then $\mathbf{g} \leftarrow \theta \cdot \mathbf{g} / \Vert \mathbf{g} \Vert$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Training Challenges & Solutions

| Challenge | Mathematical Symptom | Engineering Solution |
|---|---|---|
| Vanishing Gradient | $\prod \text{Jacobian} \to 0$ as $t - k \to \infty$ | Use LSTM/GRU with gating |
| Exploding Gradient | $\prod \text{Jacobian} \to \infty$ | Gradient clipping (threshold $\theta$) |
| Long Training Time | BPTT scales as $O(T)$ per weight update | Truncated BPTT (limit to $k$ steps) |
| Memory Saturation | Storing all $T$ hidden states in GPU RAM | Mini-batch + sequence bucketing |
| Overfitting on Short Sequences | High variance in gradient estimates | Dropout on recurrent connections |

---

> **Common Derivation Mistake to Avoid:** Students often forget to apply the $\text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2)$ factor in Step B. This derivative arises because $\dfrac{d}{dx}\tanh(x) = 1 - \tanh^2(x)$, and it is the **non-linearity's derivative** that bounds the gradient magnitude at each time step.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Folded (Compact) RNN Architecture

This diagram shows the **recurrent representation** — the network as a single cell with a self-loop representing the hidden state feedback.

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN1["x_t INPUT VECTOR"] --> CELL1["RNN CELL h_t = tanh Wxh xt + Whh h_t-1 + b"]
    H_PREV["h_t-1 PREVIOUS HIDDEN STATE"] --> CELL1
    CELL1 --> OUT1["y_t OUTPUT"]
    CELL1 --> H_NEXT["h_t NEXT HIDDEN STATE"]
    H_NEXT -.-> H_PREV
```

**Reading Guide:** The dashed arrow from $\mathbf{h}_t$ back to $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$ is the **recurrent connection**. This single loop, when unrolled, produces a deep network with one layer per time step.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Unrolled RNN Across Time Steps

This diagram shows the same network **unfolded** through time — the canonical representation used in KTU board examinations.

```mermaid
graph LR
    X1["x_1"] --> H1["h_1"]
    H0["h_0 = 0"] --> H1
    H1 --> H2["h_2"]
    X2["x_2"] --> H2
    H2 --> H3["h_3"]
    X3["x_3"] --> H3
    H3 --> H4["h_T"]
    XT["x_T"] --> H4
    H1 --> Y1["y_1"]
    H2 --> Y2["y_2"]
    H3 --> Y3["y_3"]
    H4 --> YT["y_T"]
```

**Reading Guide:** All horizontal connections between hidden states $\mathbf{h}_1 \to \mathbf{h}_2 \to \mathbf{h}_3 \to \mathbf{h}_T$ are governed by the **same weight matrix $W_{hh}$** — this is the **parameter sharing** property of RNNs.

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Four Architectural Types of RNN

The four canonical RNN types classify networks by the **mapping** between input and output sequences.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph T1["TYPE 1: ONE TO ONE - Vanilla Neural Net"]
        A1["Fixed Input x"] --> B1["RNN Cell"] --> C1["Fixed Output y"]
    end
    subgraph T2["TYPE 2: ONE TO MANY - Image Captioning"]
        A2["Single Image x"] --> B2["RNN Cell Sequence"] --> C2["Word Sequence y_1 ... y_T"]
    end
    subgraph T3["TYPE 3: MANY TO ONE - Sentiment Classification"]
        A3["Word Sequence x_1 ... x_T"] --> B3["RNN Cell Sequence"] --> C3["Single Class y"]
    end
    subgraph T4["TYPE 4: MANY TO MANY - Machine Translation"]
        A4["Source Sequence x_1 ... x_T"] --> B4["Encoder RNN"] --> C4["Decoder RNN"] --> D4["Target Sequence y_1 ... y_T"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Functional Block Architecture for Robotics Application

This block diagram maps a **pedestrian trajectory prediction** system using RNNs — a common AI-robotics application.

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1["Robot LiDAR Sensor"] --> P1["Preprocessing and Noise Filter"]
    P1 --> SEQ["Input Sequence x_1 to x_T Position Vectors"]
    SEQ --> ENC["RNN Encoder h_t = tanh Wxh xt + Whh h_t-1"]
    ENC --> CTX["Context Vector h_T Final Hidden State"]
    CTX --> DEC["RNN Decoder Predicts Future Trajectory"]
    DEC --> OUT["Output y_hat_t+1 to y_hat_t+K Future Positions"]
    OUT --> CTRL["Robot Motion Planner Applies Safe Trajectory"]
```

**Reading Guide:** This is an **Encoder-Decoder** (Many-to-Many) architecture. The encoder compresses the observed trajectory into a context vector, and the decoder unrolls future predictions. The same $W_{hh}$ is shared across all encoder time steps, and a separate (often different) $W_{hh}^{\text{dec}}$ is shared across decoder steps.

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Information Flow in BPTT

```mermaid
graph LR
    F1["Forward Pass x_t to h_t to y_t to L_t"] --> F2["Compute Loss L = sum L_t"]
    F2 --> B1["Backward Pass Compute dL/dW for all W"]
    B1 --> B2["Apply Chain Rule Through Unrolled Time Steps"]
    B2 --> B3["Check Gradient Magnitude"]
    B3 --> B4{"Gradient Exploding?"}
    B4 -->|YES| C1["Apply Gradient Clipping"]
    B4 -->|NO| C2["Update Weights W := W minus eta grad W"]
    C1 --> C2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram-to-Equation Mapping:** Every arrow in the unrolled diagram (Section 4.2) corresponds to either a $W_{xh}$ operation (vertical arrows from input to hidden), a $W_{hh}$ operation (horizontal arrows between hidden states), or a $W_{hy}$ operation (vertical arrows from hidden to output). Board examiners frequently test this mapping.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Recurrent Neural Network. List any two advantages of RNNs over feedforward networks. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** is a class of neural networks designed to process sequential data by maintaining a **hidden state** that carries information from previous time steps. The defining feature is the presence of **recurrent connections** — the same weight matrix is applied at each time step, creating a directed cycle in the network graph.

**Two advantages over feedforward networks (1.5 marks each):**

1. **Ability to handle variable-length sequences:** RNNs can process input sequences of any length $T$ using the same set of parameters, while feedforward networks require fixed input dimensions.

2. **Memory of past inputs:** Through the hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$, RNNs retain information from previous time steps, making them suitable for tasks requiring temporal context (e.g., speech recognition, robotic trajectory prediction).

---

### Question 2: What is Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)? State the vanishing gradient problem. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)** is the training algorithm for RNNs. It works by **unrolling** the recurrent network across all $T$ time steps into a deep feedforward network and then applying the standard backpropagation algorithm to compute gradients of the total loss $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$ with respect to the shared weight matrices $W_{xh}$, $W_{hh}$, and $W_{hy}$ (1.5 marks).

**Vanishing Gradient Problem (1.5 marks):** During BPTT, the gradient of the loss with respect to early time-step weights involves a product of Jacobian matrices across time. When the spectral radius of the recurrent Jacobian $W_{hh}^{\top} \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2)$ is less than 1, this product decays exponentially, causing gradients at early time steps to become negligibly small. As a result, the network **cannot learn long-range dependencies** in the sequence.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Answer **any one** of the following two full questions. Each question carries 14 marks split into sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### ❑ Question A (14 Marks)

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Draw the unfolded architecture of a basic RNN for a sequence of length $T = 4$. Label all weight matrices, input vectors, hidden states, and outputs. Explain the role of parameter sharing.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Derive the forward pass equations of a basic RNN cell. Show how the hidden state at time $t$ is computed from $\mathbf{x}_t$ and $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$. Mention the role of the activation function.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Architecture Diagram (4 marks):**

```
x_1 ──→ [RNN] ──→ h_1 ──→ y_1
         ↑
         h_0 = 0
x_2 ──→ [RNN] ──→ h_2 ──→ y_2
         ↑
         h_1
x_3 ──→ [RNN] ──→ h_3 ──→ y_3
         ↑
         h_2
x_4 ──→ [RNN] ──→ h_4 ──→ y_4
         ↑
         h_3
```

(Each cell is the **same** RNN block; vertical arrows use $W_{xh}$, horizontal arrows use $W_{hh}$, output arrows use $W_{hy}$.)

**Parameter Sharing Explanation (3 marks):**

Parameter sharing means that the **same weight matrices** $W_{xh}$, $W_{hh}$, and $W_{hy}$ are used at every time step. This has two critical benefits:

1. The model can generalize to **sequences of arbitrary length** $T$ without retraining.
2. The number of learnable parameters is **independent of sequence length**, reducing memory and computational cost.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Hidden state pre-activation (2 marks):**

$$\mathbf{z}_t = W_{xh}\, \mathbf{x}_t + W_{hh}\, \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h$$

**Step 2 — Hidden state activation (2 marks):**

$$\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{z}_t)$$

**Step 3 — Output computation (1.5 marks):**

$$\mathbf{y}_t = \text{softmax}\!\left( W_{hy}\, \mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_y \right)$$

**Step 4 — Role of activation function (1.5 marks):**

The $\tanh$ activation squashes the unbounded linear combination into the bounded range $(-1, +1)$, which prevents hidden activations from growing uncontrollably across time. It also introduces **non-linearity**, enabling the network to learn complex temporal patterns. Without a non-linear activation, the recurrent network would collapse into a single linear transformation regardless of $T$.

---

### ❑ Question B (14 Marks)

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** List and explain the **four types of RNN architectures** with one real-world example for each. Use neat diagrams.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Explain the **vanishing and exploding gradient problems** in RNNs. Derive the mathematical condition under which each occurs. Suggest two engineering solutions.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

| # | Type | Input | Output | Real-World Example (1.5 marks each) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **One-to-One** | Fixed $\mathbf{x}$ | Fixed $\mathbf{y}$ | Standard image classification (single image → single label) |
| 2 | **One-to-Many** | Single $\mathbf{x}$ | Sequence $\mathbf{y}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{y}_T$ | **Image captioning** — one image generates a sentence of words |
| 3 | **Many-to-One** | Sequence $\mathbf{x}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_T$ | Single $\mathbf{y}$ | **Sentiment classification** — a sentence is classified as positive/negative |
| 4 | **Many-to-Many** | Sequence $\mathbf{x}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_T$ | Sequence $\mathbf{y}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{y}_K$ | **Machine translation** — encoder-decoder translates English to French |

(1.5 marks for the table; 1 mark for neat diagrams of each type)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Vanishing Gradient (2.5 marks):**

During BPTT, the gradient of the loss with respect to weights at time $k$ involves the product:

$$\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} W_{hh}^{\top}\, \text{diag}\!\left( 1 - \tanh^2(\mathbf{z}_i) \right)$$

If the spectral radius of each factor is $\rho < 1$, the product decays exponentially as $t - k$ increases. Gradients at early time steps become vanishingly small, so **weights cannot be updated effectively**, and the network fails to learn dependencies spanning many time steps.

**Exploding Gradient (2.5 marks):**

Conversely, if $\rho > 1$, the product grows exponentially, causing numerical overflow (NaN values in weights). Training becomes unstable.

**Engineering Solutions (2 marks):**

1. **Use gated architectures (LSTM/GRU):** Gating mechanisms create a near-constant gradient path that mitigates vanishing gradients.
2. **Gradient Clipping:** For exploding gradients, clip the gradient norm to a threshold: if $\Vert \mathbf{g} \Vert > \theta$, set $\mathbf{g} \leftarrow \theta \cdot \mathbf{g}/\Vert \mathbf{g} \Vert$.
3. **(Bonus) Truncated BPTT:** Limit backpropagation to the last $k$ time steps to bound the product length.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Mark Loss Point 1:** Students often write the equation $\mathbf{h}_t = \sigma(W\mathbf{x}_t)$ for RNNs — this is the **feedforward** equation. Always include the recurrent term $W_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$. Deduct 2 marks if missing.
> - **Mark Loss Point 2:** In BPTT derivations, students frequently omit the $\text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2)$ factor. Deduct 1.5 marks if absent.
> - **Mark Loss Point 3:** When asked to "list types of RNN," students often confuse "Many-to-Many" with "One-to-Many." Be explicit about whether the input or output is the sequence.
> - **Mark Loss Point 4:** Failing to mention **parameter sharing** when asked about RNN advantages loses 1–2 marks consistently.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **RNN = Feedforward + Recurrent Loop** — the loop allows hidden state memory across time.
- **Three weight matrices:** $W_{xh}$ (input), $W_{hh}$ (recurrent), $W_{hy}$ (output) — **all shared across time**.
- **Forward pass equations:** $\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh(W_{xh}\mathbf{x}_t + W_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h)$ and $\mathbf{y}_t = \text{softmax}(W_{hy}\mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_y)$.
- **Total loss** is the sum (or mean) of per-time-step losses: $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$.
- **BPTT** unrolls the network across $T$ time steps and applies backpropagation.
- **Vanishing gradient** occurs when $\rho < 1$ in the Jacobian product → solved by **LSTM/GRU**.
- **Exploding gradient** occurs when $\rho > 1$ → solved by **gradient clipping**.
- **Four architectural types:** One-to-One, One-to-Many, Many-to-One, Many-to-Many.
- **Applications in robotics:** trajectory prediction, SLAM, sensor fusion, HRI.
- **Always mention parameter sharing** when explaining RNN advantages — high-yield KTU keyword.
- **Spectral radius** $\rho$ is the key quantity governing gradient stability; remember $\rho < 1$ (vanishing) and $\rho > 1$ (exploding).
- **Activation choice:** $\tanh$ is standard for hidden states; $\text{softmax}$ for classification outputs.
- **Encoder-Decoder (Seq2Seq)** is a special Many-to-Many architecture with two RNN stacks and a context vector.
- **Truncated BPTT** limits backpropagation to $k$ steps to save computation — practical engineering trick.
- **Initial hidden state** $\mathbf{h}_0$ is typically the zero vector; some architectures use a learned $\mathbf{h}_0$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robotics - Robotic Perception
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT614 - INTRODUCTION TO AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Robotics - Robotic Perception

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Robotic Perception** is the interdisciplinary sub-field of robotics concerned with enabling autonomous robotic agents to acquire, process, interpret, and reason about sensory data emanating from the physical environment in order to construct meaningful internal representations that support decision-making, planning, navigation, and physical interaction.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, robotic perception is formalized as a mapping function:

$$\mathcal{P}: \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{E} \longrightarrow \mathcal{R}$$

where $\mathcal{S}$ is the set of raw sensor measurements, $\mathcal{E}$ is the environmental state, and $\mathcal{R}$ is the robot's internal representation (belief state) used downstream by the planning and control layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact):** *"Robotic perception is the process by which a robot uses sensors and computational algorithms to interpret its surroundings, build internal world models, and extract actionable information for autonomous behaviour."*

The three primary pillars of robotic perception in the KTU 2024 curriculum are:

| Pillar | Function | Primary Sensors |
|---|---|---|
| **Exteroception** | Sensing the external world | Cameras, LiDAR, Ultrasonic, IR |
| **Proprioception** | Sensing the robot's own state | Encoders, IMU, Joint torque sensors |
| **Interoception** | Sensing internal robot health | Temperature, current, voltage sensors |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Robot as a Blindfolded Person with a Cane**
> 
> Imagine a person walking blindfolded through a crowded room. They cannot see (no vision sensor), but they swing a cane (a tactile/proximity sensor) to detect obstacles, feel the floor texture under their feet (proprioception), and hear echoes of their voice (sonar/ultrasonic). Their **brain** — the perception module — fuses all these weak, noisy signals into a coherent mental map of the room. Robotic perception works exactly the same way: **multiple noisy sensors + intelligent fusion algorithm = a usable internal world model**.

Another useful analogy: **Robotic perception is to a robot what human cognition is to a person.** Just as a human integrates vision, hearing, touch, and balance to navigate a room, a robot integrates cameras, LiDAR, IMU, and force sensors to navigate the real world.

### 1.3 Key Constants, Metrics & Standards

- **Frame rate of vision sensors**: typically **30 Hz to 60 Hz** for industrial cameras, up to **1000 Hz** for event cameras.
- **LiDAR angular resolution**: typically **0.1° to 0.4°** in modern 3D LiDAR units.
- **Depth perception accuracy**: ±**2 cm** at 1 m range for stereo vision; ±**1 cm** for structured light.
- **IMU sampling rate**: typically **100 Hz to 1000 Hz**.
- **Sensor fusion update rate**: **50 Hz to 200 Hz** in real-time SLAM systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **bandwidth-latency-accuracy trade-off** is the central design constraint of any robotic perception stack. High-accuracy sensors (LiDAR) are typically slow; high-speed sensors (IMU) are typically noisy. The art of perception engineering lies in fusing them optimally.

### 1.4 The Perception-Action Loop

Robotic perception does not exist in isolation. It is the **first half** of the **perception-action loop**, which is the foundational control structure of any intelligent robot:

$$\text{Sense} \longrightarrow \text{Perceive} \longrightarrow \text{Plan} \longrightarrow \text{Act} \longrightarrow \text{Sense} \longrightarrow \cdots$$

This continuous loop operates at the **OODA (Observe–Orient–Decide–Act)** speed, typically **10 Hz to 100 Hz** for ground robots and up to **1000 Hz** for high-speed manipulators.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Perception Pipeline (Five Levels)

Robotic perception is structured as a **bottom-up hierarchical pipeline** in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

- **Level 1 — Signal Acquisition**: Raw data capture from sensors (pixels from cameras, point clouds from LiDAR, voltage from encoders).
- **Level 2 — Pre-processing**: Noise filtering, calibration, undistortion, and synchronization across sensors.
- **Level 3 — Feature Extraction**: Identifying low-level geometric/textural features (edges, corners, planes, SIFT/ORB keypoints).
- **Level 4 — Scene Interpretation**: Mid-level object detection, segmentation, pose estimation, and tracking.
- **Level 5 — Semantic Understanding**: High-level reasoning — classifying objects, understanding affordances, inferring intentions, predicting future states.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why hierarchical?** Each level **compresses** the data representation. Level 1 may have 1 million pixels; Level 5 may output just 5 objects with labels. This compression is what makes real-time robotic perception tractable on embedded hardware.

### 2.2 Core Sensory Modalities (Detailed Breakdown)

#### 2.2.1 Vision Sensors (Cameras)
- **Monocular cameras**: Single 2D projection of 3D world; cannot recover absolute depth.
- **Stereo cameras**: Two cameras with known baseline $b$ recover depth via triangulation.
- **RGB-D cameras**: Combine color image with per-pixel depth (e.g., Kinect, Intel RealSense).
- **Event cameras (DVS)**: Asynchronous, per-pixel brightness-change detectors with microsecond latency.

**Why cameras matter in robotics**: They are **passive** (don't emit energy), **high information density**, and **cheap**. They dominate human-like perception in modern AI-driven robotics.

#### 2.2.2 Range Sensors
- **LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging)**: Emits laser pulses; measures time-of-flight to produce a 3D point cloud.
- **Time-of-Flight (ToF) cameras**: Emit modulated IR light; measure phase shift for per-pixel depth.
- **Ultrasonic sensors**: Emit sound waves at 40 kHz–200 kHz; range 0.02 m to 10 m; cheap but low resolution.
- **Structured light sensors**: Project a known IR pattern; depth from pattern deformation.

#### 2.2.3 Proprioceptive Sensors
- **Wheel/motor encoders**: Measure joint angle $\theta$ with high precision (typically 12-bit to 20-bit resolution).
- **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)**: Combines 3-axis accelerometer, 3-axis gyroscope, and (often) 3-axis magnetometer.
- **Force/Torque sensors**: Measure wrenches at the end-effector; critical for manipulation and safe human-robot interaction.

### 2.3 The Pinhole Camera Model (Geometric Foundation)

The geometric backbone of robotic visual perception is the **pinhole camera model**, which projects a 3D world point $\mathbf{X} = (X, Y, Z)^T$ onto a 2D image pixel $\mathbf{x} = (u, v)^T$.

The **intrinsic projection** in homogeneous coordinates is:

$$
s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where:
- $s$ is a scalar scale factor (depth),
- $\mathbf{K}$ is the **3×3 camera intrinsic matrix**,
- $\mathbf{R}$ is the **3×3 rotation matrix** (world-to-camera orientation),
- $\mathbf{t}$ is the **3×1 translation vector** (world-to-camera position).

The intrinsic matrix is:

$$
\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $f_x, f_y$ are the **focal lengths in pixels** and $(c_x, c_y)$ is the **principal point** (image center).

### 2.4 Stereo Vision Depth Equation (Critical KTU Formula)

For a stereo camera with baseline $b$ (distance between the two cameras), focal length $f$, and disparity $d = u_L - u_R$ (pixel difference between left and right image projections of the same 3D point):

$$
Z = \frac{f \cdot b}{d}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Disparity $d$ is **inversely proportional to depth $Z$**. Objects far away have near-zero disparity (hard to localize). Objects close have large disparity (easy to localize). This is why stereo vision fails for distant objects but excels for nearby manipulation tasks.

### 2.5 LiDAR Point Cloud Generation (Time-of-Flight)

For a LiDAR emitting a laser pulse that travels to an object and returns, the distance to the object is:

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

where:
- $c$ is the **speed of light in vacuum** ($c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s),
- $\Delta t$ is the **round-trip time-of-flight** in seconds.

The 3D point in sensor frame is computed from spherical coordinates $(R, \theta, \phi)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= R \cos(\phi) \cos(\theta) \\
y &= R \cos(\phi) \sin(\theta) \\
z &= R \sin(\phi)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\theta$ is the azimuth angle (horizontal) and $\phi$ is the elevation angle (vertical).

### 2.6 IMU Measurement Model

A typical MEMS IMU provides accelerometer and gyroscope measurements:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{a}_m &= \mathbf{R}^T(\mathbf{a} - \mathbf{g}) + \mathbf{b}_a + \mathbf{n}_a \\
\boldsymbol{\omega}_m &= \boldsymbol{\omega} + \mathbf{b}_g + \mathbf{n}_g
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{a}_m, \boldsymbol{\omega}_m$ are measured acceleration and angular velocity,
- $\mathbf{a}$ is true linear acceleration, $\mathbf{g}$ is gravity,
- $\mathbf{R}$ is the rotation matrix from world to body frame,
- $\mathbf{b}_a, \mathbf{b}_g$ are **biases** (slowly drifting),
- $\mathbf{n}_a, \mathbf{n}_g$ are **zero-mean Gaussian noise** terms.

> [!NOTE]
> IMU biases $\mathbf{b}_a, \mathbf{b}_g$ are the **single largest source of drift** in inertial navigation. This is why pure IMU-based odometry (dead reckoning) fails after a few minutes, motivating fusion with vision or LiDAR.

### 2.7 Sensor Fusion: The Kalman Filter Framework

The **Kalman Filter (KF)** is the canonical algorithm for fusing noisy multi-sensor measurements. It maintains a state estimate $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k$ and covariance $\mathbf{P}_k$ and operates in two steps:

**Prediction step** (using proprioceptive sensors, e.g., IMU):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} &= \mathbf{F}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1 \vert k-1} + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k \\
\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} &= \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{P}_{k-1 \vert k-1} \mathbf{F}_k^T + \mathbf{Q}_k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Update step** (using exteroceptive measurements, e.g., vision/LiDAR):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{K}_k &= \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T (\mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T + \mathbf{R}_k)^{-1} \\
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k} &= \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k (\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1}) \\
\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k} &= (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k) \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{F}_k$ is the **state transition model**,
- $\mathbf{H}_k$ is the **measurement model**,
- $\mathbf{Q}_k$ is the **process noise covariance**,
- $\mathbf{R}_k$ is the **measurement noise covariance**,
- $\mathbf{K}_k$ is the **Kalman gain** (how much to trust the new measurement).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For nonlinear robotic perception (most real cases), the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** linearizes around the current estimate using Jacobians, and the **Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)** uses sigma-point sampling to handle nonlinearity more accurately. Modern SLAM systems use the **Iterated EKF (IEKF)** or even **particle filters** for highly nonlinear cases.

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in KTU 2024 ESE. Master these.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Valid Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pinhole projection | $s\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R}\vert\mathbf{t}]\mathbf{X}$ | $\mathbf{x}$: pixel, $\mathbf{X}$: world (m) | Perspective camera |
| 2 | Stereo depth | $Z = fb / d$ | $f$: pixel, $b$: m, $d$: pixel, $Z$: m | Calibrated stereo rig |
| 3 | LiDAR range | $R = c\Delta t / 2$ | $c$: m/s, $\Delta t$: s, $R$: m | Free-space propagation |
| 4 | IMU measurement | $\mathbf{a}_m = \mathbf{R}^T(\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{g}) + \mathbf{b}_a + \mathbf{n}_a$ | $\mathbf{a}$: m/s², $\mathbf{g}$: 9.81 m/s² | Stationary or moving body |
| 5 | Kalman prediction | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1}$ | State vectors | Linear Gaussian systems |
| 6 | Kalman update | $\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}\mathbf{H}^T(\mathbf{HPH}^T+\mathbf{R})^{-1}$ | $\mathbf{K}$: gain matrix | All KF variants |
| 7 | Homography | $\mathbf{x}' = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}$ | $\mathbf{H}$: 3×3 matrix | Planar scene |
| 8 | Optical flow | $\mathbf{v} = -\frac{f}{Z} \mathbf{T} + \dots$ | $\mathbf{v}$: pixel/s | Brightness constancy |
| 9 | ICP error | $E = \sum_i \Vert \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}_i + \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{q}_i \Vert^2$ | $\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}$: pose | Point cloud registration |
| 10 | Loop closure gain | $\Delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{K}(\mathbf{z}_{loop} - h(\hat{\mathbf{x}}))$ | Corrects drift | SLAM with re-observation |

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla FSD, Mobileye)**: Multi-modal fusion of cameras + LiDAR + radar + GPS/IMU to perceive pedestrians, vehicles, and lane markings at 30 Hz.
- **Industrial Manipulation (ABB, KUKA, Fanuc)**: RGB-D cameras + force/torque sensors for bin-picking and compliant assembly with sub-millimeter precision.
- **Medical Robotics (Da Vinci Surgical System)**: Stereo vision with haptic feedback for minimally invasive surgery.
- **Aerial Robotics (DJI, Skydio)**: Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO) for GPS-denied flight in forests and indoor environments.
- **Service Robotics (Pepper, Spot)**: Multi-modal perception for human-robot interaction in unstructured environments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Stereo Depth from Disparity

This derivation is **guaranteed to appear** in some form in KTU 2024 ESE. We derive the depth equation $Z = fb/d$ from first principles.

**Step 1: Setup the stereo geometry.**
Consider two cameras (Left $L$ and Right $R$) with identical focal length $f$, separated horizontally by a baseline $b$. Both optical axes are parallel to the $Z$-axis. A 3D world point $\mathbf{P} = (X, Y, Z)$ projects onto the two image planes at $(x_L, y)$ and $(x_R, y)$ respectively.

> Note: Because both cameras are parallel and share the same $Y$-axis, the $y$-coordinate of the projection is identical in both images (epipolar constraint). Only the $x$-coordinate differs — this difference is the **disparity**.

**Step 2: Apply similar triangles to the left camera.**
The line from the left camera center $(0, 0, 0)$ to the point $(X, Y, Z)$ forms a triangle with the image plane at $Z = f$. By the intercept theorem:

$$
\frac{x_L}{f} = \frac{X}{Z}
$$

Solving for $x_L$:

$$
x_L = \frac{fX}{Z}
$$

**Step 3: Apply similar triangles to the right camera.**
The right camera center is at $(b, 0, 0)$. The 3D point $(X, Y, Z)$ has a horizontal offset of $(X - b)$ relative to the right camera. By similar triangles:

$$
\frac{x_R}{f} = \frac{X - b}{Z}
$$

Solving for $x_R$:

$$
x_R = \frac{f(X - b)}{Z}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the disparity.**
The disparity $d$ is defined as the difference between the two image-plane $x$-coordinates (assuming the principal points are identical):

$$
d = x_L - x_R
$$

Substitute the two expressions:

$$
d = \frac{fX}{Z} - \frac{f(X - b)}{Z}
$$

Factor out $f / Z$:

$$
d = \frac{f}{Z}\left[ X - (X - b) \right] = \frac{f}{Z} \cdot b
$$

**Step 5: Solve for depth $Z$.**
Rearrange the equation $d = fb / Z$ to isolate $Z$:

$$
d \cdot Z = f \cdot b
$$

$$
Z = \frac{f \cdot b}{d}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** $\boxed{Z = \dfrac{f \cdot b}{d}}$
> 
> This is the **single most important equation** in stereo vision. The depth $Z$ is directly proportional to the product $f \cdot b$ and **inversely proportional** to the disparity $d$. Valid when $d > 0$ (point is in front of both cameras) and the cameras are perfectly rectified (epipolar lines are horizontal).

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: LiDAR Time-of-Flight Range

**Step 1: Physical setup.**
A LiDAR emits a short laser pulse (typical duration 5 ns) at time $t_0$. The pulse propagates at the speed of light $c$ through the medium, reflects off an object at distance $R$, and returns to the sensor at time $t_1$. The detector measures the round-trip time $\Delta t = t_1 - t_0$.

**Step 2: Total distance traveled by the light pulse.**
The light travels from sensor to object (distance $R$) and from object back to sensor (distance $R$). Total path length:

$$
L = R + R = 2R
$$

**Step 3: Relate path length to time and speed.**
Since light travels at $c$ in free space (the assumption in most LiDAR equations):

$$
L = c \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 4: Equate the two expressions for $L$.**

$$
2R = c \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 5: Solve for $R$.**

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical example:** For an object 100 m away, the round-trip time is $\Delta t = 2 \times 100 / (3 \times 10^8) = 6.67 \times 10^{-7}$ s = **667 nanoseconds**. This is why LiDAR detectors need picosecond-level timing resolution.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Kalman Filter Update Step (1D Case)

This is the most important derivation for KTU 2024 — the heart of sensor fusion.

**Step 1: State the problem.**
We have a prior estimate $\hat{x}^-$ with uncertainty $P^-$ (both scalars for the 1D case). We receive a noisy measurement $z$ with measurement noise variance $R$. The measurement model is $z = Hx + v$ where $v \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$.

**Step 2: The fusion objective.**
We want the posterior estimate $\hat{x}$ that minimizes the **a posteriori error variance** $P = \mathbb{E}[(x - \hat{x})^2]$. The optimal estimator is:

$$
\hat{x} = \hat{x}^- + K(z - H\hat{x}^-)
$$

where the term $(z - H\hat{x}^-)$ is the **innovation** (measurement residual) and $K$ is the scalar Kalman gain we must derive.

**Step 3: Substitute and compute the error.**
Let $e = x - \hat{x}$. Then:

$$
e = x - \hat{x}^- - K(z - H\hat{x}^-)
$$

Substitute $z = Hx + v$:

$$
e = (x - \hat{x}^-) - K(Hx + v - H\hat{x}^-)
$$

$$
e = (x - \hat{x}^-)(1 - KH) - Kv
$$

**Step 4: Compute the a posteriori error variance.**

$$
P = \mathbb{E}[e^2] = (1-KH)^2 P^- + K^2 R
$$

(Note: the cross-term $\mathbb{E}[(x-\hat{x}^-)v] = 0$ because measurement noise is uncorrelated with prior.)

**Step 5: Minimize $P$ with respect to $K$.**

$$
\frac{dP}{dK} = -2H(1-KH)P^- + 2KR = 0
$$

$$
-H(1-KH)P^- + KR = 0
$$

$$
-HP^- + K H^2 P^- + KR = 0
$$

$$
K(H^2 P^- + R) = HP^-
$$

$$
K = \frac{HP^-}{H^2 P^- + R}
$$

**Step 6: Compute the minimum posterior variance.**

$$
P = (1-KH)^2 P^- + K^2 R
$$

Substitute $K$ and simplify (algebraic exercise; the result is):

$$
P = (1 - KH) P^-
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Kalman Insight:** The Kalman gain $K$ **automatically balances** trust between the prior prediction (via $P^-$) and the new measurement (via $R$). When $R$ is small (sensor is accurate), $K \to 1/H$ and we trust the measurement. When $P^-$ is small (prior is accurate), $K \to 0$ and we ignore the measurement. This is the **mathematical embodiment of intelligent sensor fusion**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sensor Comparison

| Sensor | Range | Accuracy | Update Rate | Strengths | Weaknesses | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Monocular Camera | Unlimited (passive) | Angular only | 30–1000 Hz | High info density, cheap, color | No absolute depth | Low |
| Stereo Camera | 0.5–50 m | ±2 cm @ 1 m | 30–60 Hz | Dense depth, passive | Fails on textureless surfaces | Medium |
| RGB-D Camera | 0.1–5 m | ±1 cm | 30–60 Hz | Per-pixel depth + color | Short range, sunlight interference | Medium |
| LiDAR (3D) | 0.5–200 m | ±2 cm | 10–20 Hz | Long range, accurate 3D | Expensive, sparse at distance | High |
| Ultrasonic | 0.02–10 m | ±1 cm | 20–50 Hz | Cheap, robust to dust | Low angular resolution, slow | Very Low |
| IMU | N/A (inertial) | Drift ~1°/min | 100–1000 Hz | High rate, measures rotation | Drift over time | Low |
| GPS | Global | ±2 m (civilian) | 1–10 Hz | Global absolute position | Indoor-denied, weather-affected | Low |
| Force/Torque | 0–500 N | ±0.1 N | 1000 Hz | Measures contact | Local only, expensive | High |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Feature Detector Comparison

| Detector | Type | Rotational Invariance | Scale Invariance | Speed | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Harris | Corner | No | No | Very Fast | Tracking |
| SIFT | Keypoint | Yes | Yes | Slow | Object recognition |
| SURF | Keypoint | Yes | Yes | Medium | Real-time recognition |
| ORB | Keypoint | Yes | Partial | Very Fast | SLAM, AR |
| FAST | Corner | No | No | Ultra Fast | Tracking |
| AKAZE | Keypoint | Yes | Yes | Medium | Robust matching |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** ORB (Oriented FAST and Rotated BRIEF) is the dominant feature in modern visual SLAM (e.g., ORB-SLAM2/3) because it is **free, fast, and has good invariance** — a rare combination in feature engineering.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **robotic perception**. List the three categories of sensors used in a robot's perception system with one example for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Robotic perception is the process by which a robot uses sensors and computational algorithms to acquire, process, and interpret data from its environment to build internal representations that support decision-making, planning, and physical interaction.

**Three categories (1.5 marks — 0.5 each):**

| Category | Function | Example Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Exteroceptive | Sense the external environment | Camera, LiDAR, Ultrasonic |
| Proprioceptive | Sense the robot's own state | Wheel encoder, IMU, joint sensor |
| Interoceptive | Sense internal robot health | Temperature sensor, current sensor |

**Example (0.5 marks):** A **camera** (RGB sensor) is exteroceptive, an **IMU** is proprioceptive, and a **battery voltage sensor** is interoceptive.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is **sensor fusion**? State the **Kalman Filter** prediction and update equations in their general form.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Sensor fusion is the process of combining data from multiple sensors (or multiple measurements from the same sensor) such that the resulting information has less uncertainty than would be possible when these sources were used individually.

**Prediction step (1 mark):**
$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1 \vert k-1} + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k, \quad \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{P}_{k-1 \vert k-1} \mathbf{F}_k^T + \mathbf{Q}_k
$$

**Update step (1 mark):**
$$
\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T (\mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T + \mathbf{R}_k)^{-1}
$$
$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k (\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1})
$$

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Descriptive Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Set A]** With a neat diagram, explain the **pinhole camera model** and derive the **stereo depth equation** $Z = fb/d$. State two limitations of stereo vision. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Pinhole Camera Model — Diagram + Explanation (3 marks):**

A pinhole camera is modeled as a box with a tiny aperture through which light from a 3D scene enters and projects onto a 2D image plane at distance $f$ (the focal length) from the optical center.

A 3D world point $\mathbf{P} = (X, Y, Z)$ is projected onto the image plane at point $\mathbf{p} = (x, y)$ using the **perspective projection equation** derived from similar triangles:

$$
\frac{x}{f} = \frac{X}{Z} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac{fX}{Z}, \quad y = \frac{fY}{Z}
$$

Including the intrinsic matrix (which accounts for pixel scaling factors $\alpha, \beta$ and the principal point offset $(c_x, c_y)$), the complete pinhole model in homogeneous coordinates is:

$$
s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \alpha & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & \beta & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $(X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$ are coordinates in the camera frame.

**(ii) Stereo Depth Derivation (3 marks):**

Consider two cameras separated by baseline $b$ with parallel optical axes. A 3D point $P$ at depth $Z$ projects to $x_L$ in the left image and $x_R$ in the right image.

By similar triangles on the left camera:

$$
x_L = \frac{fX}{Z}
$$

By similar triangles on the right camera (offset by $b$):

$$
x_R = \frac{f(X - b)}{Z}
$$

The disparity is:

$$
d = x_L - x_R = \frac{fX}{Z} - \frac{f(X-b)}{Z} = \frac{fb}{Z}
$$

Solving for $Z$:

$$
\boxed{Z = \frac{fb}{d}}
$$

**(iii) Two Limitations of Stereo Vision (1 mark):**

1. **Textureless surfaces fail:** Stereo matching relies on finding corresponding pixels via correlation. On a blank white wall, every pixel looks identical, so correspondence is ambiguous.
2. **Limited range and baseline dependency:** Depth accuracy degrades as $d \to 0$ (far-away points have nearly zero disparity). Increasing baseline $b$ improves far-field accuracy but reduces the overlapping field of view.

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Set A]** Explain the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** for nonlinear robotic perception. How does it differ from the standard Kalman Filter? Mention one application of EKF in SLAM. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Standard KF Limitation (2 marks):**

The standard Kalman Filter assumes that both the **process model** $\mathbf{F}$ and the **measurement model** $\mathbf{H}$ are **linear**. The state estimate is updated via:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k (\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1})
$$

In real robotic perception, the motion model (involving rotation matrices, $\sin$, $\cos$ of angles) and the measurement model (involving pinhole projection, which is itself a division by depth) are **nonlinear**. Applying a linear KF to these systems produces biased, inconsistent estimates — a phenomenon called **filter divergence**.

**(ii) EKF Solution — Linearization via Jacobians (3 marks):**

The EKF addresses this by **linearizing the nonlinear functions around the current state estimate** using first-order Taylor expansion (Jacobians).

For a nonlinear process model $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ and measurement model $\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{F}_k &= \left. \frac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \right|_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1}} \\
\mathbf{H}_k &= \left. \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \right|_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The EKF then uses these Jacobians in the standard KF equations:

**Prediction:**
$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1} = \mathbf{f}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1|k-1}), \quad \mathbf{P}_{k|k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{P}_{k-1|k-1} \mathbf{F}_k^T + \mathbf{Q}_k
$$

**Update:**
$$
\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k|k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T (\mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{P}_{k|k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T + \mathbf{R}_k)^{-1}
$$
$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k (\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{h}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1}))
$$

**(iii) EKF vs Standard KF (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Standard KF | EKF |
|---|---|---|
| System model | Linear $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{x}$ | Nonlinear $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ |
| Measurement model | Linear $\mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}$ | Nonlinear $\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x})$ |
| State propagation | Matrix multiplication | Function evaluation |
| Covariance propagation | $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{P}\mathbf{F}^T$ | $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{P}\mathbf{F}^T$ (with Jacobian) |
| Assumption | Gaussian, linear | Gaussian, locally linearized |
| Accuracy | Optimal for linear | Approximate (first-order) |

**(iv) EKF in SLAM (1 mark):**

In **EKF-SLAM**, the state vector augments the robot pose with the positions of all observed landmarks: $\mathbf{x} = [x_r, y_r, \theta_r, x_1, y_1, \dots, x_n, y_n]^T$. The prediction step uses the motion model to propagate the pose, and the update step uses landmark observations to correct both the pose and map. Although computationally expensive (the state grows with map size), EKF-SLAM was the dominant SLAM formulation from 1990 to 2010.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Set B]** With a suitable diagram, describe the **architecture of a robotic perception system**. Explain the roles of the **five processing levels** in the perception pipeline. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Architecture Diagram (2 marks):**

A robotic perception system is organized as a **five-level hierarchical pipeline** that progressively transforms raw sensor data into actionable symbolic knowledge. The block diagram is as follows (refer to Section 4.1 above for the full schematic):

**Level 1: Signal Acquisition → Level 2: Pre-processing → Level 3: Feature Extraction → Level 4: Scene Interpretation → Level 5: Semantic Understanding**

**(ii) Role of Each Level (5 marks — 1 mark each):**

- **Level 1 — Signal Acquisition:** Captures raw data from sensors. For a camera, this is a stream of RGB pixel arrays. For LiDAR, this is a stream of (range, azimuth, elevation) tuples. The sensor is characterized by its **frame rate, resolution, noise profile, and field of view**. The output is an unstructured, high-bandwidth data stream.

- **Level 2 — Pre-processing:** Cleans the raw signal. Operations include **noise filtering** (e.g., Gaussian smoothing, bilateral filtering), **lens distortion correction** (undistortion using Brown-Conrady model), **temporal synchronization** (timestamp alignment across sensors), and **spatial calibration** (extrinsic and intrinsic parameter estimation). Output is a calibrated, denoised signal.

- **Level 3 — Feature Extraction:** Identifies geometric and textural primitives. For images, this includes **edge detection** (Canny), **corner detection** (Harris, FAST), **keypoint detection** (SIFT, ORB), and **line/plane segmentation** for LiDAR point clouds. Output is a sparse set of salient features that compactly represent the scene.

- **Level 4 — Scene Interpretation:** Groups features into coherent entities. Operations include **object detection** (bounding boxes via YOLO/RCNN), **semantic segmentation** (per-pixel class labels), **instance segmentation** (distinguishing individual objects), and **pose estimation** (6-DoF object pose). Output is a list of objects with their categories, locations, and orientations.
