- **Level 5 — Semantic Understanding:** Performs high-level reasoning. Operations include **affordance prediction** (what actions an object supports), **intention inference** (predicting human or vehicle intent), **scene graph construction** (relationships between objects), and **task-relevant knowledge extraction** (e.g., "the cup is on the table, graspable, currently empty"). Output is symbolic knowledge consumable by the planner.

**(iii) Why Hierarchical? (Bonus — high-value KTU content):**

Each level reduces data dimensionality. A 1920×1080 RGB image has ~2 million pixels (Level 1), a few thousand keypoints (Level 3), perhaps 10 objects (Level 4), and ~5 symbolic facts (Level 5). This ~400,000× compression makes downstream planning tractable.

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Set B]** Discuss the role of **LiDAR and cameras in robotic perception**, comparing their **range, accuracy, robustness, and computational cost**. Explain the principle of **sensor fusion** in combining them with an **IMU** for Visual-Inertial-LiDAR odometry. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) LiDAR vs Camera Comparison (3 marks):**

| Attribute | Camera | LiDAR |
|---|---|---|
| Operating principle | Passive (ambient light) | Active (emits laser) |
| Output | 2D image (or 2.5D for RGB-D) | 3D point cloud |
| Range | 0.1 m to ∞ (passive) | 0.5 m to 200 m |
| Accuracy | Depth (if stereo): ±2 cm @ 1 m; mono: angular only | Range: ±2 cm regardless of distance |
| Color information | Rich RGB data | None (intensity only) |
| Robustness to lighting | Poor (fails in darkness, glare) | Excellent (independent of lighting) |
| Robustness to weather | Degraded by rain/fog | Degraded by heavy fog, snow |
| Computational cost | Very high (image processing) | Medium (point cloud processing) |
| Cost | Low to medium | High |
| Data density | Dense (every pixel) | Sparse at distance |

**(ii) Why Both? — Complementary Strengths (1 mark):**

Cameras provide **semantic richness** (color, texture, object class) at low cost. LiDAR provides **metric accuracy** and **lighting invariance** at high cost. Together, they cover each other's weaknesses: cameras classify what LiDAR detects, and LiDAR localizes what cameras see.

**(iii) Sensor Fusion in Visual-Inertial-LiDAR Odometry (3 marks):**

In a typical **Visual-Inertial-LiDAR Odometry (VILO)** system:

- **IMU** runs at **200–1000 Hz** and provides high-rate motion prediction between camera/LiDAR frames. It measures linear acceleration and angular velocity, but drifts over time.
- **Camera** runs at **30 Hz** and provides visual features (ORB keypoints) that can be matched across frames to estimate relative motion via **epipolar geometry** and the **essential matrix**.
- **LiDAR** runs at **10 Hz** and provides accurate 3D point cloud matches via **Iterative Closest Point (ICP)** registration or feature-based scan matching.

The fusion algorithm (typically an EKF or graph-optimization-based backend) maintains a unified state vector:

$$
\mathbf{x} = [p, q, v, b_a, b_g]^T
$$

where $p$ is position, $q$ is orientation quaternion, $v$ is velocity, and $b_a, b_g$ are IMU biases. The **IMU pre-integration** provides the prediction, while **camera and LiDAR measurements** provide corrections.

**The result:** A pose estimate that is simultaneously **high-rate** (from IMU), **drift-free** (from LiDAR), and **semantically grounded** (from camera) — far superior to any single sensor alone.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting units in derivations:** The stereo depth $Z = fb/d$ requires $f$ in pixels and $b$ in meters; mixing them is a **1-mark deduction** in KTU papers.
> 2. **Confusing KF prediction vs update:** The prediction step uses **IMU/odometry** (proprioceptive); the update step uses **camera/LiDAR/GPS** (exteroceptive). Reversing this is a **fundamental conceptual error** worth **2 marks lost**.
> 3. **Writing EKF as identical to KF:** The EKF uses **Jacobians** $\partial \mathbf{f}/\partial \mathbf{x}$ and $\partial \mathbf{h}/\partial \mathbf{x}$ — the linearization step is the **defining feature** of EKF. Omitting Jacobians in the answer costs **2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing disparity $d$ with depth $Z$:** Disparity is measured in **pixels**; depth is in **meters**. This distinction is commonly tested.
> 5. **Skipping the derivation steps in Part B:** KTU 2024 ESE values **step-by-step derivations** (each step worth 0.5–1 mark). Writing only the final answer without the intermediate algebra can lose **up to 3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density summary for last-minute KTU 2024 revision:**

- ☐ **Robotic perception** = process of converting sensor data into internal world models for robot action.
- ☐ Three sensor categories: **Exteroceptive** (cameras, LiDAR), **Proprioceptive** (IMU, encoders), **Interoceptive** (temperature, current).
- ☐ The **perception-action loop** runs at 10–100 Hz; perception is levels 1–2 of a 5-level pipeline.
- ☐ **Pinhole camera model:** $s\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R}\vert\mathbf{t}]\mathbf{X}$. Intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$ has focal lengths $f_x, f_y$ and principal point $(c_x, c_y)$.
- ☐ **Stereo depth equation:** $Z = fb/d$. Disparity $d$ is inversely proportional to depth. Valid only for $d > 0$ (point in front of both cameras).
- ☐ **LiDAR range equation:** $R = c\Delta t / 2$. Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- ☐ **IMU model:** $\mathbf{a}_m = \mathbf{R}^T(\mathbf{a} - \mathbf{g}) + \mathbf{b}_a + \mathbf{n}_a$. Biases $\mathbf{b}_a, \mathbf{b}_g$ cause drift.
- ☐ **Kalman Filter** has two steps: **Prediction** (using motion model) and **Update** (using measurement).
- ☐ **EKF** extends KF to nonlinear systems via **Jacobians** $\mathbf{F} = \partial \mathbf{f}/\partial \mathbf{x}$, $\mathbf{H} = \partial \mathbf{h}/\partial \mathbf{x}$.
- ☐ **Sensor fusion** combines multiple sensors to reduce uncertainty. EKF is the workhorse; modern systems use UKF, particle filters, or graph optimization.
- ☐ **Feature detectors:** ORB is the most common in modern visual SLAM (fast, free, rotation-invariant).
- ☐ **Five perception levels:** Signal Acquisition → Pre-processing → Feature Extraction → Scene Interpretation → Semantic Understanding.
- ☐ **VIO/VILO systems** combine IMU + camera (+ LiDAR) for high-rate, drift-free, semantically grounded pose estimation.
- ☐ **Standard sensors compared:** Cameras = rich + cheap + light-dependent; LiDAR = accurate + lighting-independent + expensive; IMU = high-rate + drifting.
- ☐ **Modern AI perception** uses CNN backbones + FPN + detection heads (YOLO, Mask R-CNN, DETR) for object detection and semantic segmentation.
- ☐ **Exam mantra:** Always state **units**, always show **derivation steps**, always include a **diagram** for full-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Localization and Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT614 - INTRODUCTION TO AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Localization and Mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Localization and Mapping

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Localization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Localization** is the process of determining the **pose** (position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$) of a mobile robot within a known or partially known reference frame, using onboard sensor measurements and control inputs.

Mathematically, localization is the problem of estimating a **belief distribution** over the robot's state space:

$$
bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

where:
- $x_t$ = robot state at time $t$
- $z_{1:t}$ = sequence of measurements $z_1, z_2, \dots, z_t$
- $u_{1:t}$ = sequence of control inputs (odometry) $u_1, u_2, \dots, u_t$

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mapping** is the process of constructing a spatial representation of the robot's environment from sensor observations. The map $m$ is learned such that $p(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ is maximized.

A map can be represented as:
- **Metric map** — geometric coordinates of features
- **Topological map** — graph of connected places
- **Hybrid map** — combination of both

### 1.3 The SLAM Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** is the joint estimation of both the robot's pose **and** the map of the environment, when neither is known a priori:
> $$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy: Waking Up in a Dark House**
>
> Imagine you wake up in a completely dark, unfamiliar house. You have a flashlight (sensor) and a mental diary (memory). Every time you take a step, you feel it through your feet (odometry/control input). Every time you point your flashlight, you see a wall, a door, or furniture (sensor measurement). Slowly, you build two things simultaneously:
> 1. **Localization** — "Where am I in this house?"
> 2. **Mapping** — "What does this house look like?"
>
> This is the **Chicken-and-Egg Problem**: to know where you are, you need a map; but to build a map, you need to know where you are. SLAM solves this through probabilistic reasoning.

### 1.5 Key Performance Metrics

| Metric | Description | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Localization Error** | Distance between estimated and true pose | **0.01 m – 0.5 m** |
| **Map Resolution** | Cell size in occupancy grid | **0.05 m – 0.30 m** |
| **Update Rate** | Frequency of pose estimate | **10 Hz – 100 Hz** |
| **Convergence Time** | Time to lock onto true pose | **few seconds** |
| **Computational Complexity** | Big-O cost per update | **$O(n)$ to $O(n^2)$** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Localization Problems

| Localization Type | Initial Pose | Map Knowledge | Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Position Tracking** | Known | Known | Easiest |
| **Local Localization** | Known (small error) | Known | Moderate |
| **Global Localization** | Unknown | Known | Hard |
| **Kidnapped Robot** | Unknown (teleported) | Known | Hardest |
| **SLAM** | Unknown | Unknown | Hardest |

## 2.2 Probabilistic Localization Framework — Bayes Filter

The Bayes Filter is the **backbone** of all probabilistic localization algorithms.

### Two-Step Recursion:

**Step 1 — Prediction (Motion Update):**
$$
\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Measurement Update (Correction):**
$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)
$$

where $\eta$ is a normalizer:
$$
\eta^{-1} = \int p(z_t \mid x_t') \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t') \, dx_t'
$$

- **Why prediction?** The robot moves → we propagate prior belief through motion model.
- **Why correction?** Sensor measurement reduces uncertainty → we sharpen the belief.
- **Why $\eta$?** Probabilities must integrate to 1.

## 2.3 Markov Localization

Uses the discrete Bayes Filter with a fine grid over the state space. Each cell holds a probability. After motion and measurement, the grid is updated via Bayes rule. Works for global localization but is computationally heavy: **$O(n)$** per update where $n$ is the number of grid cells.

## 2.4 Kalman Filter Localization (EKF)

Assumes Gaussian noise and linear(ized) models. State space is continuous.

### 2.4.1 Linear Motion Model
$$
x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \epsilon_t
$$

### 2.4.2 Linear Measurement Model
$$
z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t
$$

### 2.4.3 Prediction Step
$$
\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t
$$

### 2.4.4 Update Step
$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

where:
- $K_t$ = **Kalman Gain** (how much we trust the measurement)
- $R_t$ = process noise covariance
- $Q_t$ = measurement noise covariance
- $\mu_t, \Sigma_t$ = posterior mean and covariance

## 2.5 Monte Carlo Localization (Particle Filter)

Represents belief by a set of $M$ weighted samples (particles) $\{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{M}$.

### Algorithm Steps:
1. **Sampling** — draw $x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$
2. **Importance Weighting** — $w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$
3. **Resampling** — draw $M$ new particles proportional to weights

### Complexity: **$O(M)$** — independent of map size, but $M$ can be large (typically **100 – 5000**).

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas for KTU exams — they are the most asked topics in Module 4.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Posterior belief over state | All probabilistic localization |
| 2 | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})bel(x_{t-1})dx_{t-1}$ | Prediction step | Bayes Filter |
| 3 | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t)\overline{bel}(x_t)$ | Measurement update | Bayes Filter |
| 4 | $\bar{\mu}_t = A_t\mu_{t-1} + B_tu_t$ | Mean prediction | EKF |
| 5 | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t\Sigma_{t-1}A_t^T + R_t$ | Covariance prediction | EKF |
| 6 | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T(C_t\bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain | EKF |
| 7 | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - C_t\bar{\mu}_t)$ | Posterior mean update | EKF |
| 8 | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t$ | Posterior covariance | EKF |
| 9 | $w_t^{[i]} \propto w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$ | Particle weight | Particle Filter |
| 10 | $\eta^{-1} = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]}$ | Particle weight normalizer | Particle Filter |
| 11 | $l(m_i) = l(m_i) + l(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) - l_0$ | Occupancy log-odds update | Occupancy Grid |
| 12 | $l(m_i) = \log\frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}$ | Log-odds of cell occupancy | Occupancy Grid |
| 13 | $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Full SLAM posterior | SLAM |
| 14 | $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Online SLAM posterior | SLAM |
| 15 | $J = \sum_t \vert\vert z_t - h(x_t, l_j)\vert\vert^2_{R_t^{-1}}$ | Graph-SLAM cost | Graph SLAM |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility of these equations:**
> - **EKF-SLAM** is used in NASA's Mars rovers for terrain mapping.
> - **Particle Filter** is used in **Amazon Kiva robots** for warehouse localization.
> - **Occupancy Grids** are used in **self-driving cars** (e.g., Waymo, Tesla).
> - **Graph-SLAM** powers **Google Cartographer** open-source library.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bayes Filter (Localization Recursion)

We want to derive the recursive form of the posterior belief $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.

### Step 1: Apply Bayes Theorem
$$
bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})
$$

### Step 2: Apply Markov Assumption
The current state $x_t$ is sufficient to predict $z_t$ — history can be dropped:
$$
bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
$$

### Step 3: Simplify using Markov Property
Since $z_t$ depends only on $x_t$:
$$
p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)
$$

The denominator is the normalizer $\eta^{-1}$.

### Step 4: Expand the Prior using Total Probability
$$
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

### Step 5: Apply Markov to Motion Model
The state transition depends only on previous state and current control:
$$
p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)
$$

### Step 6: Combine and Identify $\overline{bel}$
$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \underbrace{\int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}}_{\overline{bel}(x_t)}
$$

$$
\boxed{bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Kalman Filter Update Equations

Starting from:
$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)
$$

### Step 1: Write Gaussian Forms

**Prior (prediction):**
$$
\overline{bel}(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(x_t; \bar{\mu}_t, \bar{\Sigma}_t)
$$

**Measurement likelihood:**
$$
p(z_t \mid x_t) = \mathcal{N}(z_t; C_t x_t, Q_t)
$$

### Step 2: Multiply the Exponentials
$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(z_t - C_t x_t)^T Q_t^{-1} (z_t - C_t x_t)\right) \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(x_t - \bar{\mu}_t)^T \bar{\Sigma}_t^{-1} (x_t - \bar{\mu}_t)\right)
$$

### Step 3: Complete the Square in $x_t$
After expanding and collecting terms in $(x_t - \mu_t)$:
$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1} (z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)
$$

Define the **Kalman Gain**:
$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

### Step 4: Simplify Posterior Mean
$$
\boxed{\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)}
$$

### Step 5: Simplify Posterior Covariance
Using the **matrix inversion lemma** $(A^{-1} + B^T C^{-1} B)^{-1} = A - AB^T(C + BAB^T)^{-1}BA$:
$$
\boxed{\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Particle Filter Importance Sampling

We want to draw samples from $bel(x_t)$ but cannot — it is complex. Instead, we sample from a **proposal distribution** $\pi(x_t)$.

### Step 1: Importance Weight Definition
$$
w_t^{[i]} = \frac{bel(x_t^{[i]})}{ \pi(x_t^{[i]} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})}
$$

### Step 2: Factor the Target Distribution
$$
bel(x_t) = p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot bel(x_{t-1})
$$

### Step 3: Choose the Proposal
Set $\pi(x_t) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ (the motion model).

### Step 4: Substitute
$$
w_t^{[i]} = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}) \cdot p(x_t^{[i]} \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}^{[i]})}{p(x_t^{[i]} \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)}
$$

### Step 5: Use Recursive Weight
$$
w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})
$$

$$
\boxed{w_t^{[i]} \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Occupancy Grid Mapping Update

We use the **log-odds representation** to convert multiplication into addition.

### Step 1: Bayes Filter for a Cell
$$
p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t})}
$$

### Step 2: Define Log-Odds
$$
l(m_i) = \log \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}
$$

The inverse:
$$
p(m_i) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(l(m_i))}
$$

### Step 3: Take Log of Both Sides
$$
l_t(m_i) = \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} + \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}
$$

### Step 4: Compact Form (Update Rule)
$$
\boxed{l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + \underbrace{l(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}_{\text{inverse sensor model}} - l_0}
$$

where $l_0$ is the prior log-odds (often $0$ for $p = 0.5$).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison

| Algorithm | Belief Representation | Key Strength | Key Weakness | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Markov Localization** | Discrete grid | Global localization | Memory: $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **EKF Localization** | Gaussian $(\mu, \Sigma)$ | Efficient, optimal for linear | Single hypothesis | $O(n^2)$ |
| **Particle Filter** | Weighted samples | Multi-modal, global | Particle depletion | $O(M)$ |
| **EKF-SLAM** | Gaussian over pose+landmarks | Closed-form solution | $O(N^2)$ scaling | $O(N^2)$ |
| **FastSLAM** | Particles + EKF per landmark | Handles $10^3$ landmarks | Particle depletion | $O(M \log N)$ |
| **Graph-SLAM** | Sparse pose graph | Globally consistent | Backend optimization | $O(n^3)$ via sparse solvers |
| **Occupancy Grid** | Log-odds per cell | Easy to build, dense maps | No landmarks | $O(n)$ per scan |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — 1D Kalman Filter

**Given:**
- $A = 1$, $B = 1$ (constant velocity = control input)
- $u_t = 1$ m
- $Q = 0.1$, $R = 0.2$
- $\mu_{t-1} = 0$, $\Sigma_{t-1} = 0.5$
- $z_t = 1.2$ m, $C = 1$

**Step 1: Prediction**
$$
\bar{\mu}_t = (1)(0) + (1)(1) = 1
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = (1)(0.5)(1) + 0.2 = 0.7
$$

**Step 2: Kalman Gain**
$$
K_t = \frac{0.7 \cdot 1}{1 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 1 + 0.1} = \frac{0.7}{0.8} = 0.875
$$

**Step 3: Update Mean**
$$
\mu_t = 1 + 0.875 \cdot (1.2 - 1) = 1 + 0.175 = 1.175
$$

**Step 4: Update Covariance**
$$
\Sigma_t = (1 - 0.875)(0.7) = 0.0875
$$

**Result:** The estimate converged to **$\mu_t = 1.175$ m** with reduced uncertainty from **0.7** to **0.0875**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The SLAM Functional Architecture

This block diagram maps the full SLAM pipeline from sensors to map output.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Robot Sensors] --> A2[LiDAR / Camera / IMU]
    A2 --> A3[Measurement Vector zt]
    B1[Wheel Encoders] --> B2[Odometry ut]
    A3 --> A3a[Landmark Extraction]
    A3a --> A3b[Data Association]
    B2 --> A3b
    A3b --> A4[Prediction Module]
    A4 --> A5[Prediction State xbar t]
    A5 --> A6[Measurement Update]
    A3b --> A6
    A6 --> A7[Posterior State xt]
    A7 --> A8[Pose Graph Optimizer]
    A8 --> A9[Map Builder]
    A9 --> A10[Occupancy Grid]
    A9 --> A11[Feature Map]
    A7 --> A12[Loop Closure Detector]
    A12 --> A8
    A10 --> A13[Final SLAM Map]
    A11 --> A13
```

## 4.2 Bayesian Localization Pipeline

This diagram shows the recursive flow of the Bayes Filter.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Start: Initial Belief bel x0] --> S2[Control Input ut]
    S2 --> S3[Apply Motion Model p xt ut xt-1]
    S3 --> S4[Predicted Belief bel bar xt]
    S4 --> S5[Sensor Measurement zt]
    S5 --> S6[Apply Measurement Model p zt xt]
    S6 --> S7[Multiply by Normalizer eta]
    S7 --> S8[Posterior Belief bel xt]
    S8 --> S9{More Data?}
    S9 --> S10[Output Robot Pose xt]
    S9 --> S2
```

## 4.3 Comparison of Localization Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R1[Localization] --> R2[Position Tracking]
    R1 --> R3[Global Localization]
    R1 --> R4[Kidnapped Robot]
    R2 --> R21[EKF / UKF]
    R3 --> R31[Markov Grid]
    R3 --> R32[Monte Carlo / Particle Filter]
    R3 --> R33[Multi Hypothesis Tracking]
    R4 --> R41[Particle Filter with Recovery]
    R4 --> R42[Monte Carlo with Random Injection]
    R21 --> R211[Single Gaussian Hypothesis]
    R32 --> R321[Set of Weighted Particles]
```

## 4.4 Mapping Module Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M1[Raw Scan Data] --> M2[Coordinate Transform]
    M2 --> M3[World Frame Points]
    M3 --> M4[Occupancy Grid Update]
    M4 --> M5[Log Odds Map]
    M5 --> M6[Threshold to Binary]
    M6 --> M7[Map Output]
    M4 --> M8[Feature Detection]
    M8 --> M9[Landmark Database]
    M9 --> M7
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Localization. Explain the kidnapped robot problem. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Localization** is the process of estimating a robot's pose (position and orientation) within a known or unknown environment using sensor measurements and control inputs. Mathematically, it is the computation of the posterior belief $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.

**The Kidnapped Robot Problem** is a localization scenario in which a well-localized robot is **teleported to an arbitrary new location** without being informed. The robot must:
1. Detect that it has been displaced.
2. Recover its pose from scratch using only sensor data.
3. Re-establish localization despite having a strong (but now incorrect) prior belief.

This is the **hardest localization problem** because the prior belief is completely wrong. **Particle filters** with random particle injection are commonly used to solve it because they can represent **multi-modal beliefs**.

*(Examiner awards 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for explanation of kidnapped problem.)*

---

### Question 2: What is SLAM? Differentiate between online and full SLAM. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)** is the problem of concurrently estimating a robot's pose and the map of an unknown environment using sensor data.

| Aspect | Online SLAM | Full SLAM |
|---|---|---|
| **Posterior** | $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ |
| **Estimates** | Current pose + map | Entire trajectory + map |
| **Memory** | Constant (bounded) | Grows with time |
| **Used in** | EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM | Graph-SLAM, Pose Graph Optimization |
| **Example** | Robot vacuum cleaner | Multi-session mapping |

*(Examiner awards 1 mark each for definition, online SLAM, and full SLAM with difference.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU ESE Rule:** Answer **ONE** of the two questions. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each.

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a): Derive the Bayes Filter recursion for robot localization. Explain the role of the motion model and measurement model. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: State the goal.** (1 mark)

We wish to compute the posterior belief over the robot's state $x_t$:

$$
bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})
$$

**Step 2: Apply Bayes Theorem.** (1 mark)

$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})
$$

**Step 3: Apply the Markov Assumption.** (1 mark)

Since $x_t$ is a complete summary of the past:
$$
p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)
$$

**Step 4: Expand using the Law of Total Probability.** (2 marks)

$$
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

**Step 5: Combine to obtain the recursion.** (1 mark)

$$
\boxed{bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}}
$$

**Step 6: Role of the two models.** (1 mark)
- **Motion model $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$**: propagates belief forward in time using odometry / control.
- **Measurement model $p(z_t \mid x_t)$**: corrects belief using sensor observations.

*(Examiner awards 1 mark for the derivation steps, 1 mark for the final boxed equation, 1 mark for stating the role of each model.)*

---

#### Part (b): Explain the Particle Filter (Monte Carlo Localization) algorithm with its key steps. Mention the resampling step and its importance. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Introduction.** (1 mark)

Particle Filter represents the belief $bel(x_t)$ by a set of $M$ weighted samples (particles):
$$
\mathcal{X}_t = \{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{M}, \quad \sum_i w_t^{[i]} = 1
$$

**Step 2: Sampling Step.** (1 mark)

Each particle is propagated using the motion model:
$$
x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})
$$

**Step 3: Importance Weighting Step.** (2 marks)

The weight of each particle is updated based on how well it explains the measurement:
$$
w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})
$$

The weights are then normalized:
$$
\tilde{w}_t^{[i]} = \frac{w_t^{[i]}}{\sum_j w_t^{[j]}}
$$

**Step 4: Resampling Step.** (2 marks)

Particles with low weights are replaced by copies of high-weight particles. This is done by drawing $M$ new particles from the current set with replacement, where the probability of selecting particle $i$ is $\tilde{w}_t^{[i]}$.

**Importance of Resampling:**
- Prevents **particle depletion** — without resampling, after a few iterations most particles have negligible weight, wasting computation.
- Concentrates computational resources in high-probability regions of state space.

**Step 5: Pose Estimation.** (1 mark)

The final pose is the weighted mean of particles:
$$
\hat{x}_t = \sum_{i=1}^{M} \tilde{w}_t^{[i]} \cdot x_t^{[i]}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a): With necessary equations, explain the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) for localization. Show the prediction and update steps. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Assumptions of EKF.** (1 mark)

EKF assumes:
- Motion and measurement models can be **linearized** around the current estimate.
- All noise is **zero-mean Gaussian**.
- Belief is unimodal (single Gaussian hypothesis).

**Step 2: Linearized Motion Model.** (1 mark)

$$
x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \epsilon_t, \quad \epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)
$$

**Step 3: Linearized Measurement Model.** (1 mark)

$$
z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t, \quad \delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)
$$

**Step 4: Prediction Step.** (1 mark)

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t
$$

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t
$$

**Step 5: Kalman Gain Computation.** (1 mark)

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \bar{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}
$$

**Step 6: Update Step.** (1 mark)

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - C_t \bar{\mu}_t)
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

**Step 7: Role of Kalman Gain.** (1 mark)

- If $Q_t \to 0$ (perfect sensor): $K_t \to C_t^{-1}$, fully trust measurement.
- If $R_t \to 0$ (perfect motion model): $K_t \to 0$, fully trust prediction.

---

#### Part (b): Explain occupancy grid mapping. Derive the log-odds update rule for an occupancy grid cell. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Introduction to Occupancy Grid.** (1 mark)

The environment is divided into a **2D grid** of equally sized cells. Each cell $m_i$ has a binary state: **occupied** or **free**. The map $m = \{m_1, m_2, \dots, m_N\}$ is the set of all cells.

**Step 2: Goal of Mapping.** (1 mark)

Estimate $p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ for each cell independently.

**Step 3: Apply Bayes Rule.** (1 mark)

$$
p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t})}
$$

**Step 4: Define Log-Odds.** (1 mark)

$$
l(m_i) = \log \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}
$$

**Step 5: Apply Log to Both Sides.** (1 mark)

$$
l_t(m_i) = \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} + \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}
$$

**Step 6: Compact Update Rule.** (1 mark)

$$
\boxed{l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + l(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) - l_0}
$$

where $l(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$ is the **inverse sensor model** and $l_0$ is the prior log-odds.

**Step 7: Decision Rule.** (1 mark)

A cell is classified as **occupied** if $l_t(m_i) > 0$ (i.e., $p > 0.5$), else **free**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the normalizer $\eta$** in Bayes filter — partial marks lost.
> 2. **Confusing prediction and update steps** in EKF — careful with subscripts.
> 3. **Markov assumption not stated explicitly** — always mention it in derivations.
> 4. **Log-odds derivation skipped** — examiners expect the log step-by-step; do not jump to the final boxed equation.
> 5. **Resampling not explained** in particle filter — always describe **why** it is needed (particle depletion).
> 6. **Units missing in numericals** — always include units (m, rad, m², etc.).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Localization and Mapping**

- **Localization** estimates the robot pose; **Mapping** estimates the environment; **SLAM** does both simultaneously.
- The **Bayes Filter** is the foundational algorithm — two steps: **prediction** and **update**.
- **Markov Assumption**: current state is sufficient summary of all past.
- **EKF** uses a single Gaussian; works well for position tracking but fails in global localization.
- **Particle Filter** uses weighted samples; can represent multi-modal beliefs; needs resampling.
- **Kalman Gain $K_t$** balances trust between motion model and measurement model.
- **Occupancy Grid** uses log-odds to make updates additive: $l_t = l_{t-1} + \text{measurement} - \text{prior}$.
- **EKF-SLAM** scales as $O(N^2)$ where $N$ = number of landmarks — limited to small maps.
- **FastSLAM** decouples landmark correlations using Rao-Blackwellization — handles **$10^3$–$10^5$** landmarks.
- **Graph-SLAM** builds a pose graph and optimizes it globally — used in modern systems like **Cartographer**.
- **Loop closure** is critical for long-term consistency — corrects accumulated drift.
- **Data association** is the problem of matching sensor observations to map landmarks — hardest in cluttered environments.
- **AMCL (Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization)** is the industry-standard ROS package for 2D localization.
- **Cartographer, Hector SLAM, GMapping, ORB-SLAM3** are popular open-source SLAM libraries.
- **Visual SLAM** uses cameras (mono/stereo/RGB-D); **Lidar SLAM** uses laser range finders.
- The **kidnapped robot problem** is the hardest — solved using random particle injection.
- **Computational complexity** determines real-time feasibility: EKF $O(n^2)$, Particle Filter $O(M)$, Grid $O(n)$ per scan.
- **Key constants to remember**: typical particle count $M \approx 100\text{–}5000$, grid resolution $0.05\text{–}0.3$ m, update rate $10\text{–}100$ Hz.
- **Measurement model $p(z_t \mid x_t)$** differs for LiDAR (range-bearing), camera (pixel projection), sonar (cone of probability).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machine Learning in Robot Perception
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT614 - INTRODUCTION TO AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Machine Learning in Robot Perception

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Machine Learning (ML) in Robot Perception** refers to the systematic application of statistical learning algorithms, neural network architectures, and data-driven inference engines that enable a robotic agent to **acquire, interpret, and generalize meaningful information** from raw, high-dimensional sensory streams (vision, LiDAR, depth, tactile, IMU) without being explicitly programmed for every possible scenario.

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, robot perception with ML is defined across three orthogonal axes:

1. **Sensory Encoding** — converting physical signals into structured feature tensors.
2. **Representation Learning** — learning compact, semantically meaningful latent embeddings.
3. **Decision Inference** — mapping these embeddings to task-level outputs (object class, 6-DoF pose, semantic segmentation mask, occupancy grid).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Per the KTU 2024 OERUT614 syllabus (Module 4), the focus spans *supervised perception models*, *unsupervised feature extraction*, *deep convolutional architectures for vision*, *sensor fusion via learned representations*, and *reinforcement-driven perception-action loops*.

### 1.2 The Three Foundational Learning Paradigms in Robotics

| Paradigm | Data Signal | Robotic Application |
|----------|------------|---------------------|
| **Supervised Learning** | $(X, y)$ labeled pairs | Object classification, semantic segmentation |
| **Unsupervised Learning** | $X$ only, no labels | Anomaly detection, autoencoder-based denoising |
| **Reinforcement Learning** | State-Action-Reward tuples | Active perception, view-point selection |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Robot as Infant" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine teaching a 2-year-old to recognize a *cup*. You do not hand her a geometry textbook. Instead, you repeatedly point and say "cup" (labels), she stumbles (errors), you correct (loss feedback), and over time her brain self-organizes the visual cortex. **ML in robot perception is exactly this** — instead of hard-coding geometric rules, the robot is exposed to thousands of labeled images, its internal weight matrices self-tune via backpropagation, and eventually a CNN "neuron" lights up whenever a cup enters its field of view.

The fundamental shift is from **hand-engineered features (SIFT, HOG, geometric primitives)** to **learned hierarchical features (edges → textures → parts → objects)**.

### 1.4 Why Traditional Rule-Based Perception Failed

Classical perception relied on:
- Hand-crafted feature descriptors (SIFT, SURF, ORB)
- Geometric model fitting (RANSAC, ICP)
- Threshold-based segmentation

These fail under **illumination variation, occlusion, deformable objects, and domain shift** — conditions that ML models handle robustly because they *learn invariance* directly from data.

### 1.5 The Closed-Loop Perception-Action Contract

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 scheme, robot perception is never isolated. It feeds the **Sense → Plan → Act** loop. ML-based perception closes this loop by enabling the agent to *act on uncertain observations* and *use action outcomes to refine perception* — the foundation of **embodied AI**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Supervised Perception Pipeline (End-to-End Logic)

1. **Data Acquisition:** RGB-D cameras, stereo rigs, LiDAR point clouds produce raw tensors $X \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$.
2. **Pre-processing:** Normalization $\tilde{X} = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$, resizing, augmentation (rotation, flip, color jitter).
3. **Feature Extraction:** A CNN encoder $f_\theta(\cdot)$ maps $\tilde{X} \rightarrow z \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ (latent embedding).
4. **Task Head:** A task-specific layer (softmax, sigmoid, regressor) maps $z \rightarrow \hat{y}$.
5. **Loss Computation:** $\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y)$ quantifies the discrepancy.
6. **Parameter Update:** $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \alpha \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$ (gradient descent).
7. **Inference:** At test time, $\hat{y} = f_{\theta^*}(X_{\text{new}})$ predicts the perception output.

### 2.2 The Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) Backbone — Operational Mechanics

A CNN is a **spatially-structured neural network** exploiting two inductive biases:
- **Local connectivity** — each neuron sees only a small receptive field.
- **Weight sharing** — the same kernel slides across all spatial positions.

The discrete 2-D convolution for a single input channel is:

$$Y[i, j] = \sum_{m=0}^{k_h - 1} \sum_{n=0}^{k_w - 1} W[m, n] \cdot X[i + m, j + n] + b$$

Where $W$ is the learnable kernel, $b$ is the bias, and $k_h, k_w$ are kernel dimensions. The ReLU non-linearity is then applied: $A = \max(0, Y)$.

### 2.3 Loss Functions for Perception Tasks

For **classification** (e.g., object recognition):

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$$

For **regression** (e.g., 6-DoF pose estimation):

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

For **detection** (bounding-box regression, Smooth L1):

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{SmoothL1}}(x) = \begin{cases} 0.5 x^2 & \text{if } \vert x \vert < 1 \\ \vert x \vert - 0.5 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### 2.4 The Gradient Descent Update Rule (Heart of Learning)

The fundamental parameter update for any learnable weight $w$ in a perception network:

$$w^{(t+1)} = w^{(t)} - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w^{(t)}}$$

Where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate** (typical range: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$). Variants include SGD with momentum, RMSProp, and Adam.

### 2.5 Softmax Activation for Multi-Class Perception

$$\hat{y}_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}}$$

This converts raw logits $z$ into a valid probability distribution over $C$ object classes.

### 2.6 Evaluation Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

| Metric | Formula | Use Case |
|--------|---------|----------|
| **Accuracy** | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Balanced classification |
| **Precision** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | When false positives are costly |
| **Recall** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | When false negatives are costly (e.g., obstacle detection) |
| **F1-Score** | $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of precision/recall |
| **IoU (Jaccard)** | $\frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Bounding-box overlap, segmentation |
| **mAP** | $\frac{1}{C} \sum_{c} \text{AP}_c$ | Object detection (PASCAL VOC, COCO) |

### 2.7 Sensor Fusion via Learned Representations

Modern robots rarely rely on a single modality. Late fusion combines modality-specific features:

$$z_{\text{fused}} = \phi_{\text{cam}}(X_{\text{RGB}}) \oplus \phi_{\text{depth}}(X_{\text{D}}) \oplus \phi_{\text{imu}}(X_{\text{IMU}})$$

Where $\oplus$ denotes concatenation, summation, or an attention-weighted combination.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ML-driven perception powers **autonomous vehicles (Tesla FSD, Waymo)**, **surgical robots (da Vinci)**, **warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva)**, **agricultural drones (precision spraying)**, and **humanoid platforms (Tesla Optimus, Figure 01)**. The economic and safety stakes make rigorous theoretical understanding of the loss landscape, gradient flow, and evaluation metrics non-negotiable for KTU graduates entering Industry 4.0 roles.

### 2.9 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Quick Revision)

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range/Unit |
|--------|---------|-------------------|
| $X$ | Input tensor (image) | $\mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$ |
| $y$ | Ground-truth label | Integer (class) or vector |
| $\hat{y}$ | Predicted output | Probability in $[0, 1]$ |
| $\theta$ | Network parameters | $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| $\alpha$ | Learning rate | $[10^{-4}, 10^{-1}]$ |
| $\mathcal{L}$ | Loss scalar | Non-negative real |
| $k$ | Kernel size | $\{1, 3, 5, 7\}$ pixels |
| $s$ | Stride | $\{1, 2\}$ |
| $p$ | Padding | $\{0, 1, \text{same}\}$ |
| IoU | Intersection-over-Union | $[0, 1]$, threshold $\geq 0.5$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gradient Descent Update Rule (Full Mathematical Walkthrough)

**Goal:** Prove that iteratively updating parameters in the direction of steepest descent on the loss function minimizes $\mathcal{L}$.

**Step 1 — Taylor Series Linearization:**

For a small perturbation $\Delta w$ around the current weight $w^{(t)}$:

$$\mathcal{L}(w^{(t)} + \Delta w) \approx \mathcal{L}(w^{(t)}) + \nabla_w \mathcal{L}(w^{(t)})^T \Delta w$$

**Step 2 — Choose $\Delta w$ to minimize the linearized loss:**

We want to minimize the second term subject to a fixed step size constraint $\Vert \Delta w \Vert^2 = \epsilon^2$:

$$\min_{\Delta w} \left[ \nabla_w \mathcal{L}^T \Delta w \right] \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \Vert \Delta w \Vert^2 = \epsilon^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply Cauchy-Schwarz inequality:**

The inner product is minimized when $\Delta w$ is anti-parallel to the gradient:

$$\Delta w = -\alpha \nabla_w \mathcal{L}(w^{(t)})$$

**Step 4 — Form the update rule:**

$$w^{(t+1)} = w^{(t)} - \alpha \nabla_w \mathcal{L}(w^{(t)})$$

This is the **canonical gradient descent update** that all deep learning optimizers (SGD, Adam, RMSProp) generalize.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gradient of the Cross-Entropy Loss with Softmax

**Goal:** Derive the clean gradient signal that makes softmax + cross-entropy the standard perception loss.

**Step 1 — Combine softmax and cross-entropy:**

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) \quad \text{where} \quad \hat{y}_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k}$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k} = -\sum_i y_i \frac{1}{\hat{y}_i} \frac{\partial \hat{y}_i}{\partial z_k}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the softmax derivative:**

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}_i}{\partial z_k} = \hat{y}_i (\delta_{ik} - \hat{y}_k)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k} = -\sum_i y_i (\delta_{ik} - \hat{y}_k) = -y_k + \hat{y}_k \sum_i y_i$$

**Step 5 — Use the one-hot property $\sum_i y_i = 1$:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k} = \hat{y}_k - y_k}$$

This beautiful result shows that **the gradient is simply the prediction error** — making softmax + cross-entropy numerically stable and computationally elegant. This is why it is the *de facto* loss for multi-class robot perception tasks.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Output Size of a Convolution Layer

**Goal:** Given input $H \times W$, kernel $k$, stride $s$, padding $p$, derive the output spatial dimension.

**Step 1 — Count how many kernel positions fit horizontally:**

The effective number of shifts is:

$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{H + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$$

**Step 2 — Intuition:** The kernel starts at column $-p$ and the last valid start position is at column $H - k + p$. Dividing by stride $s$ and adding 1 yields the formula above.

**Step 3 — Numerical example for KTU exam-style problem:**

Input $H = 224$, kernel $k = 3$, stride $s = 1$, padding $p = 1$:

$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{224 + 2(1) - 3}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor 223 \right\rfloor + 1 = 224$$

So the spatial dimension is preserved — a foundational property exploited in ResNet-style architectures.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Receptive Field Expansion in Deep CNNs

**Goal:** Derive the receptive field (RF) of a neuron in layer $L$ of a sequential CNN.

**Step 1 — Define the recurrence relation:**

$$\text{RF}_0 = 1 \quad \text{(single pixel input)}$$

$$\text{RF}_l = \text{RF}_{l-1} + (k_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{l-1} s_j$$

**Step 2 — Expand for a 3-layer network with $k_1 = k_2 = k_3 = 3$, $s_1 = s_2 = s_3 = 1$:**

$$\text{RF}_1 = 1 + (3-1) \cdot 1 = 3$$

$$\text{RF}_2 = 3 + (3-1) \cdot 1 = 5$$

$$\text{RF}_3 = 5 + (3-1) \cdot 1 = 7$$

**Step 3 — Generalize:** For $L$ layers of $3 \times 3$ convolutions with stride 1:

$$\text{RF}_L = 1 + 2L$$

This linear growth is why **deeper networks capture more global context** — a critical insight for robotic scene understanding where the robot must relate distant pixels (e.g., wheels to a car body).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Loss Function Selection for Perception Tasks

| Perception Task | Output Type | Recommended Loss | Why |
|----------------|-------------|------------------|-----|
| Object classification | One-hot vector | Cross-Entropy | Probabilistic, well-calibrated |
| Bounding-box regression | 4-D continuous vector | Smooth L1 | Robust to outliers vs MSE |
| Semantic segmentation | Per-pixel class map | Pixel-wise CE | Localized decision per pixel |
| Depth estimation | Single scalar per pixel | L1 / BerHu | Preserves sharp discontinuities |
| 6-DoF pose estimation | $(t, q)$ translation + quaternion | Geodesic loss | Respects SO(3) manifold |
| Anomaly detection | Scalar anomaly score | Reconstruction (autoencoder) | Unsupervised, label-free |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End ML Perception Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Sensor Stream] --> B[Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Feature Encoder CNN]
    C --> D[Latent Embedding z]
    D --> E{Task Head}
    E --> F[Object Class]
    E --> G[Bounding Box]
    E --> H[Segmentation Mask]
    E --> I[6 DoF Pose]
    F --> J[Decision Module]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Robot Action]
    K --> L[Environment Update]
    L --> A
```

### 4.2 CNN Backbone Architecture (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    in1[Input Image 224x224x3] --> c1[Conv 3x3 64 ReLU]
    c1 --> c2[Conv 3x3 64 ReLU]
    c2 --> p1[MaxPool 2x2]
    p1 --> c3[Conv 3x3 128 ReLU]
    c3 --> c4[Conv 3x3 128 ReLU]
    c4 --> p2[MaxPool 2x2]
    p2 --> c5[Conv 3x3 256 ReLU]
    c5 --> c6[Conv 3x3 256 ReLU]
    c6 --> c7[Conv 3x3 256 ReLU]
    c7 --> p3[MaxPool 2x2]
    p3 --> gap[Global Average Pool]
    gap --> fc1[FC 4096 ReLU Dropout]
    fc1 --> fc2[FC 4096 ReLU Dropout]
    fc2 --> outp[Softmax C Classes]
```

### 4.3 Supervised Learning Training Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Start Epoch] --> s2[Shuffle Training Data]
    s2 --> s3[Fetch Mini Batch]
    s3 --> s4[Forward Pass]
    s4 --> s5[Compute Loss L]
    s5 --> s6{Converged}
    s6 -->|No| s7[Backward Pass Gradients]
    s7 --> s8[Optimizer Step Adam or SGD]
    s8 --> s9[Update Weights]
    s9 --> s3
    s6 -->|Yes| s10[Save Checkpoint]
    s10 --> s11[End Training]
```

### 4.4 Multi-Modal Sensor Fusion Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rgb[RGB Camera Branch] --> enc1[CNN Encoder]
    dep[Depth Camera Branch] --> enc2[CNN Encoder]
    lid[LiDAR Branch] --> enc3[PointNet Encoder]
    imu[IMU Branch] --> enc4[MLP Encoder]
    enc1 --> fuse[Fusion Layer Concat or Attention]
    enc2 --> fuse
    enc3 --> fuse
    enc4 --> fuse
    fuse --> head[Task Head Perception Output]
```

### 4.5 Perception-Action Closed Loop in Embodied Robotics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    env[Environment World State] --> sens[Sensors Camera LiDAR IMU]
    sens --> perc[ML Perception Module]
    perc --> belief[Belief State z]
    belief --> plan[Planner RL or Classical]
    plan --> act[Low Level Controller]
    act --> motor[Robot Actuators]
    motor --> env
    plan --> rew[Reward Signal]
    rew --> perc
```

### 4.6 Confusion Matrix Structure (Evaluation Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    input[Predicted vs Actual] --> tp[True Positive TP]
    input --> tn[True Negative TN]
    input --> fp[False Positive FP]
    input --> fn[False Negative FN]
    tp --> m1[Precision]
    tp --> m2[Recall]
    fp --> m1
    fn --> m2
    m1 --> m3[F1 Score]
    m2 --> m3
    m3 --> out[Single Performance Number]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Style, OERUT614]**
*Differentiate between supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning in the context of robot perception. Give one robotic application for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Learning Type | Data Requirement | Robotic Application |
|---------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Supervised** | Labeled $(X, y)$ pairs | Object classification using labeled image datasets (e.g., COCO, ImageNet) |
| **Unsupervised** | Unlabeled $X$ | Anomaly detection on factory-floor robotic cells using autoencoders |
| **Reinforcement** | State-action-reward trajectories | Active viewpoint selection for a robot arm to reduce classification uncertainty |

**Key distinction:** Supervised learns a *mapping*, unsupervised learns *structure*, reinforcement learns a *policy* through environmental interaction.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for correct definition of each + 1 mark for application)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Module 4 Weightage, OERUT614]**
*What is the role of a loss function in training a perception model? Why is cross-entropy preferred over mean squared error for multi-class object recognition?*

**Model Answer:**

The **loss function** $\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y)$ quantifies the discrepancy between the model's prediction $\hat{y}$ and the ground truth $y$. It serves as the *single scalar signal* that the optimizer uses to update network weights via backpropagation.

**Cross-entropy vs MSE for classification:**

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_c y_c \log(\hat{y}_c) \quad \text{vs} \quad \mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_i (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

1. **Probabilistic interpretation:** Cross-entropy measures the divergence between the true distribution $y$ and predicted distribution $\hat{y}$ — natural for classification.
2. **Gradient quality:** The gradient $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}}{\partial z_k} = \hat{y}_k - y_k$ is linear and well-conditioned. MSE applied to softmax outputs yields vanishing gradients when $\hat{y}$ saturates near 0 or 1.
3. **Numerical stability:** Softmax + cross-entropy avoids the pathological log-of-zero issues that plague MSE on probability outputs.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for loss function role + 2 marks for cross-entropy vs MSE justification)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A.(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – End-Semester Pattern, OERUT614]**
*Explain the architecture of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for robot visual perception. With neat diagrams, describe the role of convolution, ReLU activation, and pooling layers. Derive the output dimension formula for a convolution operation.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — CNN Architecture Overview (2 marks):**
A CNN is a hierarchical feature extractor composed of alternating **convolutional** and **pooling** layers, terminating in **fully-connected** layers for task-specific inference. For a robot, the input is an RGB image $X \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$.

**Step 2 — Convolution Layer (2 marks):**
Each convolutional layer applies a set of learnable kernels $W \in \mathbb{R}^{k_h \times k_w \times C_{\text{in}} \times C_{\text{out}}}$ that slide across the input:

$$Y[i, j, c] = \sum_{m, n, k} W[m, n, k, c] \cdot X[i + m, j + n, k] + b_c$$

This exploits **local connectivity** and **weight sharing**, drastically reducing parameters compared to fully-connected networks.

**Step 3 — ReLU and Pooling (1 mark):**
ReLU introduces non-linearity: $A = \max(0, Y)$. Max-pooling downsamples: $P[i, j] = \max_{(m,n) \in \text{window}} A[i \cdot s + m, j \cdot s + n]$.

**Step 4 — Output Dimension Derivation (2 marks):**
For input $H$, kernel $k$, stride $s$, padding $p$:

$$H_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{H + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$$

**Numerical example:** $H = 224$, $k = 3$, $s = 1$, $p = 1$:

$$H_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{224 + 2 - 3}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = 224$$

Hence, the spatial map is preserved with proper padding — a property exploited in modern ResNet-style architectures.

---

**Q.A.(b) [7 Marks]**
*Discuss the backpropagation algorithm used to train a perception network. Derive the gradient of the cross-entropy loss combined with the softmax activation. Why is this combination numerically preferred?*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Backpropagation Intuition (2 marks):**
Backpropagation applies the **chain rule of calculus** to propagate the loss gradient $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}}$ backwards through the computational graph, layer by layer, computing $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_l}$ for every weight matrix $W_l$.

**Step 2 — Chain Rule Formulation (1 mark):**
For a layer $l$ with input $a_{l-1}$ and weights $W_l$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_l} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial a_l} \cdot \frac{\partial a_l}{\partial W_l}$$

**Step 3 — Derive softmax + cross-entropy gradient (3 marks):**

$$\hat{y}_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}, \quad \mathcal{L} = -\sum_i y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$$

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}_i}{\partial z_k} = \hat{y}_i (\delta_{ik} - \hat{y}_k)$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k} = -\sum_i y_i \frac{1}{\hat{y}_i} \hat{y}_i (\delta_{ik} - \hat{y}_k) = \hat{y}_k - y_k$$

**Step 4 — Why numerically preferred (1 mark):**
1. **Linear gradient:** The gradient equals the prediction error directly, avoiding vanishing-gradient pathology.
2. **Log-softmax stability:** Computing $\log(\hat{y}_i)$ analytically cancels the softmax exponential, preventing floating-point underflow.
3. **Convex landscape:** Cross-entropy induces a smoother loss surface than MSE for classification, accelerating convergence.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B.(a) [7 Marks]**
*Explain multi-modal sensor fusion in robot perception. With a block diagram, describe how camera, LiDAR, and IMU data are fused using learned representations. What are the advantages of late fusion over early fusion?*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Multi-modal perception motivation (2 marks):**
No single sensor is sufficient: cameras fail in low light, LiDAR is sparse at long range, IMU drifts over time. **Fusion** combines complementary modalities to improve robustness and accuracy.

**Step 2 — Late fusion architecture (2 marks):**
Each modality is processed by a dedicated encoder:
- $z_{\text{cam}} = \phi_{\text{CNN}}(X_{\text{RGB}})$
- $z_{\text{lid}} = \phi_{\text{PointNet}}(X_{\text{LiDAR}})$
- $z_{\text{imu}} = \phi_{\text{MLP}}(X_{\text{IMU}})$

These embeddings are concatenated:

$$z_{\text{fused}} = [z_{\text{cam}} \oplus z_{\text{lid}} \oplus z_{\text{imu}}]$$

and passed to a downstream task head.

**Step 3 — Late vs Early fusion comparison (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Early Fusion | Late Fusion |
|--------|--------------|-------------|
| Fusion stage | Raw sensor level | Feature embedding level |
| Synchronization | Strictly required | More tolerant |
| Modality-specific design | Limited | Each branch fully optimized |
| Failure isolation | One bad sensor corrupts all | Graceful degradation |
| Computational cost | Lower (single backbone) | Higher (multiple backbones) |

**Conclusion:** Late fusion is the *de facto* standard in modern robotics (e.g., nuScenes benchmark) because it enables modality-specific inductive biases and resilience to sensor dropout.

---

**Q.B.(b) [7 Marks]**
*Describe evaluation metrics for a robot perception system. For an object detector, derive the mAP metric. A robot detector yields: TP=40, FP=10, FN=20. Compute Precision, Recall, and F1-score.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Confusion matrix elements (1 mark):**
- **TP** = correctly detected objects
- **FP** = false alarms (background detected as object)
- **FN** = missed objects
- **TN** = correctly rejected background

**Step 2 — Metric definitions (2 marks):**

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}, \quad \text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}, \quad F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P + R}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical computation (2 marks):**
Given $TP = 40$, $FP = 10$, $FN = 20$:

$$P = \frac{40}{40 + 10} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.80$$

$$R = \frac{40}{40 + 20} = \frac{40}{60} \approx 0.667$$

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \times 0.80 \times 0.667}{0.80 + 0.667} = \frac{1.067}{1.467} \approx 0.727$$

**Step 4 — mAP derivation (2 marks):**
Average Precision (AP) is the area under the Precision-Recall curve. For $C$ classes:

$$\text{mAP} = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} \text{AP}_c$$

where $\text{AP}_c$ integrates $P(R)$ over $R \in [0, 1]$ for class $c$. A detection is counted as TP only if $\text{IoU} \geq 0.5$ with the ground-truth box.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in OERUT614 Module 4:**
> 1. **Confusing IoU with accuracy** — IoU is a spatial overlap metric, not a classification metric. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the softmax derivative chain** — Many students write the cross-entropy loss but fail to show the Jacobian derivation of softmax. This costs 2–3 marks in Part B.
> 3. **Using $\vert x \vert$ in markdown tables** — Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX. Markdown rendering can break your answer sheet.
> 4. **Skipping the numerical example** — A 14-mark derivation question without a numerical example typically receives at most 10/14. Always substitute values.
> 5. **Writing the convolution output as a single sum** — Examiners expect explicit indexing $Y[i, j]$ and clear mention of stride and padding.
> 6. **Ignoring vanishing gradients in MSE + softmax** — A classic conceptual trap. Always justify cross-entropy using the linear-gradient result $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k} = \hat{y}_k - y_k$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Machine Learning in Robot Perception**

- [ ] **Three ML paradigms:** Supervised (labeled), Unsupervised (structure), Reinforcement (policy).
- [ ] **CNN backbone:** Convolution + ReLU + Pooling — exploits local connectivity and weight sharing.
- [ ] **Output dimension formula:** $H_{\text{out}} = \lfloor (H + 2p - k)/s \rfloor + 1$.
- [ ] **Receptive field:** Grows linearly with depth: $\text{RF}_L = 1 + 2L$ for $3 \times 3$ stride-1 stacks.
- [ ] **Cross-entropy loss:** $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_c y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$ with gradient $\hat{y}_k - y_k$.
- [ ] **Softmax:** $\hat{y}_i = e^{z_i} / \sum_j e^{z_j}$ — converts logits to probabilities.
- [ ] **Gradient descent update:** $w^{(t+1)} = w^{(t)} - \alpha \nabla_w \mathcal{L}$.
- [ ] **Evaluation metrics:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, IoU, mAP — choose based on class balance.
- [ ] **Multi-modal fusion:** Late fusion $>$ Early fusion for heterogeneous sensors due to failure isolation.
- [ ] **Active perception:** RL-based viewpoint selection reduces uncertainty in cluttered environments.
- [ ] **Numerical safeguard:** Use `\vert` in LaTeX tables; avoid floating-point instability in softmax via log-sum-exp trick.
- [ ] **Industry 4.0 link:** Tesla FSD, Amazon Kiva, da Vinci Surgical Robot all use ML-perception pipelines.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Machine Learning in Robot Perception (OERUT614, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Domains: Mobile Robot Navigation - Path Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT614 - INTRODUCTION TO AI IN ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application Domains: Mobile Robot Navigation - Path Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mobile Robot Path Planning** is the computational problem of determining a collision-free, optimal (or near-optimal) trajectory for a mobile robot from an initial configuration to a goal configuration, while satisfying kinematic, dynamic, and environmental constraints within a known, partially known, or unknown workspace.

In the formal language of the **Configuration Space (C-space)** approach (Lozano-Pérez, 1983), the problem is expressed as finding a continuous mapping:

$$\tau: [0,1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$$

such that $\tau(0) = q_{\text{start}}$ and $\tau(1) = q_{\text{goal}}$, where $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ represents the set of all collision-free robot configurations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: Path Planning is classified under *global navigation* when the environment is fully known a priori, and *local navigation* (or *reactive navigation*) when obstacles are discovered online via onboard sensors.

### Classification of Path Planning Algorithms

| Taxonomy Branch | Sub-Class | Representative Algorithms |
|---|---|---|
| **Classical / Roadmap** | Visibility Graph, Voronoi Diagram | Visibility Graph, Shortest Path Map |
| **Cell Decomposition** | Exact & Approximate | Trapezoidal, QuadTree, Boustrophedon |
| **Potential Field** | Attractive + Repulsive | Classical APF, Navigation Functions |
| **Graph / Search Based** | Heuristic Search | Dijkstra, A*, D*, Weighted A* |
| **Sampling Based** | Probabilistic | PRM, RRT, RRT*, Informed RRT* |
| **Reactive / Behavior-Based** | Subsumption | Dynamic Window Approach (DWA), VFH |
| **Learning Based** | Neural / Reinforcement | Deep RL, DQN-based planners |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Blindfolded Traveler in a Lit Room"**
>
> Imagine you are blindfolded and dropped in the middle of a room. You cannot see the walls, but you have:
> 1. **A map** of the room (the *occupancy grid*).
> 2. **A compass** pointing toward the exit (the *heuristic function $h(n)$*).
> 3. **A pedometer** measuring how far you have walked (the *cost-so-far $g(n)$*).
> 4. **A memory** of every step you took (the *graph traversal*).
>
> Path planning is the *deliberate, mathematical act* of choosing footsteps so that the **pedometer + compass estimate** ($f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$) is always minimized, without ever colliding with the walls (obstacles). The robot is the traveler; the map is its world model; A* is the strategy that balances *where you are* against *where you wish to be*.

### Key Terminology for KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Vocabulary (Examiner's Checklist)**:
> - **Workspace ($W$)**: The 2-D or 3-D physical environment where the robot operates.
> - **Configuration ($q$)**: A complete specification of robot pose (typically $(x, y, \theta)$).
> - **C-space ($\mathcal{C}$)**: The space of all possible configurations.
> - **Free Space ($\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$)**: Subset of $\mathcal{C}$ with no collisions.
> - **Holonomic Robot**: A robot whose controllable degrees of freedom equal its total degrees of freedom (e.g., omnidirectional mecanum-wheel).
> - **Non-holonomic Robot**: A robot subject to velocity constraints (e.g., a car — cannot move sideways); classical example: the **Dubins Car** with minimum turning radius $r_{\min}$.
> - **Admissible Heuristic**: A heuristic that never overestimates the true cost to goal.
> - **Consistent Heuristic**: $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$ for every successor $n'$ of $n$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Configuration Space Paradigm

The most rigorous mathematical foundation for path planning is the **Configuration Space Obstacle** mapping. For a rigid robot $A$ moving among obstacles $B_i$, the configuration obstacle for $B_i$ is defined as:

$$\mathcal{C}B_i = \{ q \in \mathcal{C} \;\vert\; A(q) \cap B_i \neq \emptyset \}$$

The total obstacle region is the union:

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{C}B_i$$

The free configuration space:

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Reducing the geometric "robot vs. obstacles" problem to a "point vs. C-obstacles" problem is the **fundamental trick** that makes path planning tractable for both KTU problems and industrial implementations.

## 2.2 Grid / Lattice-Based Representation

The workspace is discretized into a uniform grid $G$ of resolution $\Delta$. Each cell is assigned a state $s \in \{\text{FREE}, \text{OBS}\}$. The 4-connectivity and 8-connectivity neighborhood actions are:

$$N_4(s) = \{ s \pm (1,0), s \pm (0,1) \}$$

$$N_8(s) = N_4(s) \cup \{ s \pm (1,1), s \pm (1,-1) \}$$

The transition cost between $s$ and $s' \in N_8(s)$ is:

$$c(s, s') = \begin{cases} \Delta & \text{if } s' \in N_4(s) \setminus N_4(s) \\ \Delta\sqrt{2} & \text{if } s' \in N_8(s) \setminus N_4(s) \end{cases}$$

## 2.3 The A* Algorithm — The Heart of Modern Path Planning

The A* algorithm (Hart, Nilsson & Raphael, 1968) computes an optimal path by minimizing the evaluation function:

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

where:

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $g(n)$ | **True cost** accumulated from start node to current node $n$ |
| $h(n)$ | **Heuristic estimate** of cost from $n$ to goal |
| $f(n)$ | **Total estimated cost** of the cheapest solution passing through $n$ |

### 2.3.1 Optimality Conditions (Admissibility & Consistency)

A heuristic is **admissible** iff:

$$h(n) \leq h^*(n) \quad \forall n$$

where $h^*(n)$ is the true optimal cost from $n$ to goal.

A heuristic is **consistent** (or **monotone**) iff:

$$h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n') \quad \forall \text{ successors } n' \text{ of } n$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (A* Optimality)**: If $h(n)$ is admissible, A* is guaranteed to return an optimal path. If $h(n)$ is additionally consistent, A* is also optimally **efficient** — any A*-consistent algorithm that expands the same node set must expand *at least* as many nodes as A*.

### 2.4 The Potential Field Method

The Artificial Potential Field (APF) method (Khatib, 1986) treats the robot as a point under the influence of two scalar fields:

$$U_{\text{total}}(q) = U_{\text{att}}(q) + U_{\text{rep}}(q)$$

The force is the negative gradient:

$$\mathbf{F}(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{total}}(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{att}}(q) - \nabla U_{\text{rep}}(q)$$

**Attractive Potential** (conic or parabolic):

$$U_{\text{att}}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\xi \,\rho_{\text{goal}}^2(q)$$

where $\rho_{\text{goal}}(q) = \vert\vert q - q_{\text{goal}} \vert\vert$ and $\xi > 0$ is the scaling gain.

**Repulsive Potential** (for obstacle $i$ at distance $\rho_i$):

$$U_{\text{rep},i}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_i(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2 & \text{if } \rho_i(q) \leq \rho_0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $\rho_0$ is the influence radius and $\eta > 0$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Local Minima Problem**: The gradient field can vanish at non-goal points where attractive and repulsive forces cancel — a classical *local minima trap*. KTU exam questions frequently test this pitfall.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas — Memorize Before KTU ESE**

| # | Concept | Equation | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A* Total Cost | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Cost in same units (m, s, J) |
| 2 | Admissibility | $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ | Guarantees optimality |
| 3 | Consistency | $h(n) \leq c(n,n') + h(n')$ | Guarantees graph-search optimality |
| 4 | Manhattan Distance | $d_M = \vert x_g - x \vert + \vert y_g - y \vert$ | 4-connected grids |
| 5 | Euclidean Distance | $d_E = \sqrt{(x_g-x)^2 + (y_g-y)^2}$ | Admissible for any direction |
| 6 | Diagonal / Octile | $d_O = \max(\Delta x, \Delta y) + (\sqrt{2}-1)\min(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ | 8-connected grids |
| 7 | Chebyshev Distance | $d_C = \max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert)$ | King-move chess heuristic |
| 8 | Attractive Force | $\mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} = -\xi(q - q_{\text{goal}})$ | Negative gradient of $U_{\text{att}}$ |
| 9 | Repulsive Force (scalar form) | $\mathbf{F}_{\text{rep}} = \eta \left(\tfrac{1}{\rho_i} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\tfrac{1}{\rho_i^2}\,\nabla\rho_i$ | For $\rho_i \leq \rho_0$ |
| 10 | Dijkstra (uninformed) | $f(n) = g(n)$ | Special case of A* with $h(n)=0$ |
| 11 | RRT Growth (kinematic) | $q_{\text{new}} = q_{\text{near}} + \delta \cdot \dfrac{q_{\text{rand}} - q_{\text{near}}}{\vert\vert q_{\text{rand}} - q_{\text{near}} \vert\vert}$ | Step size $\delta$ |
| 12 | Weighted A* | $f(n) = g(n) + \epsilon \cdot h(n), \quad \epsilon > 1$ | Sub-optimal, faster |
| 13 | Path Length (continuous) | $L = \int_{0}^{1} \vert\vert \dot{\tau}(t) \vert\vert \, dt$ | Total arc length |
| 14 | Time-Optimal Velocity | $v^*(t) = \pm v_{\max}$, switching at $t^*$ | Bang-bang control |
| 15 | Reeds-Shepp Length | $L_{\text{RS}} = \sum_i (\pm R_{\min}\theta_i + \pm s_i)$ | Car-like non-holonomic |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Applications:**
> - **Autonomous Vehicles (Mobileye, Waymo, Tesla FSD)**: Hybrid A* on lattice + RRT* for parking maneuvers.
> - **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva, Fetch)**: A* on occupancy grids with traffic-aware heuristics.
> - **Industrial Manipulators (KUKA, ABB)**: RRT-Connect in C-space with collision libraries like FCL and PCD.
> - **UAVs / Drones (DJI, Skydio)**: Visibility graph + Voronoi for multi-obstacle 3-D flight.
> - **Planetary Rovers (NASA Perseverance)**: D* Lite with online replanning for SLAM-integrated navigation.
> - **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci)**: Potential-field-constrained motion planning with virtual fixtures.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Admissibility of Euclidean Heuristic on a Grid

**Claim:** For an 8-connected grid with uniform cost $\Delta$ for cardinal moves and $\Delta\sqrt{2}$ for diagonal moves, the Euclidean distance $h_{\text{E}}(n) = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$ is an admissible heuristic.

**Proof (Step-by-Step):**

> **Step 1 — True cost lower bound:**
> Any path from $n$ to $n_{\text{goal}}$ must travel at least the straight-line Euclidean distance in the plane, because the shortest geometric curve between two points is the line segment.

$$h^*(n) \geq \vert\vert q - q_{\text{goal}} \vert\vert_2 = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$$

> **Step 2 — Heuristic definition:**
> Define the Euclidean heuristic:

$$h_{\text{E}}(n) = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$$

> **Step 3 — Comparison:**
> Combine Step 1 and Step 2:

$$h_{\text{E}}(n) = h^*(n)_{\min} \leq h^*(n) \quad \square$$

Hence $h_{\text{E}}(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$, satisfying admissibility.

> **Step 4 — Consistency check:**
> We must verify $h_{\text{E}}(n) \leq c(n, n') + h_{\text{E}}(n')$. By the triangle inequality:

$$\vert\vert q - q_{\text{goal}} \vert\vert_2 \leq \vert\vert q - q' \vert\vert_2 + \vert\vert q' - q_{\text{goal}} \vert\vert_2$$

> **Step 5 — Conclusion:**
> Because Euclidean distance is a metric, $h_{\text{E}}$ is both admissible and consistent, and A* on an 8-connected grid using $h_{\text{E}}$ yields an optimal path.

## 3.2 Derivation: Optimality Bound for Weighted A*

**Claim:** Weighted A* with weight $\epsilon > 1$ returns a path whose cost is at most $\epsilon$ times the optimal cost.

**Proof:**

> **Step 1 — Setup:**
> Let $\mathcal{P}^*$ be the optimal path with true cost $g^* = g(q_{\text{goal}})$. Let $\mathcal{P}$ be the path returned by weighted A*.

> **Step 2 — Termination condition:**
> Weighted A* terminates when the goal is removed from the priority queue. At that moment, for every node $n$ remaining in the queue:

$$f(n) = g(n) + \epsilon\,h(n) \geq f(q_{\text{goal}}) = g(\mathcal{P})$$

> **Step 3 — Bound on optimal:**
> Because $h$ is admissible and $n$ lies on some candidate path:

$$\epsilon\,g^* \geq \epsilon\,(g(n) + h(n)) \geq g(\mathcal{P})$$

Wait — we need to track the optimal node. By admissibility of $h$:

$$h(q_{\text{goal}}) = 0$$

For any $n$ on the optimal path:

$$\epsilon\,g^* \geq \epsilon\,(g(n) + h(n))$$

> **Step 4 — Final inequality:**

$$g(\mathcal{P}) \leq \epsilon\,g^*$$

Therefore, weighted A* is **$\epsilon$-suboptimal**. $\square$

## 3.3 Derivation: Gradient of the Attractive Potential Field

**Problem:** Compute the attractive force $\mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} = -\nabla U_{\text{att}}$.

> **Step 1 — Recall the scalar potential:**

$$U_{\text{att}}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\xi \, \vert\vert q - q_{\text{goal}} \vert\vert^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\xi \sum_{i=1}^{n}(q_i - q_{i,\text{goal}})^2$$

> **Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. each component $q_i$:**

$$\frac{\partial U_{\text{att}}}{\partial q_i} = \xi\,(q_i - q_{i,\text{goal}})$$

> **Step 3 — Assemble the gradient vector:**

$$\nabla U_{\text{att}}(q) = \xi\,(q - q_{\text{goal}})$$

> **Step 4 — Apply the negative gradient for the force:**

$$\mathbf{F}_{\text{att}}(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{att}}(q) = -\xi\,(q - q_{\text{goal}})$$

> **Step 5 — Physical interpretation:**
> The attractive force is a *restoring* (Hookean) force always pointing from the robot toward the goal with magnitude proportional to the distance. Its magnitude grows without bound as the robot moves farther away, providing strong global guidance but potentially causing instability near the goal.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **Algorithm Selection Matrix for KTU Design Problems**

| Algorithm | Completeness | Optimality | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Suitable For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BFS** (uninformed grid) | Yes | Yes (uniform cost) | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Small grids, unweighted |
| **Dijkstra** | Yes | Yes | $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Weighted graphs |
| **A*** | Yes | Yes (admissible $h$) | $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Single-query grid |
| **Weighted A*** | Yes | $\epsilon$-suboptimal | $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Real-time bounded |
| **D* Lite** | Yes | Yes | $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ incremental | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Unknown / dynamic env |
| **Visibility Graph** | Yes | Yes (shortest) | $O(n^2 \log n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | Polygon obstacles |
| **Voronoi Diagram** | Yes | Yes (clearance-optimal) | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | Clearance-maximization |
| **RRT** | Probabilistic | No (asymptotically) | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | High-dim C-space |
| **RRT*** | Probabilistic | Asymptotically optimal | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | Kinodynamic planning |
| **APF** | No (local minima) | Local optimum only | $O(k)$ per step | $O(m)$ | Reactive local nav |

> **Notation:** $\vert V \vert$ = number of vertices, $\vert E \vert$ = number of edges, $n$ = number of obstacles / samples, $k$ = number of iterations, $m$ = number of obstacles.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Mobile Robot Navigation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Perception: Sensors LIDAR Camera IMU] --> B[Localization: Pose Estimate]
    B --> C[Mapping: Build Occupancy Grid]
    C --> D{Environment Known?}
    D -- YES --> E[Global Planner: A STAR or Dijkstra]
    D -- NO --> F[Local Planner: DWA or APF]
    E --> G[Trajectory Smoother]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Velocity Commands: V and OMEGA]
    H --> I[Mobile Robot Actuators]
    I --> J[Odometry Feedback]
    J --> B
```

## 4.2 A* Search Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Initialize OPEN with start node] --> A[Set f of start = h of start]
    A --> B[Pop node n with min f from OPEN]
    B --> C{Is n the goal?}
    C -- YES --> P[Reconstruct path via parent pointers]
    C -- NO --> D[Move n to CLOSED set]
    D --> E[For each successor n dash of n]
    E --> F{Is n dash in CLOSED?}
    F -- YES --> E
    F -- NO --> G[Compute tentative g of n dash]
    G --> H{Better path found?}
    H -- YES --> I[Update g, f, parent of n dash]
    H -- NO --> E
    I --> J[Insert or update n dash in OPEN]
    J --> E
    E --> K{More successors?}
    K -- YES --> E
    K -- NO --> B
    B --> L{OPEN empty?}
    L -- YES --> R[Failure: No path exists]
    L -- NO --> B
```

## 4.3 Artificial Potential Field Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GOAL[Goal Position] -->|Attractive Force F ATT| ROBOT[Robot]
    OB1[Obstacle 1] -->|Repulsive Force F REP1| ROBOT
    OB2[Obstacle 2] -->|Repulsive Force F REP2| ROBOT
    OB3[Obstacle 3] -->|Repulsive Force F REP3| ROBOT
    ROBOT --> NET[Net Force F NET equals F ATT plus F REP1 plus F REP2 plus F REP3]
    NET --> MOTION[Motion Direction equal to F NET]
```

## 4.4 Path Planning Algorithm Taxonomy (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Mobile Robot Path Planning]
    ROOT --> C1[Classical Deterministic]
    ROOT --> C2[Sampling Based]
    ROOT --> C3[Reactive Local]
    ROOT --> C4[Learning Based]
    C1 --> S1[Roadmap: Visibility Graph, Voronoi]
    C1 --> S2[Cell Decomposition: Exact, Approximate]
    C1 --> S3[Potential Field: APF, Navigation Function]
    C2 --> T1[Single Query: RRT, RRT STAR, RRT CONNECT]
    C2 --> T2[Multiple Query: PRM, Lazy PRM]
    C3 --> L1[Dynamic Window Approach DWA]
    C3 --> L2[Vector Field Histogram VFH]
    C3 --> L3[Closest Gap BG]
    C4 --> N1[Deep Reinforcement Learning DQN, PPO]
    C4 --> N2[Imitation Learning: Behavior Cloning]
    C4 --> N3[Neural Roadmaps: CNN Path Predictor]
```

## 4.5 A* Node Expansion Visualization (Conceptual Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S([S Start]) -->|g equals 1| N1([A])
    S -->|g equals 1| N2([B])
    S -->|g equals 1 point 4| N3([C diagonal])
    N1 -->|g equals 2| N4([D])
    N1 -->|g equals 2 point 4| N5([E diagonal])
    N2 -->|g equals 2| N6([F])
    N3 -->|g equals 2 point 4| N7([G])
    N4 -->|g equals 3| G8([Goal G])
    N5 -->|g equals 3 point 4| G8
    N6 -->|g equals 3| G8
    N7 -->|g equals 3 point 4| G8
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each node shows its $g$-cost from start. The diagonal moves use $\sqrt{2}$ multiplier; cardinal moves use $\Delta = 1$. A* would prioritize expanding nodes with the lowest $f = g + h$.
